procedure_overview: Acetabulum Fracture ORIF (Anterior/Ilioinguinal) (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Acetabulum supported by anterior and posterior columns forming an inverted Y connected to the sacrum via the sciatic buttress. Anterior column: anterior ilium (gluteus medius tubercle), anterior wall and dome, iliopectineal eminence, lateral superior pubic ramus.

Ilioinguinal approach windows: lateral (iliac crest to internal iliac fossa / pelvic brim), middle (between iliopsoas and femoral vessels for pelvic brim / quadrilateral surface / corona mortis), medial (medial to femoral vessels for pubic rami / obturator canal).

Corona mortis: anastomosis of external iliac (epigastric) and internal iliac (obturator) vessels; at risk with lateral dissection over the superior pubic ramus in the middle window. Can cause brisk bleeding if injured.

External iliac vessels and femoral nerve: femoral nerve is most common compression neuropraxia from medial retraction in the middle window; vessels at risk of thrombosis or injury with aggressive retraction or dissection.

Obturator neurovascular bundle: exits the pelvis via the obturator canal in the medial window; at risk during medial dissection or plate placement on the quadrilateral surface or superior pubic ramus.

LFCN, iliopsoas, and round ligament/spermatic cord: LFCN at risk in the lateral window near ASIS; iliopsoas must be mobilized laterally in the middle window; round ligament or spermatic cord protected in the medial window.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Lateral window (iliac crest / internal iliac fossa): incision from iliac crest to ASIS or along the iliac wing. Elevate iliacus/iliopsoas from the internal fossa to the pelvic brim and SI joint. Protect LFCN near ASIS. Exposes anterior column and iliac fossa for reduction and plating.

Middle window (pelvic brim / quadrilateral surface / corona mortis): develop between iliopsoas (lateral) and femoral vessels (medial, protect femoral sheath). Exposes the pelvic brim, quadrilateral surface, and corona mortis (ligate if needed). Key for anterior column and quadrilateral plate access.

Medial window (pubic rami / obturator canal): develop medial to the femoral vessels (protect the femoral sheath). Exposes the superior and inferior pubic rami and obturator canal. Useful for medial wall or rami fractures; protect obturator bundle and round ligament/spermatic cord.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Anastomosis of external iliac (epigastric) and internal iliac (obturator) vessels crosses the superior pubic ramus; at risk with lateral dissection in the middle window of the ilioinguinal approach. Can retract and cause brisk or occult bleeding.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and ligate or control the vessel(s) early in the middle window before they retract into the obturator foramen or behind the ramus. Have clips, sutures, or hemostatic agents ready.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Brisk bleeding, hematoma, or need for embolization/revision. Can be life-threatening if unrecognized in a polytrauma patient.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Middle window of ilioinguinal approach for anterior acetabulum

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Femoral nerve lies on the iliopsoas in the middle window; most common compression neuropraxia from medial retraction against the iliopectineal eminence or pelvic brim. Vessels at risk of thrombosis with prolonged or aggressive retraction.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit duration and force of medial retraction. Use careful retractor placement (pointed or Hohmann under direct vision on the brim). Periodically release retractors. Identify the nerve on the iliopsoas if extending exposure.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Femoral neuropathy (quadriceps weakness, knee buckling, sensory loss on anterior thigh). Usually neuropraxia that improves, but can be permanent. Vessel thrombosis may require vascular intervention.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Middle window retraction in ilioinguinal approach

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Exits the pelvis through the obturator canal in the medial window; at risk during dissection medial to the femoral vessels or during quadrilateral surface or rami plate application.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Protect the bundle with a retractor in the obturator foramen. Avoid deep medial dissection or screw penetration into the canal. Identify early if medial wall work is required.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Obturator nerve palsy (adductor weakness, medial thigh sensory loss) or vascular injury to obturator vessels. Can affect gait and hip adduction strength.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial window and quadrilateral exposure in ilioinguinal approach

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Courses near the ASIS and can be injured in the lateral window incision or during iliac crest elevation and retraction.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the LFCN (or its branches) early near the ASIS in the lateral window. Protect with a vessel loop or retract medially/laterally with the skin flap. Avoid blind cautery or excessive retraction at the ASIS.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterolateral thigh numbness or painful meralgia paresthetica. Usually tolerable but can be distressing; sometimes permanent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral window (iliac crest) of ilioinguinal approach

common_mistakes: Failure to identify and control the corona mortis in the middle window, leading to brisk bleeding, hematoma, or need for embolization/revision.
Prolonged or aggressive medial retraction in the middle window causing femoral nerve compression neuropraxia (quadriceps weakness and buckling).
Injury to the obturator neurovascular bundle during medial window dissection or quadrilateral plate application, resulting in adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss.
LFCN injury near the ASIS in the lateral window incision or retraction, causing anterolateral thigh numbness or painful meralgia paresthetica.
Inadequate protection of the round ligament or spermatic cord in the medial window, especially in male patients, leading to cord injury or hernia risk.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the three windows of the ilioinguinal approach and what key structure is exposed or at risk in each? A: Lateral: iliac fossa / anterior column, LFCN at ASIS. Middle: pelvic brim / quadrilateral surface / corona mortis, femoral nerve and external iliac vessels. Medial: pubic rami / obturator canal, obturator neurovascular bundle and round ligament/spermatic cord.

Q: What is the corona mortis, where is it encountered in the ilioinguinal approach, and why must it be controlled? A: Anastomosis of external iliac (epigastric) and internal iliac (obturator) vessels crossing the superior pubic ramus. Encountered in the middle window during lateral dissection over the ramus. Uncontrolled injury causes brisk bleeding or hematoma; ligate early before retraction.

Q: Which nerve is most commonly affected by retraction in the middle window of the ilioinguinal approach, and how is injury minimized? A: Femoral nerve (on iliopsoas). Limit duration and force of medial retraction; use careful retractor placement under vision on the brim; periodically release retractors. Most injuries are neuropraxias that improve.

Q: Describe the ilioinguinal approach windows for anterior acetabulum exposure and the primary danger in the middle window. A: Lateral window for iliac fossa/anterior column (LFCN risk). Middle window between iliopsoas and femoral vessels for brim/quadrilateral/corona (corona bleed + femoral n/vessel retraction risk). Medial window for rami/obturator (obturator bundle risk).

Q: How is the obturator neurovascular bundle protected in the medial window, and what is the consequence of injury? A: Protect with a retractor in the obturator foramen; avoid deep medial dissection or screw penetration into the canal. Injury causes obturator nerve palsy (adductor weakness, medial thigh sensory loss) or vascular compromise.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Lateral window (iliac crest / internal iliac fossa)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision from iliac crest to ASIS or along the iliac wing. Elevate iliacus/iliopsoas from the internal fossa to the pelvic brim and SI joint. Protect LFCN near ASIS. Exposes anterior column and iliac fossa for reduction and plating.
key_structures: SI joint (posterior limit of lateral window)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Provides access to the anterior column and iliac fossa for column reduction and plate application. LFCN protection is the key superficial step.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Middle window (pelvic brim / quadrilateral surface / corona mortis)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Develop between iliopsoas (lateral) and femoral vessels (medial, protect femoral sheath). Exposes the pelvic brim, quadrilateral surface, and corona mortis (ligate if needed). Key for anterior column and quadrilateral plate access. Limit medial retraction to protect femoral nerve.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The critical working window for most anterior acetabular fractures. Corona identification and femoral nerve protection determine safety and exposure quality.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Develop medial to the femoral vessels (protect the femoral sheath). Exposes the superior and inferior pubic rami and obturator canal. Useful for medial wall or rami fractures; protect obturator bundle and round ligament/spermatic cord. Less extensile than the middle window for quadrilateral work.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Gives medial access for rami and medial wall fractures. Obturator protection is essential; this window is often used in combination with the middle window.

validation_warnings: Acetabulum anterior payload is strictly ilioinguinal (three windows). Kocher-Langenbeck, sciatic nerve protection, short external rotators, gluteus maximus split, and posterior wall/column anatomy belong exclusively to the posterior acetabulum payload and are referenced here only to clarify separation. Source material previously contained stringified SAR and generic content; cleaned to public 1034 facts and rich 6-field windows/SAR only.
danger_zones: Corona mortis in middle window: identify and ligate early on superior pubic ramus; uncontrolled bleed or retraction into obturator foramen can be life-threatening.
Femoral nerve (middle window retraction): limit force/duration; most common neuropraxia causing quad weakness; usually improves but can be permanent.
Obturator neurovascular bundle (medial window): protect in obturator canal; injury causes adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss.
LFCN (lateral window near ASIS): identify and protect; injury causes anterolateral thigh numbness or meralgia paresthetica.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is strictly for anterior acetabulum fracture ORIF via the ilioinguinal approach (lateral, middle, and medial windows). Focus is on anterior column, quadrilateral surface, corona mortis, external iliac/femoral neurovascular, obturator bundle, and LFCN/iliopsoas protection. Kocher-Langenbeck, sciatic nerve, short external rotators, and posterior wall/column content is not included except as explicit 'not this approach' reference in validation_warnings only.

procedure_overview: Acetabulum Fracture ORIF (Posterior/Kocher-Langenbeck) (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Kocher-Langenbeck for posterior wall/column, most transverse and T-shaped fractures. Exposure to posterior wall, lateral posterior column, indirect true pelvis/anterior posterior column, and proximal femur.

Sciatic nerve protection: initial location along quadratus femoris; extend hip and flex knee to relax the nerve; gentle retraction only; release short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus/gemelli) posteriorly and tag for repair; peroneal division is more vulnerable laterally.
Gluteus maximus split: superficial interval splits the gluteus maximus (inferior gluteal n.) and IT band. Deep interval between short external rotators and femur after their release.
Short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus, gemelli): released and tagged for later repair; obturator internus can act as a cushion over the sciatic nerve when retracted.
Superior gluteal neurovascular bundle: at risk if the gluteus maximus split is extended too far proximally (superior gluteal n. exits the greater sciatic notch).

Quadratus femoris and inferior gluteal structures: quadratus femoris is the initial landmark for sciatic nerve identification; inferior gluteal artery/nerve are posterior and can be injured with aggressive inferior dissection.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Superficial (gluteus maximus split): patient lateral or prone. Incision from PSIS or posterior to GT, curving distally along the femoral shaft. Split gluteus maximus in line with its fibers (inferior gluteal n.) and IT band. Limit proximal split to protect superior gluteal structures.

Deep (short external rotators and sciatic protection): identify and tag the short external rotators (piriformis superior, obturator internus/gemelli inferior). Release them from the femur posteriorly and retract (obturator internus can cushion the sciatic). Identify sciatic nerve along quadratus femoris; extend hip/flex knee for slack. Expose posterior wall and lateral posterior column.

Posterior column / wall exposure and quadrilateral palpation: after ER release and sciatic protection, the posterior wall, lateral posterior column, and (indirectly) quadrilateral surface are accessible. Hip extension and knee flexion are maintained throughout. Quadratus femoris and inferior gluteal structures are respected inferiorly.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies on the posterior column and quadratus femoris; at risk during gluteus maximus split, short ER release, retraction, or posterior wall/column work. Peroneal division is more lateral and vulnerable to stretch or direct injury. Position (hip extension, knee flexion) and obturator internus cushion are protective.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify sciatic early along quadratus femoris. Extend hip and flex knee throughout the case. Release short ERs posteriorly and tag (obturator internus cushions the nerve). Use only gentle retraction; never place retractors behind the nerve without protection.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop (peroneal) or tibial nerve deficit, sensory loss, chronic pain, or need for tendon transfer/brace. Often permanent if severe stretch or transection.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Kocher-Langenbeck (posterior) for acetabulum / posterior wall/column

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Exits the greater sciatic notch and supplies gluteus medius/minimus; at risk if the gluteus maximus split is carried too far proximally toward the notch.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit the proximal extent of the gluteus maximus split. Identify the bundle if exposure must extend superiorly. Use blunt dissection in the proximal gluteal interval.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Gluteus medius/minimus denervation or vascular injury leading to abductor weakness, Trendelenburg gait, and poor hip function. Can be permanent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Proximal gluteus maximus split in Kocher-Langenbeck

structures_at_risk.structure: Inferior gluteal artery / nerve and quadratus femoris
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Inferior gluteal structures and quadratus femoris are posterior and inferior; at risk with aggressive inferior dissection or retraction for distal posterior column exposure.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Respect the quadratus femoris as the initial sciatic landmark; limit inferior release unless needed for column reduction. Protect the inferior gluteal pedicle during deep inferior work.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Inferior gluteal nerve palsy (gluteus maximus weakness) or bleeding from the artery. Can affect hip extension power and wound healing.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Inferior exposure in Kocher-Langenbeck for low posterior column fractures

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Released and tagged for exposure and later repair; the obturator internus tendon can be used as a cushion over the sciatic nerve when retracted. Over-release or poor tagging affects stability and nerve protection.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Tag each rotator before release. Release from the femur posteriorly (not from the notch if possible). Use the obturator internus to cushion and retract the sciatic. Repair at closure for stability.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of external rotation strength, posterior instability, or sciatic nerve irritation if the cushion effect is lost. Poor repair contributes to heterotopic ossification or weakness.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deep interval of Kocher-Langenbeck

key_landmarks: Landmarks: ASIS, iliac crest, sciatic notches, quadrilateral surface (palpable for posterior column reduction).
Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.
Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.
Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.

common_mistakes: Failure to maintain hip extension and knee flexion throughout the case, allowing sciatic nerve stretch and foot drop (peroneal division most vulnerable).
Extending the gluteus maximus split too far proximally toward the greater sciatic notch, injuring the superior gluteal neurovascular bundle and causing abductor weakness or bleeding.
Inadequate tagging or repair of the short external rotators (especially obturator internus), losing the cushion effect over the sciatic and contributing to instability or nerve irritation.
Aggressive inferior dissection without respecting the quadratus femoris and inferior gluteal structures, causing bleeding or inferior gluteal nerve palsy affecting hip extension.
Placing retractors or instruments behind the sciatic nerve without protection or visualization, leading to direct nerve injury or stretch.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: In Kocher-Langenbeck (posterior) for acetabulum, what maneuvers protect the sciatic nerve (initial location, hip/knee position, short ER role)? A: Identify along quadratus femoris initially. Extend the hip and flex the knee throughout the case to slack the nerve. Release short ERs (piriformis, obturator internus/gemelli) posteriorly; the obturator internus tendon can cushion and retract the nerve. Use only gentle retraction.

Q: What is the role of the obturator internus in sciatic nerve protection during Kocher-Langenbeck, and why are the short ERs tagged before release? A: The obturator internus tendon lies over the sciatic and can be used as a cushion when the nerve is retracted. Tagging allows anatomic repair at closure for stability and to maintain the cushion effect; loss contributes to instability or nerve irritation.

Q: Which division of the sciatic nerve is more vulnerable in the Kocher-Langenbeck approach and why? A: The peroneal division (lateral) is more vulnerable to stretch or direct injury than the tibial division. It lies more lateral on the posterior column and is at higher risk during retraction or ER release.

Q: What is the superior gluteal neurovascular bundle and how is it protected in the gluteus maximus split of Kocher-Langenbeck? A: The superior gluteal bundle exits the greater sciatic notch and supplies the abductors. Limit the proximal extent of the gluteus maximus split; identify the bundle if superior exposure is required. Proximal extension risks abductor denervation or bleeding.

Q: Describe the Kocher-Langenbeck approach for posterior acetabulum and the primary structure at risk throughout. A: Superficial: split gluteus maximus/IT band (limit proximal for superior gluteal). Deep: release and tag short ERs, identify sciatic on quadratus femoris, maintain hip extension/knee flexion, use obturator internus as cushion. Primary risk: sciatic nerve (peroneal division) injury causing foot drop.

surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The critical deep interval. Sciatic protection (position + obturator internus cushion + gentle retraction) is the defining safety step of the Kocher-Langenbeck approach.

Maintain hip extension/knee flexion. Quadratus femoris and inferior gluteal structures are respected inferiorly. Repair short ERs at closure for stability.

Superior gluteal neurovascular bundle: limit proximal gluteus maximus split; proximal extension risks abductor denervation or bleeding.
Inferior gluteal artery/nerve and quadratus femoris: respect as inferior landmarks; aggressive inferior work risks bleeding or gluteus maximus weakness.
Short external rotators: tag before release; obturator internus cushions sciatic; poor repair affects stability and nerve protection.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is strictly for posterior acetabulum fracture ORIF via the Kocher-Langenbeck approach (gluteus maximus split, short external rotator release, sciatic protection with position and obturator internus cushion). Focus is on posterior wall/column exposure, sciatic nerve maneuvers, and short ER anatomy. Ilioinguinal, corona mortis, external iliac vessels, femoral nerve, obturator bundle (as anterior structures), and anterior column/windows content is not included except as explicit 'not this approach' reference in validation_warnings only.

procedure_overview: ACL Reconstruction (soft_tissue_repair). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: ACL provides anterior and lateral rotatory stability to the knee. Anatomic reconstruction restores native dimensions, collagen orientation, and insertion sites (tibial and femoral footprints).
Tibial footprint: located in the center of the tibial plateau just anterior to the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus and medial to the lateral tibial spine; ~10-12 mm wide.
Femoral footprint: on the lateral wall of the intercondylar notch, posterior and inferior to the lateral intercondylar ridge (resident's ridge); avoids posterior wall blowout.
Intercondylar notch: narrow notch or steep lateral wall increases risk of graft impingement or non-anatomic tunnel placement; notchplasty may be needed.
Saphenous nerve / infrapatellar branch: at risk during hamstring graft harvest (medial incision); identify and protect to avoid numbness or neuroma on medial leg.
Meniscus and root relationships: lateral meniscus posterior horn is adjacent to the ACL tibial footprint; medial meniscus ramp lesions or root tears often addressed concurrently.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Arthroscopic portals: anterolateral (viewing) portal just lateral to patellar tendon at joint line; anteromedial (working) portal under direct vision in the notch or medial gutter. Accessory anteromedial or posteromedial for meniscus work or tibial tunnel.

Notch and footprint preparation: debride ACL remnant to identify footprints. Tibial tunnel starts on medial proximal tibia, exits in center of tibial footprint. Femoral tunnel on lateral notch wall posterior to resident's ridge, avoiding posterior wall blowout (leave 1-2 mm back wall).

Graft passage and fixation: pass graft through tunnels; femoral fixation (suspensory or aperture) and tibial (screw or button). Protect patellar tendon and saphenous during harvest if using autograft. Cycle knee to check isometry and avoid impingement.

structures_at_risk.structure: Infrapatellar branch of saphenous nerve (hamstring harvest)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the medial proximal tibia in the subcutaneous plane; transected during vertical or oblique incision for hamstring tendon harvest.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Make the harvest incision 2-3 cm distal to the tibial tubercle, identify the branch under the skin or fascia, and protect it with a vessel loop or retract medially.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or painful neuroma on the anteromedial leg below the knee. Common complaint after hamstring ACL; usually tolerable but can be bothersome.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Hamstring autograft harvest for ACL reconstruction

structures_at_risk.structure: Posterior wall / back wall of lateral femoral condyle (femoral tunnel)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: If the femoral tunnel is placed too posterior or the back wall is violated during reaming, the graft loses cortical fixation and can blow out posteriorly.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Leave 1-2 mm of posterior wall intact when drilling the femoral tunnel. Use offset guide or direct visualization; confirm position before reaming. Convert to outside-in or two-incision if wall is thin.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Femoral tunnel blowout requiring backup fixation, larger tunnel, or revision to different technique. Can lead to early graft failure or instability.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Arthroscopic femoral tunnel drilling in ACL reconstruction

structures_at_risk.structure: Medial collateral ligament / medial capsule (during tibial tunnel or notch work)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Overzealous medial retraction or tunnel starting point too medial can injure the deep MCL or capsule, especially in tight knees.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use careful portal placement and retractors; start tibial tunnel more central on the medial tibia. Avoid aggressive valgus stress during notch work.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Medial laxity or pain; rare but can contribute to rotational instability if combined with ACL deficiency.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Arthroscopic ACL reconstruction tibial tunnel and notch preparation

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral meniscus posterior horn / root (adjacent to tibial footprint)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The posterior horn of the lateral meniscus is immediately adjacent to the ACL tibial footprint; iatrogenic injury during tibial tunnel reaming or debridement can destabilize the meniscus.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the posterior horn under direct vision before tibial tunnel drilling. Use a guide that protects the meniscus or adjust tunnel trajectory slightly anterior/central if needed.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Lateral meniscus posterior horn tear or root avulsion leading to meniscal extrusion, accelerated lateral compartment wear, and potential need for meniscal repair or transplant.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Tibial tunnel placement in ACL reconstruction

key_landmarks: Tibial tubercle (harvest and tibial tunnel start reference); lateral femoral condyle (femoral footprint on notch wall); intercondylar notch (for visualization and orientation).
Tibial tubercle (harvest and tibial tunnel start reference); lateral femoral condyle (femoral footprint on notch wall); intercondylar notch (for visualization and orientation).
Tibial tubercle (tunnel/harvest), lateral femoral condyle (femoral footprint), notch (visualization).
Tibial tubercle (harvest and tibial tunnel start reference); lateral femoral condyle (femoral footprint on notch wall); intercondylar notch (for visualization and orientation).
Tibial tubercle (harvest/tibial tunnel reference); lateral femoral condyle (femoral footprint on notch wall); notch (visualization landmark).
common_mistakes: Injury to the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve during hamstring harvest incision, causing persistent medial leg numbness or neuroma.
Non-anatomic femoral tunnel placement anterior to the lateral intercondylar ridge (resident's ridge), leading to rotational instability or extension loss from impingement.
Posterior wall blowout of the lateral femoral condyle during femoral tunnel reaming, resulting in loss of cortical fixation and need for alternative fixation or revision.

Iatrogenic injury to the lateral meniscus posterior horn or root while preparing the tibial footprint or drilling the tibial tunnel, causing meniscal extrusion and accelerated lateral compartment arthritis.
Inadequate notch preparation or failure to address a narrow notch, leading to graft impingement, loss of extension, or early failure.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the locations of the native ACL tibial and femoral footprints, and why is the femoral footprint described relative to the lateral intercondylar ridge? A: Tibial: center of plateau anterior to lateral meniscus posterior horn. Femoral: lateral notch wall posterior/inferior to resident's ridge. Ridge is the key landmark to avoid non-anatomic anterior placement or posterior wall blowout.

Q: How is the saphenous nerve protected during hamstring graft harvest for ACL, and what is the consequence of injury? A: Identify the infrapatellar branch under the skin or fascia with a 2-3 cm distal medial incision; protect with retractor or vessel loop. Injury causes medial leg numbness or neuroma below the knee.

Q: What is posterior wall blowout in ACL femoral tunnel drilling and how is it avoided? A: Drilling too posterior or reaming through a thin wall removes the cortical rim for suspensory fixation. Leave 1-2 mm back wall; use offset guide and confirm under visualization before reaming; convert technique if wall is inadequate.

Q: Describe the relationship of the lateral meniscus posterior horn to the ACL tibial footprint and the risk during tunnel placement. A: The posterior horn is immediately adjacent to the footprint. Iatrogenic injury during debridement or reaming can cause meniscus root tear or extrusion, leading to lateral compartment degeneration.

Q: What portals are used for ACL reconstruction and why is the anteromedial portal often placed under direct visualization? A: Anterolateral viewing (lateral to patellar tendon) and anteromedial working (in notch/medial gutter under vision). Visualization prevents iatrogenic cartilage or meniscus injury and ensures the portal allows instrument access to both footprints.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anterolateral viewing portal placed just lateral to the patellar tendon at or slightly above the joint line (soft spot). Anteromedial working portal created under direct visualization in the intercondylar notch or medial gutter. Accessory portals (anteromedial or posteromedial) for meniscus or tibial tunnel work as needed. Protect infrapatellar fat pad and saphenous branches.
structures_at_risk: Infrapatellar branch of saphenous nerve (medial portal)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Portal placement is the first critical step for all arthroscopic ACL work. Poor portals lead to difficult visualization, iatrogenic cartilage damage, or inability to reach footprints.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Intercondylar notch and ACL footprint preparation
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Debride ACL remnant with shaver or radiofrequency to clearly define tibial and femoral footprints. Tibial footprint is anterior to the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus. Femoral footprint is on the lateral notch wall, posterior and inferior to the lateral intercondylar ridge (resident's ridge). Notchplasty if narrow to prevent graft impingement.
structures_at_risk: Posterior wall of lateral femoral condyle (blowout risk)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Accurate footprint restoration is key to anatomic ACL reconstruction and rotational stability. Posterior wall blowout or non-anatomic placement leads to failure.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Tunnel drilling, graft passage, and fixation
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Tibial tunnel: start on medial proximal tibia, exit in center of tibial footprint (guide protects meniscus). Femoral tunnel: on lateral notch wall with offset guide to leave 1-2 mm posterior wall. Pass graft (hamstring, BTB, or allograft) and fix femur first (suspensory or interference), then tibia after cycling and tensioning. Protect saphenous during hamstring harvest.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Tunnel position and graft tension determine success. Blowout or impingement causes early failure or loss of motion.

validation_warnings: ACL reconstruction payload emphasizes arthroscopic portals and footprint anatomy rather than traditional open layers. danger_zones: Infrapatellar saphenous branch during hamstring harvest: identify and protect on medial tibia; transection causes numbness/neuroma on anteromedial leg.
Posterior wall of lateral femoral condyle: leave 1-2 mm intact; blowout loses fixation and requires revision technique.
Lateral meniscus posterior horn/root: adjacent to tibial footprint; iatrogenic tear during tunnel work causes extrusion and lateral wear.
MCL/medial capsule: protect during tibial tunnel or notch work; over-retraction causes medial laxity or pain.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for ACL reconstruction, primarily arthroscopic (portals, notch, footprints). Medial parapatellar open exposure is referenced only for graft harvest or select cases. Focus on anatomic footprint restoration, posterior wall protection, saphenous safety during harvest, and meniscus relationships. Content is ACL-specific; no TKA, hip/THA, or other ligament reconstruction anatomy is mixed in.

procedure_overview: Distal Femur Fracture ORIF (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Distal femur: medial/lateral condyles, trochlea, intercondylar notch. Hoffa (coronal) fragment variant important (often missed, needs fixation). Gastrocnemius deforming force on distal fragment (extension).
Approaches: parapatellar (medial or lateral) for intra-articular; lateral for extra-articular. Protect extensor mechanism and posterior neurovascular (popliteal, tibial/peroneal).
Lateral approach: vastus lateralis elevation, lateral intermuscular septum. Protect perforators, femoral bundle anteromedial, sciatic posterior. Good for lateral plating or extra-articular.

Lateral approach for extra-articular or lateral plating: vastus lateralis elevation, lateral intermuscular septum, protect perforators and femoral bundle. Parapatellar for intra-articular: midline or parapatellar incision, evert/sublux patella, expose notch/condyles/trochlea for reduction (fix Hoffa if present). Protect extensor and posterior neurovascular.
Posterior structures: popliteal vessels, tibial/peroneal nerves at risk with traction, retraction, or posterior condyle work. Use careful technique and position.
Reduction: traction, bumps, clamps for condylar block to shaft. Fix Hoffa if present (screws). Plate lateral or medial depending approach/pattern. Check rotation/alignment with fluoro.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Lateral: patient supine or lateral. Incision lateral thigh to knee. Elevate vastus lateralis, control perforators. Expose lateral condyle/shaft. Protect anteromedial femoral bundle and posterior sciatic/popliteal. Good for extra-articular or lateral plate.

Lateral: incision lateral thigh to knee, elevate vastus lateralis, control perforators, expose lateral condyle/shaft, protect anteromedial femoral bundle and posterior sciatic/popliteal. Parapatellar: midline or parapatellar incision, develop to retinaculum, enter joint, evert/sublux patella, expose trochlea/notch/condyles for intra-articular reduction and fixation (Hoffa screws if needed). Protect patellar tendon insertion.

Intra-articular work: reduce condyles (Hoffa if present), restore articular surface, then align to shaft. Use traction/ bumps for length. Check notch for intercondylar involvement. Posterior structures protected throughout.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Immediately posterior to distal femur/condyles; at risk with traction, posterior retraction, or work on posterior condyles/Hoffa. High energy fractures can have vascular injury.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use careful traction and posterior retractors only under vision. Hip/knee flexion for relaxation. Direct visualization for posterior work. Pre-op vascular exam and post-op monitoring.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Vascular compromise (ischemia, bleeding), nerve palsy (foot drop, tibial deficit). May require vascular repair or fasciotomy.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Distal femur exposure (lateral or parapatellar) with posterior risk

structures_at_risk.structure: Extensor mechanism (quad tendon, patella, patellar tendon)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: At risk during parapatellar arthrotomy, eversion, or if fracture involves trochlea/patella. Poor handling leads to tracking issues or lag.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Protect patellar tendon insertion with retractor/pin. Evert/sublux carefully (quad snip if needed). Repair retinacula and assess tracking after reduction/fixation.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Extensor lag, patellar maltracking, or re-operation for extensor mechanism.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Parapatellar approach for intra-articular distal femur

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Anteromedial to distal femur; at risk with medial retraction or if approach drifts in lateral work.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Stay lateral on bone in lateral approach. Careful anterior retraction in parapatellar. Direct vision for medial work if needed.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Femoral neuropathy or vascular injury.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral or parapatellar distal femur exposure

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Often missed on AP; requires specific fixation (screws). Unfixed leads to malunion or arthritis.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: CT for intra-articular patterns. Reduce and fix with anterior-to-posterior or posterior-to-anterior screws (countersink). Check reduction on lateral fluoro.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Malunion, step-off, arthritis, or need for revision fixation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Intra-articular distal femur ORIF (condylar reduction)

key_landmarks: Tibial tubercle and pes anserinus for harvest; portals for notch and footprint access.
Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.

common_mistakes: Missing Hoffa fragment (coronal split) on imaging or intra-op, leading to malunion or step-off arthritis.
Inadequate posterior protection (popliteal/tibial/peroneal) during traction or posterior condyle work, causing vascular or nerve injury.
Poor handling of the extensor mechanism or retinacula in parapatellar approach, causing extensor lag, patellar maltracking, or need for re-operation.
Ignoring deforming forces (gastroc extension on distal) or failing to assess/correct rotation/alignment, leading to poor function.
Overlooking compartment or neurovascular status pre/post-op in high-energy distal femur fractures.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is a Hoffa fragment in distal femur fractures and why is it important to recognize/fix? A: Coronal plane split of the lateral (or medial) condyle, often missed on AP. Requires specific screw fixation (AP or PA, countersunk). Unfixed leads to malunion, step-off, and arthritis.

Q: Describe the deforming forces and key posterior structures at risk in distal femur fractures. A: Gastrocnemius pulls distal fragment into extension. Popliteal vessels, tibial and peroneal nerves immediately posterior - at risk with traction or posterior work. Pre/post neurovascular exam essential.

Q: How do you protect the extensor mechanism in parapatellar approach for intra-articular distal femur ORIF and assess tracking? A: Protect patellar tendon insertion with retractor or temporary pin. After reduction and fixation, cycle the knee and observe patellar tracking on the trochlea. Repair retinacula anatomically.

Q: How do you protect the extensor mechanism in parapatellar approach for intra-articular distal femur ORIF and assess tracking? A: Protect patellar tendon insertion with retractor or temporary pin. After reduction and fixation, cycle the knee and observe patellar tracking on the trochlea. Repair retinacula anatomically.

Q: Why is rotation/alignment critical in distal femur ORIF and how do you assess? A: Malrotation or varus/valgus leads to poor function, arthritis, or limp. Assess with contralateral comparison, fluoro (AP/lateral of knee + hip), or direct landmarks (lesser trochanter profile, condylar axis). Correct before final fixation.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Lateral: incision lateral thigh to knee, expose vastus lateralis fascia. Parapatellar: midline or parapatellar, develop to retinaculum. Protect sensory branches and plan for eversion or lateral exposure.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard approaches for distal femur (lateral for extra-artic or lateral plate; parapatellar for intra-articular). Extensor protection key for parapatellar.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Lateral: elevate vastus lateralis, control perforators, expose lateral condyle/shaft. Parapatellar: enter joint, evert/sublux patella, expose notch/trochlea/condyles. Protect popliteal bundle posteriorly with retractors/position. Reduce condyles (fix Hoffa if present) then to shaft.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Working layer for articular reduction and shaft alignment. Posterior protection and Hoffa recognition/fixation are critical for intra-articular patterns.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After fixation, assess patellar tracking (parapatellar) or overall alignment/rotation (lateral). Close in layers. Monitor neurovascular and compartments. Post-op protected ROM and weightbearing per stability.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Confirms reduction quality and protects repair. Rotation/alignment critical for function; tracking for parapatellar cases.

structures_at_risk.structure: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Runs ulnar to or within the FCR interval in the volar approach; at risk during skin incision, subcutaneous dissection, or FCR sheath opening.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect the branch (usually 1-2 cm ulnar to FCR or in the interval) before incising fascia. Retract carefully; do not cauterize blindly in the subcutaneous plane.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or painful neuroma in the thenar eminence/palm. Usually tolerable but can be annoying for patients with volar incisions.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Volar FCR approach for distal radius ORIF

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: At risk in Henry (volar radial) extension or if the approach is extended proximally; also relevant for dorsal approaches. Lies in supinator; pronation moves it away.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: For pure FCR volar: limited proximal extension. If Henry needed, fully pronate forearm before incising supinator mid-belly. Identify the nerve if proximal dissection is required.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Finger and thumb extension weakness (finger drop), loss of thumb radial abduction. Wrist extension preserved (ECRL).

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the field in dorsal approaches or radial-sided volar extensions near the styloid. At risk during subcutaneous dissection or radial styloid work.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify under the brachioradialis fascia in radial-sided or dorsal work. Protect with vessel loop; avoid blind clamping or excessive retraction.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or neuroma on the dorsal radial hand (first web, thumb, index, middle). Can be more symptomatic than motor loss.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Dorsal or radial extension of volar approach for distal radius

structures_at_risk.structure: Flexor pollicis longus (FPL) and flexor tendons; EPL (dorsal reference)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Volar plates placed distal to the watershed line (especially lunate facet) can irritate or rupture FPL. EPL at risk from dorsal screw penetration (Lister's tubercle reference).
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Keep volar plate proximal to watershed line. Confirm screw lengths with skyline or 23° elevated lateral views. Avoid prominent dorsal screws near Lister's/EPL.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: FPL or EPL attritional rupture (late tendon rupture). Loss of thumb IP flexion or extension. Requires secondary tendon reconstruction.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Volar (and dorsal reference) plating in distal radius ORIF

attending_pimp_questions: Q: Which nerve is at risk in the FCR interval during volar distal radius ORIF and how is it protected? A: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve. Identify and protect it 1-2 cm ulnar to FCR before incising fascia; do not cauterize blindly.

Q: What is the consequence of placing a volar plate distal to the watershed line? A: FPL or flexor tendon irritation or late rupture. Keep plate proximal to watershed; confirm screw length with skyline view.
Q: How is the PIN protected if a Henry extension is needed? A: Fully pronate the forearm before incising supinator mid-belly; limit proximal extension for pure FCR cases.
Q: What structure is at risk from prominent dorsal screws near Lister's tubercle? A: EPL tendon. Use 23 degree elevated lateral or skyline views to confirm screw lengths and avoid penetration.

Q: Why is the superficial radial nerve at risk in radial or dorsal extensions and how to avoid neuroma? A: It crosses under brachioradialis fascia; identify and protect with vessel loop; avoid blind clamping.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision along the FCR tendon, usually slightly ulnar to the tendon or following its radial border with a curve at the wrist crease. Develop subcutaneous plane; identify and protect the palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve (usually ulnar to FCR or in the interval). The radial artery is radial to the FCR and should be protected during retraction.
key_structures: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve
structures_at_risk: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve (numbness in palm)
Superficial radial nerve branches if incision extended radially
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard volar approach for distal radius ORIF. Establishes the internervous plane between FCR (median) and FPL (AIN). Incorrect plane risks nerve injury or difficult deep exposure.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep muscular plane (FCR sheath to PQ)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incise the FCR tendon sheath on its dorsal/radial side (ulnar retraction of FCR protects the median nerve). Retract FPL ulnarly (AIN). Identify and incise the radial and ulnar borders of pronator quadratus (PQ) and elevate it subperiosteally from the volar radius. The watershed line (most volar prominent aspect, especially lunate facet) is the critical distal limit for plate placement to avoid FPL irritation.
structures_at_risk: Median nerve / recurrent motor branch (excessive ulnar retraction or blind dissection)
FPL tendon (at risk from plate/screws distal to watershed)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: This plane gives direct access to the volar distal radius for reduction and plating. Failure to respect the watershed line is a common cause of late FPL rupture. For Henry extension, pronate forearm to protect PIN in supinator.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Volar radius exposure and deep retraction
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After PQ elevation, the volar radius, fracture, and joint are exposed. Use Hohmann or other retractors carefully; radial artery is protected radially, median nerve/FPL ulnarly. For intra-articular work or extension, identify Lister's tubercle dorsally for reference (EPL ulnar to it). Confirm plate position proximal to watershed and assess screw lengths with 23° elevated lateral or skyline views.
structures_at_risk: PIN (if dorsal reference or Henry extension used)
EPL tendon (attritional rupture risk from long dorsal screws or prominent hardware near Lister's)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Final exposure for reduction, provisional fixation, and definitive volar plating. Directly related to avoiding tendon irritation and ensuring stable fixation for early motion.

danger_zones: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve in FCR interval - identify before fascial incision; injury causes thenar numbness or neuroma.
PIN in Henry/supinator - pronate fully before mid-belly split; injury causes finger/thumb extension loss (wrist extension spared).
Superficial radial nerve in radial/dorsal work - protect under BR fascia; injury causes dorsal radial hand numbness/neuroma.
FPL/EPL from plate position or screw penetration - plate proximal to watershed, confirm lengths with skyline/23deg lateral; late tendon rupture requires reconstruction.

procedure_overview: Femoral Shaft Fracture ORIF (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Femur largest/strongest bone with anterior bow. Linea aspera on posterior middle third is key attachment and compressive strut. Thigh has 3 compartments.
Deforming forces: proximal fragment abducted (gluteus med/min) and flexed (iliopsoas); distal segment varus (adductors) and extension (gastrocnemius on posterior condyles).

Lateral approach: patient lateral or supine, incision along lateral thigh over vastus lateralis. Elevate or split vastus lateralis anteriorly from linea aspera to expose shaft. Protect perforating branches of profunda femoris.
Key neurovascular: femoral nerve/vessels anteromedial (retract carefully); sciatic nerve posterior (protect with position/retractors); profunda femoris perforators cross field laterally.
Reduction: traction, bumps, or external fix for length/alignment/rotation. Plate on lateral or anterolateral safe corridor (avoid medial neurovascular).
Blood loss: closed fractures significant (1-2L possible in thigh); open higher. Monitor compartments.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Lateral thigh: incision from greater trochanter region distally along lateral femur. Develop plane anterior to vastus lateralis or split it. Identify and control perforators as they pierce lateral intermuscular septum.

Deep exposure: elevate vastus lateralis subperiosteally or split to linea aspera. Protect femoral neurovascular bundle by anterior retraction; sciatic lies posterior - use hip flexion/knee flexion if needed for relaxation.
Fracture work: reduce with traction/ bumps/ clamps. Apply lateral plate (broad or narrow) in safe corridor. Check rotation/length with fluoro or direct. Close fascia loosely if compartment concern.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Cross the lateral thigh through vastus lateralis or intermuscular septum during lateral approach; can be lacerated during elevation or plate placement.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and ligate or control perforators as encountered. Use careful subperiosteal elevation and have hemostasis ready before stripping muscle.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Significant bleeding into thigh, hematoma, possible compartment syndrome or need for transfusion/revision. Can obscure field.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral approach for femoral shaft ORIF

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lie in anterior compartment; at risk with anterior retraction or if approach drifts medially during shaft exposure.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Stay lateral on the femur. Use anterior retractors carefully under direct vision. Avoid aggressive medial dissection.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Femoral neuropathy (quad weakness) or vascular injury (ischemia/bleeding).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral femur shaft exposure

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Posterior to femur; at risk with posterior retraction, traction, or in flexed positions if not protected during shaft work.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Maintain awareness of posterior structures. Use posterior retractors sparingly; consider hip/knee flexion for relaxation in some positions. Direct visualization when possible.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Sciatic palsy (foot drop, sensory loss, pain). May be stretch or direct; recovery variable.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral approach deep exposure for femoral shaft

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Elevated or split for exposure; perforators must be controlled to prevent bleeding and allow clean plate bed.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Elevate subperiosteally where possible. Tag and ligate perforators sequentially. Protect muscle for closure and function.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Bleeding, poor exposure, muscle devascularization, or weak knee extension if extensive damage.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral vastus lateralis work for shaft plating

attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the deforming forces on the proximal and distal fragments in a femoral shaft fracture? A: Proximal: abduction (gluteus med/min) and flexion (iliopsoas). Distal: varus (adductors) and extension (gastrocnemius).

Q: Describe the key steps and primary structures at risk in the lateral approach for femoral shaft ORIF. A: Incision lateral thigh, elevate/split vastus lateralis, control profunda perforators, protect femoral bundle anteromedially and sciatic posteriorly. Risk: bleeding from perforators, nerve injury.

Q: Why is early control of profunda femoris perforators critical in lateral femoral exposure? A: They cross the field laterally and can cause brisk bleeding or hematoma if lacerated during muscle elevation or plating. Control prevents obscured field and compartment risk.

Q: How do you protect the sciatic nerve during lateral shaft exposure? A: Awareness of its posterior position; use gentle retraction, consider hip flexion/knee flexion for relaxation in some setups, and avoid aggressive posterior work without visualization.

Q: What is the blood loss risk and compartment monitoring need in closed femoral shaft fractures? A: 1-2L or more into thigh possible (closed). Monitor for compartment syndrome (tense thigh, pain out of proportion); may need fasciotomy or delayed closure.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Elevate or split vastus lateralis anteriorly from linea aspera. Identify and control perforators (profunda branches) as they cross from posterior to anterior. Expose the lateral femoral shaft subperiosteally for plate application. Protect anteromedial femoral bundle and posterior sciatic with retractors/position.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working layer for fracture reduction and plating. Perforator control and neurovascular protection are critical for safe, bloodless exposure.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After plate fixation and reduction check, close fascia loosely if swelling or compartment risk. Skin closure in layers. Monitor thigh compartments post-op (high risk in closed fractures with significant blood loss into thigh).

surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Thigh compartments can develop syndrome after shaft fractures/ORIF due to bleeding/swelling. Loose closure and monitoring prevent missed diagnosis.

Focus on deforming forces, perforator control, anteromedial femoral bundle and posterior sciatic protection, and safe lateral corridor. No hip arthroplasty, ankle, or tibial content mixed. Content is shaft-specific.

procedure_overview: Hallux valgus correction via distal chevron, proximal osteotomy, scarf, or Lapidus, combined with lateral soft tissue release (adductor hallucis, lateral capsule) and medial eminence resection/capsulorrhaphy. Critical anatomy: first metatarsal head blood supply (vulnerable to AVN with aggressive lateral release or over-stripping), lateral plantar nerve (proper digital branch to hallux) at risk during lateral release, sesamoid complex and windlass mechanism, angular measurements (HVA, IM angle, DMAA) guiding osteotomy level and type. Focus on balanced release without overcorrection (hallux varus) or undercorrection (recurrence/transfer metatarsalgia).
must_know_anatomy: Medial deviation of 1st MT, lateral deviation/pronation of hallux. Increased IM angle, HVA, DMAA. Sesamoids sublux lateral. Medial capsule contracture, lateral capsule/adductor pull hallux lateral. Deformity affects windlass mechanism and medial longitudinal arch support.

Osteotomy (chevron distal, proximal, scarf, Lapidus) chosen based on angular measurements to correct IM/HVA/DMAA. Medial eminence resection and capsular plication medially; lateral soft tissue release (adductor tendon, lateral capsule, sometimes transverse metatarsal ligament) but protect the lateral plantar nerve (proper digital branch) to the hallux.

First metatarsal head blood supply: dorsal and plantar metatarsal arteries and intraosseous supply. Aggressive lateral release or excessive capsular stripping can devascularize the head leading to AVN.

Sesamoid position and FHL tendon: lateral subluxation of sesamoids under the 1st MT head contributes to pronation and loss of medial column stability. Release and realignment aim to recenter the sesamoid complex under the head.
Lateral plantar nerve (to hallux): at risk during lateral soft tissue release via dorsal web space or through osteotomy. Injury causes numbness or neuropathic pain in the hallux pulp/plantar surface.

Common complications tied to anatomy: AVN of 1st MT head (aggressive lateral release), hallux varus (over-release or over-correction of IM angle), recurrence (inadequate correction or poor medial plication), transfer metatarsalgia (shortening or elevation of 1st ray).

structures_at_risk.structure: First metatarsal head blood supply (dorsal/plantar metatarsal arteries and intraosseous)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Aggressive lateral soft tissue release (adductor + lateral capsule) or excessive periosteal stripping for osteotomy can interrupt the tenuous supply to the capital fragment, especially with chevron or other distal osteotomies.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit lateral release to adductor tendon and lateral capsule only as needed for correction; avoid complete circumferential stripping of the metatarsal head. Use careful technique with chevron to preserve dorsal and plantar vessels.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: AVN of the first metatarsal head: collapse, flattening, joint incongruity, pain, and stiffness (hallux rigidus-like picture) in a young active patient.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial approach for eminence/osteotomy + lateral release (dorsal or trans-osteotomy)

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral plantar nerve (proper digital branch to hallux)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lateral soft tissue release (adductor hallucis insertion, lateral capsule, transverse metatarsal ligament) is performed in the first web space or through the osteotomy; the nerve lies plantar-lateral and can be transected or stretched.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect the nerve during dorsal web space release or limit release depth. Use direct visualization or careful blunt technique; do not blindly release plantar structures.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness, dysesthesia, or neuropathic pain on the plantar and lateral aspect of the hallux. Can be debilitating for push-off and shoe wear.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral soft tissue release component of hallux valgus correction

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Subluxed sesamoids and contracted lateral structures maintain the deformity. Over-release or improper realignment during correction can lead to medial sesamoid subluxation or FHL bowstringing after overcorrection.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Release only enough lateral structures to allow sesamoid recentralization under the head after osteotomy and medial plication. Avoid complete sesamoid excision or excessive medial displacement of the capital fragment.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Hallux varus (overcorrection), loss of push-off power, or painful callus under lesser metatarsals (transfer metatarsalgia).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral release + medial capsulorrhaphy + osteotomy realignment

structures_at_risk.structure: Dorsal medial cutaneous nerve (superficial peroneal branch) and medial dorsal sensory nerves
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Medial incision over the first metatarsophalangeal joint and eminence; subcutaneous dissection or retraction can injure superficial sensory branches.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect cutaneous nerves during skin incision and subcutaneous flap elevation. Use full-thickness flaps where possible and gentle retraction.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or painful neuroma on the dorsomedial hallux or forefoot, affecting shoe wear and sensation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial approach to first MTP for eminence resection and capsular work

key_landmarks: First metatarsal head and medial eminence (prominence to resect)
Sesamoids (plantar, assess subluxation on AP and sesamoid views)
First metatarsophalangeal joint and proximal phalanx base
First tarsometatarsal joint (for Lapidus fusion decision)
Intermetatarsal angle reference and hallux valgus angle on weightbearing AP
key_intervals: Medial: skin, subcutaneous tissue (protect dorsal medial cutaneous nerve branches from superficial peroneal), joint capsule (medial capsulotomy or Y-shaped for plication).

Lateral release: dorsal first web space interval or through distal osteotomy; adductor hallucis tendon, lateral capsule, transverse metatarsal ligament; protect lateral plantar digital nerve plantar-laterally.

Osteotomy plane: for chevron, V-cut in distal metaphysis preserving dorsal and plantar blood supply to head; scarf is longitudinal with transverse limbs; proximal osteotomies or Lapidus at TMT require more proximal exposure.
common_mistakes: Aggressive lateral release causing 1st MT head AVN
Overcorrection of IM angle or excessive medial plication leading to hallux varus
Inadequate lateral release or poor medial capsular repair resulting in recurrence
Shortening or dorsiflexion of the 1st ray causing transfer metatarsalgia under 2nd/3rd heads
Injury to lateral plantar nerve or dorsal cutaneous branches during release or medial approach
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is the blood supply to the first metatarsal head and why is aggressive lateral soft tissue release a risk factor for AVN during hallux valgus correction? A: Dorsal and plantar metatarsal arteries plus intraosseous supply. Aggressive adductor + lateral capsule release plus stripping can devascularize the capital fragment after distal osteotomy (chevron), leading to head collapse.

Q: Describe the windlass mechanism and how hallux valgus deformity affects the medial longitudinal arch and sesamoid position. A: Windlass tightens plantar fascia with hallux dorsiflexion to raise the arch. Hallux valgus pronates and subluxes the sesamoids laterally, weakening the medial column and allowing arch collapse and metatarsalgia.

Q: What are the key angular measurements (IM angle, HVA, DMAA) and how do they guide osteotomy choice (distal chevron vs proximal vs Lapidus)? A: HVA (hallux valgus angle), IM (1-2 intermetatarsal), DMAA (distal metatarsal articular angle). Mild: distal chevron. Moderate-severe with high IM: proximal osteotomy or scarf. Hypermobile TMT or high IM with instability: Lapidus fusion.

Q: During lateral soft tissue release for hallux valgus, which nerve is at risk and what structure does it innervate? A: Lateral plantar nerve (proper digital branch to the hallux). It provides plantar and lateral sensation to the hallux; injury causes numbness or neuropathic pain affecting push-off.

Q: What are common mistakes leading to hallux varus, recurrence, or transfer metatarsalgia after hallux valgus correction? A: Hallux varus: over-release of lateral structures or over-correction of IM. Recurrence: under-correction or inadequate medial plication. Transfer metatarsalgia: 1st ray shortening/elevation from osteotomy malposition or excessive shortening.
night_before_review_checklist: Review OB hallux valgus page (12134) and the anatomy_hallux_valgus_correction module for angular criteria and release risks
Memorize: protect lateral plantar n. during lateral release; limit release to avoid 1st MT head AVN; sesamoids must be recentralized
Know how HVA/IM/DMAA dictate distal chevron vs proximal/scarf vs Lapidus
Review consequences: AVN (aggressive lateral), varus (overcorrection), recurrence (undercorrection), transfer MTalgia (ray malposition)
Practice the 5 specific pimp questions with source-backed answers and avoidance pearls
Confirm case-specific osteotomy choice and any arthrodesis (Lapidus) vs osteotomy plan
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique, fixation choice, or attending-specific osteotomy preferences
Always confirm angular measurements on weightbearing radiographs and assess first TMT stability for Lapidus vs osteotomy decision
Lateral release technique (dorsal web vs trans-articular) varies; nerve protection is critical regardless of approach variant
approach_name: Medial approach to first MTP + lateral soft tissue release (dorsal web or trans-osteotomy)

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision centered over medial eminence or slightly dorsal. Subcutaneous tissue contains branches of the dorsal medial cutaneous nerve (superficial peroneal). Elevate full-thickness flaps to protect these sensory nerves and the capsule.
key_structures: Dorsal medial cutaneous nerve branches (superficial peroneal)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Safe medial access for eminence resection, medial capsulotomy/plication, and osteotomy. Nerve injury is a common source of patient dissatisfaction.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Y- or L-shaped capsulotomy allows later plication. Medial capsule is contracted. After osteotomy and lateral release, imbricate the capsule to correct pronation and reduce the medial eminence redundancy.
key_structures: Abductor hallucis (may be released or plicated)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Medial soft tissue balancing is required for stable correction after bony realignment. Inadequate plication contributes to recurrence.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Performed dorsally in first web space or through the osteotomy site. Release adductor hallucis insertion on proximal phalanx and lateral capsule (sometimes transverse metatarsal ligament). The lateral plantar digital nerve lies just plantar to the release plane and must be protected.
key_structures: Lateral plantar nerve (proper digital to hallux)
structures_at_risk: First MT head blood supply (if over-released or stripped)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Adequate but not excessive lateral release allows the sesamoids to reduce under the head after osteotomy. Over-release is the main driver of AVN and varus.

danger_zones: Aggressive lateral release or circumferential stripping of 1st MT head (AVN risk) - release only adductor + lateral capsule as needed
Lateral plantar digital nerve during web space or trans-osteotomy release (numbness, neuropathic pain to hallux)
Overcorrection of IM angle or excessive medial capsular plication (hallux varus deformity)
Dorsal medial cutaneous nerve branches on medial approach (neuroma or dorsal hallux numbness affecting shoe wear)
approach_specific_notes: Hallux valgus correction uses a medial approach over the first MTP for eminence work and osteotomy, plus a lateral release (usually dorsal first web space) to balance the soft tissues and recenter the sesamoids. The key superficial nerve at risk medially is the dorsal medial cutaneous (superficial peroneal branch); the critical nerve on lateral release is the lateral plantar proper digital nerve to the hallux. Osteotomy choice (chevron distal vs proximal/scarf vs Lapidus) is driven by angular measurements and TMT stability. All anatomy is forefoot-specific; do not mix with ankle, midfoot, or plantar fascia procedures.

procedure_overview: Hip Hemiarthroplasty (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Direct lateral (Hardinge): split IT band, split gluteus medius/vastus lateralis (limit proximal split to 3-5 cm above greater trochanter to protect superior gluteal nerve).
Greater trochanter is the key landmark. Detach anterior portion of gluteus medius/minimus and vastus lateralis from the GT for anterior exposure of femoral neck and head.
Superior gluteal nerve at risk if split extends >3-5 cm proximal to GT; runs within gluteus medius approximately 5 cm above the trochanter.

Short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus) lie posterior; for hemi they may be preserved or partially released depending on exposure needs. Sciatic nerve lies posterior and is protected by limited posterior dissection.

Abductor repair at closure: suture the anterior gluteus medius/minimus and vastus lateralis flap back to the greater trochanter (bone tunnels or anchors) to restore abductor mechanism and prevent Trendelenburg gait.

Femoral neck cut and head removal: trial head sizing to restore offset and leg length. Acetabulum may be reamed lightly for bipolar if cartilage poor. No full posterior capsule repair required for hemi.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Incision centered over greater trochanter. Split IT band in line with fibers. Split gluteus medius and vastus lateralis anteriorly from the trochanter, limiting the proximal extent to 3-5 cm above the GT to protect the superior gluteal nerve.
Detach the anterior gluteus medius/minimus and anterior vastus lateralis from the greater trochanter as a flap. Expose the anterior capsule and femoral neck for head removal.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Courses ~5 cm proximal to GT within gluteus medius. Excessive proximal split in Hardinge approach can transect or stretch it.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit the proximal split of gluteus medius/vastus lateralis to 3-5 cm above the greater trochanter. Use blunt dissection and identify the nerve if extending proximally.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Abductor weakness, Trendelenburg gait, difficulty with single-leg stance and stair climbing. Often permanent if complete transection.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct lateral (Hardinge) for hip hemiarthroplasty

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies posterior; at risk with excessive posterior retraction or deep dissection during femoral preparation in hemi.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Maintain awareness of posterior structures. Use anteriorly directed retractors for femoral exposure. Avoid aggressive posterior release unless necessary for version correction.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop, sensory loss. Less common in pure lateral but possible with hemi dislocation maneuvers.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Hardinge approach during hip dislocation and stem insertion

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Detached anteriorly from GT for exposure. Inadequate repair leads to abductor insufficiency.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Tag and preserve as much tendon as possible on the detached anterior flap. Repair securely to GT with non-absorbable suture through bone or anchors at closure. Protect repair post-op with abduction precautions if needed.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Trendelenburg limp, abductor weakness, hip instability in stance phase. Common cause of poor function after lateral approach hemi.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct lateral Hardinge - exposure and closure

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Fracture or osteotomy of GT (if used) or poor reattachment of abductors can compromise function.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use careful osteotomy technique if extending exposure. Secure reattachment of anterior abductors and vastus lateralis to GT.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Abductor lurch, pain, need for revision abductor repair.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Hardinge for hemiarthroplasty

key_landmarks: Greater trochanter: incision center and abductor detachment/repair site for Hardinge hemi.
Femoral neck and head: primary working landmarks after anterior abductor flap elevation.
IT band: split in line with fibers as superficial interval to gluteus medius split.
common_mistakes: Extending the gluteus medius split too far proximal (>5 cm above GT), injuring superior gluteal nerve and causing permanent abductor weakness.
Inadequate repair of anterior abductors to the greater trochanter, leading to Trendelenburg gait and poor function.
Over-retraction posteriorly during femoral broaching, risking sciatic nerve injury.
Poor head sizing or version leading to instability or leg length discrepancy in hemiarthroplasty.
Failure to protect the abductor repair postoperatively with appropriate precautions.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What nerve is at risk in direct lateral (Hardinge) approach and how far proximal can you safely split the gluteus medius? A: Superior gluteal nerve. Limit the split to 3-5 cm proximal to the greater trochanter to avoid injury.

Q: Describe the key steps of the Hardinge approach for hip hemiarthroplasty and the primary structure at risk during the split. A: Split IT band, split gluteus medius/vl anteriorly (limit 3-5cm above GT for sup. gluteal n.), detach anterior abductors from GT. Primary risk: superior gluteal nerve leading to abductor insufficiency.
Q: Why is repair of the anterior abductors critical after Hardinge hemiarthroplasty? A: Restores the abductor mechanism attached to GT. Failure causes Trendelenburg gait and poor single-leg stance.

Q: How is the sciatic nerve protected during Hardinge exposure for hemi? A: Limited posterior dissection, anteriorly directed retractors for femoral work, awareness of its position posterior to the femur during broaching.

Q: What is the typical head size selection and stability assessment in hemiarthroplasty via lateral approach? A: Trial heads to restore leg length and offset; check stability in flexion/adduction and extension/external rotation. Avoid overstuffing the joint.

Memorize key SAR and landmarks
Practice the 10+ pimp questions
Walk through the night-before checklist
Confirm any case-specific fluoroscopy or approach details
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique or attending-specific preferences
Always verify with latest imaging and team briefing

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to IT band and gluteus medius split)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision centered over greater trochanter. Split IT band in line with fibers. Split gluteus medius and vastus lateralis anteriorly from the trochanter, limiting the proximal extent to 3-5 cm above the GT to protect the superior gluteal nerve.
structures_at_risk: Superior gluteal nerve (if split >3-5 cm proximal to GT)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard lateral approach for hemiarthroplasty. Provides good anterior exposure of neck and head while preserving most of the posterior abductors. The split limit is the key safety point.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (abductor detachment and femoral neck exposure)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Detach the anterior gluteus medius/minimus and anterior vastus lateralis from the greater trochanter as a flap. Expose the anterior capsule and femoral neck. Release capsule as needed for head dislocation. Protect sciatic nerve with limited posterior retraction.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Gives direct access to the femoral head/neck for hemi. The anterior abductor flap must be repaired back to bone at the end to restore function.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After stem and head implantation and reduction, repair the anterior gluteus medius/minimus and vastus lateralis flap securely to the greater trochanter using heavy suture through bone tunnels or anchors. Close IT band and skin in layers. Abductor repair is critical for hemi outcome.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The repair layer that determines postoperative abductor function. Poor repair is a common source of limp after lateral approach hemi.

validation_warnings: Hip hemiarthroplasty payload focused on Hardinge (direct lateral) approach, which is one common technique for hemi. Some surgeons prefer posterior for hemi; this payload does not cover posterior hemi details. Source material was previously mixed across hip approaches; cleaned to Hardinge-specific facts only.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for hip hemiarthroplasty via direct lateral (Hardinge) approach. Common for hemi because it provides excellent anterior exposure of the neck/head with the patient supine or lateral. The defining safety point is limiting the abductor split to protect the superior gluteal nerve. No full posterior ER release or anterior TFL/sartorius interval is used. Abductor repair to the GT at closure is as important as the exposure itself for functional outcome. Content is lateral/Hardinge-specific; posterior or anterior THA anatomy is not mixed in.
danger_zones: Superior gluteal nerve: limit Hardinge split to 3-5 cm above GT; injury causes permanent abductor weakness and Trendelenburg gait.
Abductor repair: secure reattachment of anterior flap to GT is essential; failure leads to abductor insufficiency and poor function.
Sciatic nerve: protect with limited posterior retraction during femoral work; injury causes foot drop.
Greater trochanter: avoid fracture during exposure or stem insertion; poor abductor reattachment site compromises outcome.

procedure_name: Humeral Shaft Fracture ORIF (Plate Fixation via Anterolateral or Posterior Approaches)
procedure_overview: Plate ORIF of humeral shaft fractures when IM nailing not chosen (distal metaphyseal, radial nerve exploration, open fractures, or specific deformity). Two main approaches: anterolateral distal (brachialis split or lateral to it) and posterior (triceps split). Radial nerve is the dominant structure at risk in both (spiral groove on posterior humerus, exits lateral septum in distal 1/3 - Holstein-Lewis pattern vulnerable). Profunda brachii artery travels with radial nerve in spiral groove. Goal: anatomic reduction, stable plate fixation (usually 4.5 mm DCP or LCP), radial nerve protection or exploration.
must_know_anatomy: Radial nerve course: exits spiral groove ~4-7 cm from acromion? (posterior to anterolateral at mid/distal third junction), pierces lateral intermuscular septum ~10-12 cm proximal to lateral epicondyle, then lies between brachialis and brachioradialis. Holstein-Lewis fracture (distal 1/3 spiral) has high association with radial nerve injury at the septum exit.

Anterolateral distal approach: interval or split in brachialis (dual innervation: musculocutaneous lateral, radial medial - safe split). Develop between brachialis and brachioradialis distally. Radial nerve identified and protected as it exits the septum laterally. Plate placed on anterolateral or anterior surface.

Posterior triceps-split approach: split long and lateral heads of triceps (or medial/lateral for more distal). Radial nerve and profunda brachii lie in spiral groove on posterior humerus and must be identified, mobilized, and protected (vessel loop) before plate application. Good for posterior plating or radial n. exploration.

Profunda brachii (deep brachial artery) and its branches travel with the radial nerve in the spiral groove; at risk with posterior exposure or fracture displacement. Ligation or repair may be needed if injured.

Brachialis muscle: key anterior landmark; splitting it (or working lateral to it) provides access to the anterior humerus while protecting the radial nerve (which is posterior/lateral).
