The musculocutaneous nerve (lateral antebrachial cutaneous) is at risk with excessive lateral retraction or proximal dissection.

Fracture reduction and plate: restore length, alignment, rotation. Use 4.5 mm broad or narrow plate with at least 3-4 screws proximal and distal (8-10 cortices). Lag screws for oblique/spiral patterns. Avoid radial nerve entrapment under plate or screws.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies in spiral groove (posterior humerus) and exits laterally through intermuscular septum in distal third (Holstein-Lewis vulnerability). At risk from fracture displacement, callus, or iatrogenic injury during anterolateral (exiting septum) or posterior (in groove) exposure/plating.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect (vessel loop) early in both approaches. In anterolateral: find as it exits septum between brachialis/brachioradialis. In posterior: mobilize from spiral groove before splitting triceps fully or placing plate. Document wrist/thumb extension and dorsal hand sensation pre- and post-op.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Wrist drop, loss of finger/thumb MP extension, loss of thumb abduction/extension, sensory loss first dorsal web. Usually neurapraxia from stretch/contusion but can be lacerated or entrapped; exploration indicated for open fractures or no recovery.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Both anterolateral distal and posterior triceps-split approaches for humeral shaft ORIF

structures_at_risk.structure: Profunda brachii artery (and radial collateral branches)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Travels with radial nerve in spiral groove on posterior humerus; can be lacerated by fracture or during posterior exposure/mobilization of the nerve.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the neurovascular bundle in the spiral groove during posterior approach before full triceps split or plate placement. Control bleeding with bipolar or ties; repair if major branch injured.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Bleeding/hematoma, ischemia to posterior compartment or radial nerve (worsening palsy), or false aneurysm. May require vascular repair.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior triceps-split approach (primary); also relevant in displaced Holstein-Lewis patterns

structures_at_risk.structure: Musculocutaneous nerve / lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (in brachialis)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Musculocutaneous nerve enters brachialis proximally; its terminal lateral antebrachial cutaneous branch can be injured with anterolateral dissection, excessive retraction, or proximal plate placement.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit proximal dissection in the anterolateral approach. Identify and protect the nerve on the undersurface of brachialis if extending proximally. Split brachialis in midline or slightly lateral when using anterolateral.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of brachialis contribution to elbow flexion (usually compensated by biceps/brachioradialis) and numbness over lateral forearm (lateral antebrachial cutaneous distribution).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterolateral distal approach (brachialis work)

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral intermuscular septum (radial nerve exit point)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The radial nerve pierces the septum in the distal third; fracture patterns or aggressive retraction/plate placement at this level can tether or injure the nerve.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: In Holstein-Lewis or distal third fractures, expect the nerve at the septum. In anterolateral approach, deliberately identify the nerve exiting the septum before distal plate extension.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Radial nerve entrapment or laceration at the septum; persistent or new palsy post-op.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Distal third humeral shaft ORIF (both approaches, especially anterolateral)

key_landmarks: Lateral epicondyle / supracondylar ridge (distal reference for anterolateral approach and plate)
Spiral groove (posterior humerus - radial n. + profunda location on posterior surface)
Lateral intermuscular septum (radial n. exit point ~10-12 cm proximal to lateral epicondyle)
Brachialis (anterior landmark - split or lateral border for anterolateral exposure)
Triceps (long/lateral heads for posterior split; radial n. in groove deep to it)
key_intervals: Anterolateral distal: develop or split brachialis (safe due to dual innervation); distally between brachialis and brachioradialis. Radial n. protected as it exits septum laterally.
Posterior triceps split: split between long and lateral heads (or more distal medial/lateral heads). Identify and mobilize radial n. + profunda from spiral groove before bone work or plating.
Brachioradialis (lateral) is a key landmark distally for both approaches; radial n. lies between it and brachialis anteriorly after exiting the septum.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: Anterolateral or posterior plate placement on the humerus (4.5 mm DCP/LCP preferred). At least 3-4 bicortical screws above and below fracture (8-10 cortices total). Lag screw compression for spiral/oblique patterns through or outside plate.

Radial nerve exploration/decompression: indicated for open fractures, severe displacement, or new palsy after closed reduction. Nerve is identified proximally in the spiral groove or at septum exit and traced through the zone of injury.

Holstein-Lewis pattern (distal third spiral fracture with butterfly): high radial nerve entrapment risk at septum; often requires anterolateral approach for direct nerve visualization and plate that bypasses the nerve laterally.
common_mistakes: Failure to identify and protect the radial nerve at the lateral intermuscular septum (anterolateral) or in the spiral groove (posterior), resulting in iatrogenic palsy
Placing the plate over or entrapping the radial nerve, especially in distal third or Holstein-Lewis patterns
Inadequate proximal/distal screw purchase or failure to use lag screws for spiral patterns leading to loss of reduction
Excessive retraction on brachialis or brachioradialis injuring the radial nerve or its branches (or musculocutaneous terminal branch)
Ignoring the profunda brachii during posterior exposure, leading to bleeding or nerve ischemia
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP and lateral of entire humerus (include shoulder and elbow joints) to confirm length, alignment, rotation, and plate/screw position outside the joint.
Confirm radial nerve is not under the plate on final images (if explored, document its position relative to plate).
Check for varus/valgus and flexion/extension deformity on orthogonal views before final fixation.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: Describe the radial nerve course on the humeral shaft and why Holstein-Lewis (distal third spiral) fractures have a high association with radial nerve injury. A: The nerve runs in the spiral groove posteriorly then pierces the lateral intermuscular septum ~10-12 cm proximal to the lateral epicondyle to lie between brachialis and brachioradialis. In Holstein-Lewis, the nerve is tethered at the septum and vulnerable to the fracture spike or callus.

Q: What is the key maneuver or landmark to find and protect the radial nerve in the anterolateral distal approach for humeral shaft ORIF? A: Identify the nerve as it exits the lateral intermuscular septum between brachialis and brachioradialis distally; protect it with a vessel loop before extending the plate distally.

Q: In the posterior triceps-split approach, where is the radial nerve and profunda brachii, and what must be done before plate application? A: They lie together in the spiral groove on the posterior humerus deep to the triceps. The bundle must be identified, mobilized gently, and protected (loop) before splitting the triceps fully or placing the posterior plate.

Q: Why is brachialis splitting (or working lateral to it) safe in the anterolateral approach, and what nerve is at risk with excessive proximal or lateral retraction? A: Brachialis has dual innervation (musculocutaneous laterally, radial medially), so midline split is safe. The radial nerve (after exiting septum) and lateral antebrachial cutaneous (terminal musculocutaneous) are at risk with over-retraction.

Q: What are the indications to choose plate ORIF over IM nailing for humeral shaft fractures, and what is the minimum fixation construct? A: Distal metaphyseal fractures, need for radial nerve exploration, open fractures with specific soft tissue needs, or when nailing is contraindicated. Use 4.5 mm plate with 3-4 screws (8-10 cortices) proximal and distal, plus lag screws for spiral patterns.
night_before_review_checklist: Review OB humeral shaft fractures (1016) and the two approach modules (anterolateral distal + posterior triceps split) for radial n. course, Holstein-Lewis, and protection steps
Memorize: radial n. in spiral groove (posterior) → exits lateral septum (anterolateral danger) → between brachialis/brachioradialis; profunda travels with it
Know approach choice: anterolateral for distal/radial n. exploration; posterior for direct groove access or posterior plating
Review consequences of radial n. injury (wrist drop, finger/thumb extension loss) and documentation requirements
Practice the 5 pimp questions with specific landmarks (septum exit, spiral groove) and protection maneuvers per approach
Confirm case factors: fracture location/pattern, open vs closed, pre-op radial n. function, planned approach and plate type
limitations: Anatomy and approach focused case prep for humeral shaft plate ORIF only; does not replace full trauma decision-making (IMN vs plate), radial nerve recovery timeline/exploration thresholds, or rehab protocols
Radial nerve status must be documented pre- and post-operatively; exploration is mandatory for open fractures or new complete palsy after reduction
approach_name: Anterolateral distal or posterior triceps-split approaches for humeral shaft ORIF

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision along the lateral border of biceps or anterolateral humerus. Develop plane or split brachialis (safe dual innervation). Distally, work between brachialis and brachioradialis. Identify radial nerve exiting the lateral intermuscular septum and protect before distal exposure or plating. Plate on anterolateral or anterior humerus.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Preferred for distal third fractures and when radial nerve exploration is needed. Direct visualization of the nerve at its vulnerable exit point.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Patient lateral or prone. Incision midline posterior arm. Split between long and lateral heads of triceps (or more distal heads). Identify the radial nerve and profunda brachii in the spiral groove on the posterior humerus; mobilize gently with a vessel loop before completing the split or applying the plate. Good exposure of the entire posterior humerus.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Direct access to the radial nerve in its groove and excellent for posterior plating or when the nerve is already injured and needs exploration/decompression.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep exposure and nerve protection (common to both)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After identifying the radial nerve (and profunda in posterior), the fracture is reduced and a 4.5 mm plate is applied with compression (lag screws) or neutralization. The nerve is inspected, freed of callus if entrapped, and documented to lie free of the plate and screws. Brachialis or triceps is closed over the plate.
key_structures: Radial nerve (protected with loop or tucked safely)
structures_at_risk: Radial nerve entrapment under plate or screws
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The critical step that prevents iatrogenic nerve injury. Always visualize and protect the nerve before final fixation in these approaches.

danger_zones: Radial nerve at lateral intermuscular septum exit (anterolateral approach) or in spiral groove (posterior) - identify early, protect with loop; Holstein-Lewis patterns especially dangerous
Profunda brachii artery traveling with radial nerve in posterior groove - control during posterior exposure; injury causes bleeding or nerve ischemia
Placing plate or screws over or around the radial nerve - always inspect nerve position relative to final construct
Excessive retraction on brachioradialis or brachialis - risks radial nerve or its lateral antebrachial cutaneous branch
approach_specific_notes: Humeral shaft ORIF uses either anterolateral distal (brachialis work, radial n. at septum exit) or posterior triceps-split (direct groove access). Both require explicit radial nerve identification and protection. This is pure trauma exposure for diaphyseal or distal metaphyseal fractures; do not mix with proximal humerus, elbow arthroplasty, or distal humerus ORIF anatomy. IM nailing is an alternative but has different radial nerve risks at the distal locking screws.

procedure_name: Lateral Ankle Ligament Repair (Brostrom +/- Gould Augmentation)
procedure_overview: Anatomic repair of lateral ankle ligaments (primarily ATFL, often CFL) for chronic instability after failed nonoperative care. Brostrom procedure: imbricate or reattach the attenuated ATFL (and CFL) to the fibula. Gould modification: augment with inferior extensor retinaculum. Key anatomy is superficial (sural nerve, peroneal tendons) and the ligament footprints on fibula and talus/calcaneus. Approach is anterior or curvilinear over the ATFL/CFL origin on the anterior distal fibula.
must_know_anatomy: Lateral ankle ligaments: ATFL (most commonly injured, from anterior distal fibula to talar neck, resists anterior talar translation and inversion in plantarflexion), CFL (from distal fibula tip to calcaneus, resists inversion in dorsiflexion, also stabilizes subtalar), PTFL (posterior, rarely primary surgical target). ATFL is intracapsular; CFL is extra-capsular.

Lateral malleolus landmarks: anterior distal fibula (ATFL origin), tip/fibular groove (CFL and peroneal tendons), peroneal tubercle on calcaneus (peroneal tendon separator). Sinus tarsi is the lateral soft spot anterior to the lateral malleolus tip.

Brostrom repair: anterior or curvilinear incision over ATFL/CFL. Identify and imbricate the attenuated ligament or reattach to fibula with anchors/sutures. Test stability. Gould augmentation: mobilize and advance the inferior extensor retinaculum over the repair for additional strength and to reinforce against inversion.

Superficial peroneal nerve (SPN) and sural nerve: SPN (or its intermediate dorsal cutaneous branch) crosses the field anteriorly and can be injured in the proximal incision or during retraction. Sural nerve is posterior, near the peroneal tendons and CFL footprint; at risk with posterior extension or peroneal tendon work.

Peroneal tendons (peroneus longus and brevis): lie in the retromalleolar groove and can be subluxed or injured during posterior dissection for CFL or if the incision is placed too far posterior. The peroneal tubercle on the calcaneus is a landmark for the tendons distally.

Inferior extensor retinaculum (IER): lies superficial to the ATFL/CFL; mobilized in Gould augmentation to reinforce the repair. Also protects the extensor tendons (EHL, EDL, EDB) and deep peroneal nerve more anteriorly.

structures_at_risk.structure: Sural nerve (and its lateral dorsal cutaneous branch)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Courses posterior to the lateral malleolus, near the peroneal tendons and CFL origin on the fibula tip. At risk during posterior or distal extension of the incision, retraction, or dissection around the peroneal tendons/CFL footprint.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Keep the incision anterior or gently curved over the anterior fibula and ATFL origin. Identify the nerve if the incision extends posteriorly; protect or resect/bury the end if injured. Avoid deep posterior retraction near the peroneals.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or painful neuroma over the lateral foot and 5th toe. Can cause chronic pain with shoe wear or uneven surfaces.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral ankle Brostrom/Gould repair (posterior or distal incision extension, peroneal work)

structures_at_risk.structure: Superficial peroneal nerve (intermediate dorsal cutaneous branch)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the anterior ankle and can enter the proximal or anterior portion of the lateral incision. Vulnerable during skin incision, subcutaneous dissection, or retraction of the proximal skin flap.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Place the incision carefully (anterior or curvilinear over the ATFL). Identify the nerve in the subcutaneous tissue proximally and protect it with a vessel loop or gentle retraction medially/laterally as the interval is developed.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or neuroma on the dorsum of the foot (dorsal 3rd/4th web spaces or dorsolateral foot). Can be more symptomatic than sural injury for some patients.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral ankle incision (proximal or anterior portion)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lie immediately posterior to the distal fibula in the retromalleolar groove and can be subluxed or directly injured if the incision or dissection extends too far posteriorly for CFL exposure or if retractors are placed aggressively.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Keep the main incision anterior over the ATFL origin on the fibula. If CFL work or peroneal exploration is needed, extend carefully and identify the tendons in their sheath before retraction. Repair any tendon injury or retinacular tear.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Peroneal tendon subluxation, tear, or tenosynovitis postoperatively; chronic lateral ankle pain and instability that can compromise the ligament repair outcome.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral ankle (CFL exposure or posterior extension)

structures_at_risk.structure: Inferior extensor retinaculum (IER) and extensor tendons (EHL/EDL)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The IER lies superficial to the ATFL and is mobilized for Gould augmentation; the extensor tendons (EHL, EDL, EDB) and deep peroneal nerve lie just anterior/medial and can be injured with overly anterior dissection or retraction.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Mobilize the IER as a flap for augmentation without detaching it completely from its insertions. Stay lateral to the extensor tendons; identify EHL/EDL if the incision drifts anteriorly.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of IER augmentation strength (higher failure risk), extensor tendon injury (drop toe or weakness), or deep peroneal nerve injury (first web numbness, EDB weakness).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Superficial dissection and Gould augmentation

key_landmarks: Anterior distal fibula / ATFL origin (primary incision landmark and repair site)
Lateral malleolus tip and fibular groove (CFL origin, peroneal tendon location)
Sinus tarsi (soft spot anterior to fibula tip; helps orientation for ATFL/CFL)
Peroneal tubercle on calcaneus (distal peroneal tendon landmark)
Inferior extensor retinaculum (superficial to ATFL; mobilized for Gould augmentation)
key_intervals: Skin and subcutaneous: anterior or curvilinear incision centered over the ATFL origin on the anterior distal fibula. Identify and protect SPN (anterior/proximal) and sural (posterior) in the subcutaneous plane.

Ligament layer: ATFL (intracapsular, from fibula to talar neck) is imbricated or reattached to fibula. CFL (extra-capsular, fibula tip to calcaneus) is identified and repaired if attenuated. IER is mobilized superficially for Gould augmentation.

Deep: anterior capsule may be opened or plicated with the ligaments. Peroneal tendons are protected posteriorly if CFL work is performed. No deep neurovascular structures in the direct lateral ankle for this repair.
common_mistakes: Injury to sural or superficial peroneal nerve from poorly placed incision or inadequate subcutaneous protection (chronic neuroma pain)
Failure to mobilize and advance the inferior extensor retinaculum in Gould augmentation (higher recurrence rate)
Overly posterior incision or aggressive retraction injuring or subluxing the peroneal tendons (post-op peroneal pain and instability)
Inadequate imbrication or anchor placement on the fibula leading to recurrent instability
Ignoring associated pathology (peroneal tears, osteochondral lesions, impingement) that must be addressed at the same time
attending_pimp_questions: Q: Which lateral ankle ligament is most commonly injured and what are its attachments and function? A: ATFL (anterior talofibular ligament). Originates from the anterior distal fibula and inserts on the talar neck. Primary restraint to anterior talar translation and inversion in plantarflexion. Intracapsular.

Q: What is the Gould modification of the Brostrom procedure and why is the inferior extensor retinaculum used? A: After anatomic repair/imbrication of the ATFL (and CFL), the IER is mobilized and advanced over the repair to augment strength and provide additional lateral restraint. It improves outcomes in patients with poor tissue quality or high demand.

Q: Which nerves are at risk in the lateral ankle Brostrom/Gould incision and where are they relative to the landmarks? A: Superficial peroneal nerve (or intermediate dorsal cutaneous branch) anteriorly/proximally (crosses near the proximal incision). Sural nerve posteriorly (near peroneal tendons and CFL footprint on fibula tip). Identify in subcutaneous tissue.

Q: Where is the CFL origin and how does its function differ from ATFL? A: CFL originates from the tip of the lateral malleolus (fibular groove area) and runs to the calcaneus (posterior to peroneal tubercle). It resists inversion in dorsiflexion and contributes to subtalar stability. Extra-capsular, unlike ATFL.

Q: What are common mistakes leading to failure or nerve injury in lateral ankle ligament repair? A: Nerve injury (sural or SPN) from poor incision placement or retraction; failure to augment with IER (Gould) when tissue is poor; missing peroneal tendon pathology or osteochondral lesions; inadequate fixation of the repair to the fibula.
night_before_review_checklist: Review OB ankle ligament injury (3002) and the lateral ankle ligament modules (footprint and anatomy) for ATFL/CFL/PTFL, Brostrom/Gould technique, and nerve/tendon risks
Memorize landmarks: anterior distal fibula (ATFL), fibula tip (CFL + peroneals), sinus tarsi, peroneal tubercle, IER for augmentation
Know the two nerves (SPN anterior/proximal, sural posterior) and peroneal tendons (protect if CFL work)
Review Gould augmentation steps and why IER is used (strength and reinforcement)
Practice the 5 pimp questions with ligament functions, nerve locations relative to incision, and failure modes
Confirm case details: isolated ATFL vs ATFL+CFL, tissue quality, any peroneal or OCD findings on MRI, planned incision and augmentation
limitations: Anatomy and surgical exposure focused case prep for Brostrom +/- Gould lateral ligament repair only; does not replace full decision-making for when to operate, associated pathology (peroneal tears, OCD, impingement, hindfoot alignment), or rehab protocols
Always assess for and address concomitant injuries (peroneal subluxation/tears, osteochondral lesions) at the time of ligament repair
approach_name: Lateral ankle approach for Brostrom +/- Gould ligament repair

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anterior or curvilinear incision centered over the ATFL origin on the anterior distal fibula (extend as needed for CFL). In the subcutaneous plane, identify and protect the superficial peroneal nerve (or intermediate dorsal cutaneous branch) anteriorly/proximally and the sural nerve posteriorly near the peroneals. Raise full-thickness flaps where possible.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Nerve protection is the first priority in this superficial exposure. Poor incision placement or blind retraction is the most common source of chronic neuroma pain after ankle ligament surgery.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Identify the attenuated ATFL (intracapsular, from fibula to talar neck) and CFL (extra-capsular, fibula tip to calcaneus). Imbricate the ligament or reattach to the fibula with anchors or transosseous sutures. Test anterior drawer and inversion stability after repair. Mobilize the IER as a flap for augmentation.
structures_at_risk: Peroneal tendons (if posterior extension for CFL)
Extensor tendons / EHL (if incision drifts too anterior)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The repair itself. Anatomic restoration of ATFL (and CFL when involved) is the goal; IER augmentation (Gould) adds strength especially when native tissue is poor.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Advance the mobilized inferior extensor retinaculum over the ligament repair and suture it to the fibula or periosteum to reinforce the construct. Close the subcutaneous layer and skin carefully to avoid nerve entrapment. Peroneal tendons and retinaculum are inspected/repaired if pathology was present.
key_structures: Skin and subcutaneous (nerve protection during closure)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The augmentation step that differentiates Gould from standard Brostrom and improves outcomes in higher-risk patients. Proper IER mobilization without detaching it completely is key.

danger_zones: Sural nerve posterior to fibula tip / near peroneal tendons - keep incision anterior, identify if extending posteriorly; injury = lateral foot/5th toe neuroma or numbness
Superficial peroneal (intermediate dorsal cutaneous) anteriorly/proximally - protect in subcutaneous plane during proximal incision and flap elevation; injury = dorsal foot numbness/neuroma
Peroneal tendons in retromalleolar groove - protect if CFL exposure or posterior work is needed; avoid iatrogenic subluxation or tear
Inadequate IER mobilization or repair in Gould augmentation - higher recurrence of instability; mobilize as a flap and advance without complete detachment
approach_specific_notes: Lateral ankle Brostrom +/- Gould is a superficial soft-tissue procedure focused on the ATFL/CFL footprints on the fibula, talus, and calcaneus. The key superficial nerves are SPN (anterior) and sural (posterior); peroneal tendons are immediately posterior and must be protected. The IER is used for augmentation in the Gould modification. This is distinct from ankle fracture ORIF (which may use similar skin but involves bone and syndesmosis), arthroscopic lateral ligament repair (portals), or midfoot/plantar procedures. All content is specific to chronic lateral ankle instability repair.

procedure_overview: Meniscus Repair (soft_tissue_repair). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Medial and lateral menisci: shock absorption, load distribution, joint stability. Zones (red-red, red-white, white-white) determine healing potential and repair vs meniscectomy decisions. Root tears and ramp lesions are important variants.

Standard arthroscopic portals: anterolateral (viewing) just lateral to patellar tendon at joint line; anteromedial (working) under vision. Accessory posteromedial or posterolateral for posterior horn work or all-inside repair.

Medial compartment: medial meniscus posterior horn and body; MCL can be tight (pie-crusting or figure-4 position for access). Saphenous nerve/vein at risk with inside-out or outside-in sutures medially.

Lateral compartment: lateral meniscus posterior horn near popliteal hiatus; common peroneal nerve and popliteal neurovascular structures at risk with lateral or posterior work. Popliteus tendon relationship important.
Meniscocapsular junction and red-red zone: good blood supply; ideal for repair (all-inside, inside-out, outside-in). White-white zone has poor healing; often partial meniscectomy.

Posterior horn/root anatomy: lateral root near the posterior aspect of the tibial plateau (key arthroscopic landmark for posterior horn visualization and root repair); medial root near PCL. Root tears cause meniscal extrusion and rapid compartment wear; repair when possible.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Systematic diagnostic: patellofemoral, medial gutter/compartment (MCL, medial meniscus), intercondylar notch (for visualization reference), lateral compartment (lateral meniscus, popliteus). Probe meniscus for tear type and zone. Notch used as landmark for posterior work orientation.

Meniscus work planes: medial - work in meniscocapsular plane; protect saphenous with retractors or needle localization for inside-out. Lateral - respect popliteal hiatus; peroneal nerve is posterior and lateral; use all-inside devices carefully near posterior horn.

Repair techniques and closure: all-inside (fast but device cost/neurovascular risk), inside-out (gold standard for posterior horn, requires safety incision), outside-in (for anterior body). After repair, cycle and check stability; close portals. For select posterior horn/root, mini-open parapatellar or posterior approach may be used.

structures_at_risk.structure: Saphenous nerve and vein (medial meniscus repair)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The saphenous nerve (infrapatellar branch and sartorial branch) and accompanying vein lie in the posteromedial subcutaneous tissue and capsule; at risk during inside-out suture passage or posteromedial portal/accessory incision for medial meniscus posterior horn repair.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use a posteromedial safety incision or retractors for inside-out sutures; identify the nerve under the skin before needle passage. For all-inside, stay anterior to the capsule and avoid deep penetration posteriorly.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness on the medial leg/foot or painful neuroma. Saphenous neuralgia is a known complication of medial meniscus repair and can be more symptomatic than the original tear.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial meniscus repair (inside-out or posteromedial access)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies posterior and lateral to the fibular head and lateral capsule; at risk with lateral meniscus posterior horn work, all-inside device penetration too far posterior, or posterolateral portal/accessory incision.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Stay anterior in the lateral compartment; use all-inside devices with depth stops and direct visualization. For inside-out, use a posterolateral safety incision and identify the nerve. Avoid figure-4 position if it brings the nerve into the field.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop, sensory loss on dorsum of foot, or chronic pain. Peroneal injury is devastating and often requires bracing or tendon transfer if complete.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral meniscus repair (all-inside or posterolateral access)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The popliteal artery and tibial nerve lie directly posterior to the capsule and posterior horn of both menisci; at risk with aggressive all-inside penetration or posterior horn resection too far back.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use controlled all-inside device depth, direct visualization, and avoid instruments in the posterior recess without protection. For medial, pie-crust the MCL rather than forcing; for lateral, respect the popliteal hiatus.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Vascular injury (pseudoaneurysm or acute ischemia) or tibial nerve deficit. Requires immediate vascular repair; can lead to amputation in worst cases.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior horn meniscus repair or meniscectomy (all-inside or open)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Tight medial compartment limits visualization and instrument access to the medial meniscus posterior horn; over-stressing or pie-crusting can injure the MCL.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use figure-4 position or valgus stress carefully; consider limited MCL pie-crusting (needles or radiofrequency) only as needed for access. Avoid excessive force.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: MCL sprain or tear leading to medial laxity, pain, or delayed rehab. Usually heals but can prolong recovery or require bracing.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial meniscus repair in tight compartment

key_landmarks: Joint line (palpable for portal placement and meniscus zone reference).
Tibial tubercle (reference for anterolateral portal and patellar tendon).
Femoral condyles (for compartment access and valgus/varus stress during diagnostic).
Popliteal hiatus (lateral posterior landmark for lateral meniscus posterior horn work).
Posteromedial/posterolateral corner (for safety incisions in inside-out repairs).
common_mistakes: Failure to identify and protect the saphenous nerve during medial inside-out repair or posteromedial portal, leading to numbness or neuroma on the medial leg.
Over-penetration with all-inside devices on the lateral posterior horn, injuring the common peroneal nerve or popliteal bundle and causing foot drop or vascular catastrophe.
Missing or failing to repair a root tear or ramp lesion, resulting in meniscal extrusion, loss of hoop stress, and rapid lateral or medial compartment wear.
Inadequate zone assessment (repairing white-white or resecting repairable red-red), leading to poor healing or unnecessary loss of meniscal tissue.
Aggressive MCL pie-crusting or valgus stress in a tight medial compartment without protection, causing iatrogenic MCL injury and medial instability.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the meniscal zones (red-red, red-white, white-white) and how do they influence repair vs meniscectomy decisions? A: Red-red (peripheral, good blood supply) heals well with repair. Red-white (middle) has intermediate healing. White-white (inner) has poor healing and is often partially resected. Root and ramp lesions are repaired when possible regardless of zone to prevent extrusion.

Q: Which nerve/vein is at risk during medial meniscus repair and how is it protected in inside-out technique? A: Saphenous nerve (infrapatellar and sartorial branches) and vein in the posteromedial subcutaneous tissue. Use a posteromedial safety incision, identify the nerve, and retract it during suture passage. For all-inside, control device depth.

Q: How is the common peroneal nerve protected during lateral meniscus posterior horn repair? A: The nerve lies posterior and lateral to the fibular head. Stay anterior in the compartment, use all-inside devices with depth stops under visualization, and use a posterolateral safety incision with nerve identification for inside-out sutures. Avoid excessive lateral portal placement.

Q: What is the relationship of the popliteal neurovascular bundle to the posterior horns and why is it a danger zone in meniscus work? A: The popliteal artery and tibial nerve lie directly behind the capsule and posterior horns. Aggressive all-inside penetration or posterior resection can cause vascular injury or nerve deficit. Use controlled depth, direct vision, and protect with retractors or position (knee flexion).

Q: Why are root tears and ramp lesions important to identify and repair in meniscus surgery? A: Root tears cause meniscal extrusion and rapid compartment degeneration (equivalent to total meniscectomy). Ramp lesions (posteromedial meniscocapsular) affect rotational stability and are often missed. Repair restores hoop stress and joint preservation when technically feasible.
night_before_review_checklist: Review linked modules and source URLs
Memorize key SAR and landmarks
Practice the 10+ pimp questions
Walk through the night-before checklist
Confirm any case-specific fluoroscopy or approach details
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique or attending-specific preferences
Always verify with latest imaging and team briefing

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anterolateral viewing portal just lateral to patellar tendon at joint line. Anteromedial working portal under direct vision. Accessory posteromedial or posterolateral portals for posterior horn access or suture retrieval. Protect fat pad and neurovascular branches during portal creation.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Portal placement determines access to meniscus zones. Poor portals lead to iatrogenic cartilage damage or inability to repair posterior tears.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Intra-articular compartment / meniscocapsular plane (medial and lateral)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After portal entry, use 30° scope to visualize medial compartment (MCL, medial meniscus body/posterior horn) and lateral compartment (lateral meniscus, popliteus tendon, popliteal hiatus). Probe tear type, location (red-red vs white-white), and stability. Work in the meniscocapsular plane for repair.
key_structures: Lateral meniscus (posterior horn, root, popliteal hiatus)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working plane for all meniscus repair techniques. Zone identification (red-red good healing) guides repair vs resection. Neurovascular proximity is highest posteriorly.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Posterior horn / root access and repair planes (all-inside, inside-out, outside-in)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: For posterior horn tears: all-inside devices from anterior portals (depth control critical); inside-out requires safety incision (posteromedial for medial, posterolateral for lateral) with nerve protection; outside-in for anterior body or select posterior. Root repairs use transtibial or all-inside techniques. Protect popliteal structures at all times.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Posterior and root tears have the highest neurovascular risk and the greatest impact on joint preservation if repaired. Technique choice balances access, healing, and safety.

validation_warnings: Meniscus repair is performed primarily via arthroscopic portals and intra-articular compartments rather than traditional open surgical layers. The three entries below describe portal placement, meniscocapsular working planes, and neurovascular danger zones for resident preparation. Open or mini-open parapatellar access is reserved for select posterior horn or root repairs. Source material previously contained dev paths, stringified SAR, and cross-contamination; cleaned to public OB URLs and rich 6-field content only.
danger_zones: Saphenous nerve/vein (medial): identify in posteromedial safety incision or control all-inside depth; injury causes medial leg numbness/neuroma.
Common peroneal nerve (lateral): posterior/lateral to fibular head; protect with depth stops and visualization; injury causes foot drop.
Popliteal neurovascular bundle (posterior horns): direct posterior to capsule; controlled technique only; injury causes ischemia or tibial nerve deficit.
MCL (medial access): careful valgus or pie-crusting; over-stress causes medial laxity and prolonged recovery.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for meniscus repair, performed primarily via arthroscopic portals and intra-articular compartments (anterolateral viewing, anteromedial working, accessory posterior portals). Open or mini-open parapatellar or posterior access is reserved for select posterior horn or root repairs. Neurovascular danger zones (saphenous medially, peroneal/popliteal laterally) are emphasized. Content is meniscus-specific; ACL/TKA/hip contamination has been removed except for relevant meniscus-root or footprint relationships.

procedure_name: Monteggia Fracture ORIF (Ulna + Radial Head Dislocation)
procedure_overview: Monteggia fracture-dislocation: ulnar shaft fracture + radial head dislocation (Bado classification; Type I anterior most common). Key principle: anatomic reduction and fixation of the ulna (length and alignment) usually reduces the radial head spontaneously. If radial head remains dislocated or unstable after ulna fixation, open reduction via Kocher lateral interval may be needed. Radius component (if both-bone pattern) uses anterior Henry approach. PIN (posterior interosseous nerve) is the major motor nerve at risk in both Henry (supinator) and Kocher (crosses arcade of Frohse). Superficial radial nerve sensory branch at risk distally in Henry. Landmarks: bicipital tuberosity (biceps insertion), radial styloid, lateral epicondyle.
must_know_anatomy: Monteggia = ulnar fracture + radial head dislocation. Bado Type I (anterior radial head dislocation) is most common in adults and children. Anatomic ulnar reduction (length, rotation, alignment) is the key step that usually reduces the radial head without direct radial head surgery.

Anterior Henry approach for radius (when radius is fractured or for exposure): superficial interval between brachioradialis (radial n.) and FCR/PT (median n.). Cephalic vein and superficial radial nerve (sensory) are at risk in the distal interval. Deep: pronate the forearm to move the PIN laterally/posteriorly in supinator before incising the supinator mid-belly for plate placement.

Ulna approach: direct subcutaneous along the posterior ulnar border between ECU (posterior) and FCU (anterior) or just through the subcutaneous fascia. Minimal deep dissection; the ulna is subcutaneous throughout most of its length. Plate on the posterior or lateral surface depending on fracture and soft tissue.

If radial head requires open reduction (irreducible or unstable after ulna fixation): Kocher interval (anconeus posterior, ECU anterior). The PIN exits the supinator ~1-2 finger breadths distal to the radial head and crosses from anterior to posterior; pronation and careful retraction protect it. Avoid the LCL complex (anterior to the interval) to prevent posterolateral rotatory instability.

Bicipital tuberosity (radial) and biceps tendon insertion: key landmark for proximal radius exposure and for understanding the deforming force (biceps supinates and flexes the proximal radius fragment). Lateral epicondyle and radial styloid are palpable references for reduction and pin/plate starting points.

PIN (posterior interosseous nerve = deep branch of radial n.): motor to extensor compartment (finger/thumb extensors, ECU, supinator). At risk in Henry within supinator (arcade of Frohse) and in Kocher as it emerges distal to radial head. Injury causes loss of finger and thumb extension (wrist extension preserved via ECRL which is innervated more proximally).

structures_at_risk.structure: Posterior interosseous nerve (PIN, deep radial nerve branch in supinator)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In Henry volar radius approach the PIN lies within the supinator; incising the supinator without pronation or identification risks direct injury. In Kocher lateral for radial head, the PIN emerges from supinator ~1-2 finger breadths distal to the radial head and can be compressed by retractors or dissection.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: For Henry: fully pronate the forearm to translate the PIN posteriorly/laterally, then incise supinator in its mid-belly over the plate position. For Kocher: limit distal dissection, use careful retraction, identify the nerve if exposure extends. Document finger/thumb extension pre- and post-op.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of finger and thumb metacarpophalangeal extension (finger drop), loss of thumb radial abduction/extension, weak wrist extension with radial deviation (ECRL spared). Major functional deficit for grip and release.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterior Henry (volar radius) for radius component; Kocher lateral if radial head open reduction required

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In the distal Henry approach the superficial radial nerve emerges from under brachioradialis and crosses the interval toward the radial styloid; it is vulnerable during skin incision, subcutaneous dissection, or radial styloid exposure.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the nerve under the brachioradialis fascia in the distal forearm before extending the incision too far distally. Protect with a vessel loop or gentle retraction; do not blindly clamp or cauterize in the subcutaneous plane near the radial styloid.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness or painful neuroma on the dorsal radial hand (first web space, dorsal thumb/index/middle proximal phalanges). Can be more symptomatic than motor loss for some patients.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Distal anterior Henry approach to the radius

structures_at_risk.structure: Radial head and lateral collateral ligament complex (for stability after reduction)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The radial head must be reduced and stable after ulnar fixation. If open reduction via Kocher is performed, the LCL (lateral ulnar collateral ligament) lies anterior to the Kocher interval; violating it causes posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) of the elbow.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Reduce and fix the ulna first (length/alignment/rotation) before deciding on radial head surgery. If Kocher is needed, stay posterior to the LCL (anconeus-ECU interval) and do not release the LCL origin from the lateral epicondyle. Confirm radial head concentricity under fluoro after ulna fixation.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Recurrent radial head subluxation/dislocation, elbow instability, pain, and limited motion. PLRI from LCL injury is a devastating complication of lateral elbow approaches.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Monteggia (ulna fixation + conditional radial head open reduction via Kocher)

structures_at_risk.structure: Biceps tendon insertion and bicipital tuberosity (proximal radius)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The biceps inserts on the bicipital tuberosity (posteromedial radius); in proximal radius or Monteggia patterns the deforming force (biceps) flexes and supinates the proximal fragment. Exposure or plating near the tuberosity risks tendon injury or heterotopic bone.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use the bicipital tuberosity as a landmark for fragment control and rotation reference. Protect the biceps tendon during proximal radius exposure; avoid stripping its insertion when placing proximal screws or plates.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of supination strength or elbow flexion strength; heterotopic ossification or synostosis risk if the tendon is violated or the space is stripped.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Proximal radius exposure (Henry or extended) in Monteggia or both-bone patterns

key_landmarks: Lateral epicondyle (Kocher interval reference and radial head location)
Bicipital tuberosity (biceps insertion on proximal radius - rotation and deforming force landmark)
Radial styloid (distal radius reference; superficial radial nerve crosses nearby)
Olecranon and ulnar subcutaneous border (ulna approach and length reference)
Radial head (palpable laterally when dislocated; confirm concentricity after ulna fixation on fluoro)
key_intervals: Henry volar (anterior radius): between brachioradialis (radial n.) laterally and FCR/pronator teres (median n.) medially. Superficial radial n. and cephalic vein in distal subcutaneous; PIN in supinator deep (pronation protects).

Ulna direct/subcutaneous: along posterior ulnar border. Interval between ECU (posterior) and FCU (anterior) or simply through subcutaneous tissue and periosteum. Minimal deep neurovascular structures.

Kocher lateral (radial head when needed): between anconeus (radial n.) posteriorly and ECU (PIN) anteriorly. Protects the LCL if dissection stays posterior; PIN emerges distal to radial head in supinator.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: Monteggia principle: anatomic ulnar reduction and stable fixation (length, alignment, rotation) reduces the radial head in the majority of cases. Check radial head stability and concentricity on fluoro (AP, lateral, oblique) after ulna plating before closing or proceeding to open radial head reduction.

Bado Type I (anterior): most common. Ulna fracture usually apex anterior/proximal; radial head dislocates anteriorly. Reduce ulna first (correct the apex anterior deformity) to allow radial head to reduce.

If radial head irreducible or unstable after ulna fixation: limited Kocher approach, clear interposed tissue (capsule, annular ligament), reduce head, repair annular ligament or stabilize with transcapitellar pin (temporary) if needed. Avoid LCL injury.
common_mistakes: Focusing on the radial head before achieving anatomic ulnar length and alignment (radial head will often not stay reduced)
Injury to PIN in supinator during Henry approach (failure to pronate or incise too anteriorly in supinator)
Superficial radial nerve injury in distal Henry (dorsal radial hand numbness/neuroma)
Violating the LCL complex during Kocher approach for radial head (resulting in PLRI)
Missing associated injuries (olecranon fracture, radial head fracture, Essex-Lopresti variant with DRUJ injury)
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP and lateral elbow/forearm after ulna reduction and provisional fixation: confirm radial head is concentric in the capitellum on all views (no persistent subluxation).
True lateral of elbow: radial head should be centered on the capitellum; ulna length and bow restored.
Final plates/screws: confirm no intra-articular penetration at elbow or wrist, proper ulnar rotation (bicipital tuberosity orientation), and radial head stability through full pronation/supination.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: In Monteggia fracture-dislocation, what is the most important first step to reduce the radial head, and why does ulnar fixation usually suffice? A: Achieve anatomic length, alignment, and rotation of the ulna. The annular ligament and capsule are usually intact enough that restoring ulnar length and the proximal radioulnar relationship reduces the radial head without direct surgery on the radius or elbow.

Q: During the anterior Henry approach for a radius component in Monteggia or both-bone forearm fracture, what maneuver protects the PIN within the supinator, and what does PIN injury cause? A: Fully pronate the forearm to translate the PIN posteriorly and laterally before incising the supinator mid-belly. PIN injury causes loss of finger and thumb MP extension (finger drop) with preserved wrist extension (ECRL spared).

Q: If open reduction of the radial head is required after ulnar fixation in a Monteggia, which interval is used and what structure must be protected to avoid posterolateral rotatory instability? A: Kocher interval (anconeus posterior, ECU anterior). Stay posterior to the LCL complex (lateral ulnar collateral ligament); violating the LCL origin from the lateral epicondyle causes PLRI.

Q: Which sensory nerve is at risk in the distal Henry approach near the radial styloid, and what is the clinical consequence of injury? A: Superficial radial nerve (sensory). Injury causes numbness or painful neuroma on the dorsal radial hand (first web, dorsal thumb/index/middle). Often more symptomatic for patients than motor loss.

Q: What are the key palpable/fluoro landmarks for Monteggia ORIF and how do they guide reduction and nerve protection? A: Lateral epicondyle (radial head location, Kocher reference), bicipital tuberosity (biceps insertion, proximal radius rotation control), radial styloid (distal radius and superficial radial n. reference). Use them to judge ulnar length/rotation and safe intervals.
night_before_review_checklist: Review OB Monteggia (1024), both-bone forearm (1023/1025), and Henry volar approach (12010) pages for Bado, ulna-first principle, and PIN protection
Memorize: reduce/fix ulna first (length/alignment/rotation) before addressing radial head; pronate for Henry supinator work to protect PIN
Know Kocher interval (anconeus-ECU) only if radial head needs open reduction; protect LCL to avoid PLRI
Document PIN motor (finger/thumb extension) and superficial radial sensation pre/post-op
Practice the 5 pimp questions with ulna-first principle, pronation maneuver, and specific nerve consequences
Confirm associated injuries to rule out (radial head fracture, DRUJ/Essex-Lopresti, olecranon extension)
limitations: Anatomy and exposure focused case prep for Monteggia fracture-dislocation ORIF; does not replace full trauma classification (Bado), decision-making for radial head arthroplasty vs ORIF vs excision, or rehab protocols

Radial head open reduction via Kocher is not routine; most Monteggia radial heads reduce with ulnar fixation alone. Always attempt closed radial head reduction after ulna fixation under fluoro before opening the elbow
PIN and superficial radial nerve protection details are approach-specific (Henry vs Kocher); document motor and sensory function of the radial nerve branches pre- and post-operatively
approach_name: Monteggia: ulna direct subcutaneous + conditional Henry volar radius or Kocher lateral radial head

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: The ulna is subcutaneous along its posterior border from olecranon to ulnar styloid. Incision along the subcutaneous ridge; develop the interval between ECU (posterior) and FCU (anterior) or simply elevate subcutaneous tissue and periosteum. Minimal deep neurovascular risk. Plate can be placed on the posterior or lateral surface depending on fracture pattern and soft-tissue envelope.
structures_at_risk: Minimal major nerves/vessels; dorsal cutaneous branch of ulnar nerve distally if incision extends
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Primary and most important approach in Monteggia. Anatomic ulnar fixation (length, bow, rotation) is what reduces the radial head. This is a trauma exposure; not a soft-tissue or arthroplasty approach.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Anterior Henry (volar radius) for radius component or exposure
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Superficial interval: brachioradialis (radial n.) laterally and FCR/PT (median) medially. Cephalic vein and superficial radial nerve in the distal subcutaneous plane. Deep: pronate the forearm to move the PIN posteriorly in the supinator, then incise the supinator mid-belly over the planned plate. Used when the radius is also fractured or for proximal radius control.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The main anterior forearm approach for radius in both-bone or Monteggia patterns with radius involvement. Pronation is the key maneuver for PIN safety. Distinct from dorsal Thompson approach.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Kocher lateral (radial head open reduction - only if needed after ulna fixation)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Interval between anconeus (radial n. innervation) posteriorly and ECU (PIN) anteriorly. The radial head is exposed after splitting or elevating the interval. The LCL complex lies anterior to this interval; stay posterior to it. The PIN emerges from supinator just distal to the radial head and must be protected with limited distal dissection and careful retraction.
key_structures: LCL complex (anterior to the interval - protect)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Used selectively when the radial head will not reduce or is unstable after anatomic ulnar fixation. Not the default approach. Protecting the LCL is as important as protecting the PIN in this interval.

danger_zones: PIN in supinator (Henry) or emerging distal to radial head (Kocher) - pronate for Henry, limit distal dissection and use careful retraction for Kocher; injury = finger/thumb drop
Superficial radial nerve in distal Henry near radial styloid - identify under brachioradialis; injury = dorsal radial hand numbness/neuroma
LCL complex anterior to Kocher interval - stay posterior in anconeus-ECU plane; injury = posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI)
Failure to restore ulnar length/alignment first - radial head will not reduce or remain stable; leads to chronic dislocation and elbow dysfunction
approach_specific_notes: Monteggia is a trauma fracture-dislocation exposure. The ulna is addressed via direct subcutaneous posterior approach (primary). The radius (if involved) uses anterior Henry. Radial head open reduction uses Kocher lateral only if the head remains irreducible or unstable after ulnar fixation. This is distinct from elective tendon releases, arthroscopy, or arthroplasty approaches around the elbow/forearm. PIN protection (pronation in Henry, limited distal in Kocher) and LCL protection (Kocher) are the two highest-yield pearls. Do not mix with TKA, distal radius, or other unrelated anatomy.

procedure_overview: Open reduction and internal fixation of pelvis ring and acetabular fractures using approach-specific exposures based on fracture pattern (anterior vs posterior column involvement). Key anatomic concept: acetabulum as inverted Y of anterior and posterior columns joined by sciatic buttress. Approaches must be chosen and executed with precise knowledge of the neurovascular structures at risk in each window (ilioinguinal, Stoppa/anterior intrapelvic, Kocher-Langenbeck posterior, percutaneous).
must_know_anatomy: Acetabulum supported by anterior column (anterior ilium, anterior wall/dome, iliopectineal eminence, superior pubic ramus) and posterior column (quadrilateral surface, posterior wall/dome, ischial tuberosity, greater/lesser sciatic notches) forming an inverted Y connected to the sacrum via the sciatic buttress. Fracture classification and approach selection are driven by column and wall involvement.

Corona mortis: anomalous anastomosis between external iliac (epigastric) and obturator (internal iliac) vessels; present in 10-15% of patients, crosses the superior pubic ramus ~3-5 cm from the pubic symphysis. At high risk during lateral dissection over the ramus in any anterior approach (ilioinguinal or Stoppa). Must be identified and ligated.

Kocher-Langenbeck (posterior approach): for posterior wall/column, most transverse, and T-shaped fractures. Patient lateral or prone. Incision posterior to GT, split gluteus maximus, identify and release short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus) while protecting sciatic nerve. Sciatic nerve protection: initial location along quadratus femoris, extend hip/flex knee, gentle retraction with obturator internus, release short ERs from femur.

Ilioinguinal (anterior approach): exposure of internal iliac fossa, pelvic brim, quadrilateral surface, and rami through three windows (lateral, middle, medial). Risks: femoral nerve (compression from medial retraction or retractor), femoral vessels (thrombosis or injury), LFCN (in lateral window), corona mortis (ligate in middle window), obturator nerve/vessels (medial window). Protect femoral sheath with a retractor or finger.

Stoppa / anterior intrapelvic (modified Stoppa): anterior access to quadrilateral surface and posterior column through a Pfannenstiel or transverse incision, developing the retropubic space and lateral pelvic brim. Excellent for anterior column + posterior hemitransverse or certain T-shaped. Still requires corona mortis ligation and femoral/obturator protection; less LFCN risk than classic ilioinguinal lateral window.

Percutaneous screw fixation (anterior or posterior column, supra-acetabular, iliosacral): requires perfect understanding of column corridors and fluoroscopic landmarks (obturator-oblique, iliac-oblique, inlet/outlet, lateral sacral views). Risks: L5 nerve root (anterior to sacral ala), superior gluteal neurovascular bundle (supra-acetabular), femoral head penetration, intra-articular hardware.

structures_at_risk.structure: Corona mortis (anastomosis of external iliac/epigastric and obturator vessels)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the superior pubic ramus (often 3-5 cm lateral to symphysis) in 10-15% of patients; encountered during lateral or middle window dissection in ilioinguinal or Stoppa approaches, or during percutaneous anterior column screw placement.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: In anterior approaches, deliberately dissect the superior ramus and identify/ligate the vessel before it retracts. In percutaneous work, use inlet/obturator-oblique views to avoid the ramus corridor if anomalous vessel suspected on pre-op CT angio.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Profuse bleeding that is difficult to control once retracted into the obturator foramen or pelvis; can lead to hemodynamic instability or require embolization/laparotomy.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Any anterior pelvic approach (ilioinguinal, Stoppa) or anterior column percutaneous screws

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies on quadratus femoris posterior to the hip; at risk during Kocher-Langenbeck exposure, retraction, or fracture reduction. Also vulnerable to posterior column screws or hardware.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Locate the nerve along quadratus femoris early. Extend hip and flex knee to relax the nerve. Release short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus) from the femur and use obturator internus as a cushion for gentle retraction. Avoid excessive traction or posterior retractor placement.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop, loss of plantarflexion/dorsiflexion, sensory loss below knee (2-10% permanent risk even with careful technique). Inferior gluteal artery injury adds bleeding and may compromise nerve perfusion.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Kocher-Langenbeck posterior approach for posterior wall/column or transverse/T-shaped acetabulum/pelvis fractures

structures_at_risk.structure: Femoral nerve, femoral vessels, and LFCN (anterior approaches)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Femoral nerve lies on iliopsoas and can be compressed by medial retractors or retractor blades in the ilioinguinal middle/lateral windows or Stoppa. Femoral vessels are in the femoral sheath (protect with retractor). LFCN is in the lateral window fascia (ilioinguinal).
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use a finger or broad retractor to protect the femoral sheath medially. Limit medial retraction time. Identify and protect LFCN in the lateral window fascia. In Stoppa, stay extraperitoneal and avoid aggressive medial femoral retraction.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Femoral n.: quadriceps weakness, knee buckling, sensory loss anterior thigh/medial leg. Femoral vessels: thrombosis, bleeding, or limb ischemia. LFCN: anterolateral thigh numbness (usually tolerable).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Ilioinguinal or Stoppa anterior approaches

structures_at_risk.structure: Obturator nerve and vessels (medial window / Stoppa)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Exit the pelvis through the obturator canal; at risk during medial window retraction or dissection in ilioinguinal/Stoppa, or with medial hardware (quadrangular plate, anterior column screws).
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the obturator bundle in the medial window and protect with a retractor. Avoid deep medial retractors or screws that penetrate the quadrilateral surface into the true pelvis.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Obturator nerve: adductor weakness, sensory loss medial thigh. Vascular injury: bleeding into the obturator foramen or true pelvis.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial window of ilioinguinal or Stoppa/anterior intrapelvic approaches

key_landmarks: ASIS and iliac crest (lateral window of ilioinguinal)
Pubic symphysis and superior ramus (corona mortis location, medial window)
Iliopubic eminence / pelvic brim (key for anterior column reduction and plate)
Quadrilateral surface (posterior column landmark visible from anterior or posterior)
Greater/lesser sciatic notches (posterior column boundaries, Kocher-Langenbeck reference)
Iliac crest and PSIS (for posterior or percutaneous iliosacral starting points)
key_intervals: Ilioinguinal: three windows (lateral: iliac fossa between iliopsoas and iliac crest; middle: between iliopsoas and femoral vessels; medial: between vessels and symphysis/rectus). LFCN in lateral, corona in middle, obturator/femoral in medial.

Stoppa/anterior intrapelvic: Pfannenstiel or low transverse, develop retropubic space, elevate iliopsoas from pelvic brim, access quadrilateral surface and posterior column from inside the true pelvis. Corona mortis still critical.
Kocher-Langenbeck: posterior to GT, split gluteus maximus, short ER interval (protect sciatic with obturator internus), access posterior wall/column and quadrilateral surface from outside.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: Anterior column plate (along pelvic brim/iliopectineal line) or quadrangular plate for anterior + posterior hemitransverse. Posterior column plate (Kocher-Langenbeck) or reconstruction plate along posterior column.

Percutaneous anterior column, posterior column, or iliosacral screws: require perfect fluoro (obturator-oblique for anterior column, iliac-oblique for posterior, inlet/outlet + lateral for SI). Safe corridors avoid L5 root (anterior to S1 ala) and superior gluteal bundle.
Reduction sequence often starts with the most displaced column or the one that will allow the other to key in (e.g., posterior column first in some T-shaped).
common_mistakes: Missing and retracting corona mortis during anterior ramus dissection (uncontrollable bleeding)
Excessive or prolonged medial retraction in ilioinguinal/Stoppa causing femoral nerve palsy or vessel thrombosis
Inadequate sciatic nerve protection (hip extension/knee flexion, obturator internus cushion, short ER release) during Kocher-Langenbeck (permanent foot drop)
Intra-articular or neurovascular hardware from percutaneous screws (L5 root, femoral head, superior gluteal)
Wrong approach selection (e.g., trying to fix a posterior wall through an anterior approach) leading to inadequate reduction or iatrogenic injury
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: Obturator-oblique and iliac-oblique (Judet) views to confirm anterior/posterior column reduction and no intra-articular hardware.
Inlet/outlet and lateral sacral for iliosacral or posterior column percutaneous screws (avoid L5 root anterior to ala, confirm safe corridor).
AP pelvis and Judet views after reduction and fixation to verify column alignment, joint congruity, and no protrusio or step-off.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: During an ilioinguinal or Stoppa approach for anterior acetabulum or pelvis ring, where and how is corona mortis encountered and why must it be identified and ligated (10-15% anomalous)? A: It crosses the superior pubic ramus ~3-5 cm lateral to the symphysis. Once cut and retracted into the obturator foramen it is very difficult to control and can cause major bleeding; ligate early under direct vision.

Q: In Kocher-Langenbeck (posterior) for pelvis/acetabulum, what maneuvers protect the sciatic nerve (initial location, hip/knee position, short external rotator release)? A: Nerve lies on quadratus femoris. Extend the hip and flex the knee to slack the nerve. Identify along quadratus, release short ERs (piriformis, obturator internus) from the femur, and use obturator internus as a cushion for gentle retraction.

Q: What are the anterior vs posterior column boundaries of the acetabulum (inverted Y) and how does this guide approach selection for fracture patterns? A: Anterior column: anterior ilium to superior ramus (iliopectineal line). Posterior column: quadrilateral surface to ischium (posterior wall to sciatic notch). Anterior patterns (anterior wall/column, anterior + posterior hemitransverse) favor ilioinguinal/Stoppa; posterior wall/column/transverse/T-shaped favor Kocher-Langenbeck.

Q: Which neurovascular structures are at highest risk with medial retraction or retractor placement in the anterior (ilioinguinal/Stoppa) approach, and how is the femoral sheath protected? A: Femoral nerve (on iliopsoas, compression → quad weakness), femoral vessels (thrombosis or laceration in sheath), obturator bundle (medial window). Protect the femoral sheath with a broad retractor or finger; limit retraction time and force.

Q: Name the key palpable landmarks for assessing posterior column reduction in the quadrilateral surface during pelvis/acetabulum ORIF. A: Quadrilateral surface itself (palpated from anterior or posterior), greater/lesser sciatic notches (posterior column boundaries), ischial tuberosity/spine, and the posterior wall step-off or rotation relative to the intact ilium.
night_before_review_checklist: Review OB pelvis (1034) and acetabular fractures pages plus the anatomy_pelvis_ring_fracture_orif module for column anatomy, corona mortis, and approach-specific risks/protection
Memorize the three anterior windows (ilioinguinal) vs Stoppa vs Kocher-Langenbeck; know which fracture patterns each is for
Know corona mortis location (superior ramus) and mandatory ligation; sciatic protection maneuvers (hip ext/knee flex + obturator internus); femoral sheath protection
Review pimp questions on column boundaries, approach selection, and specific nerve/vessel avoidance per window
Confirm the exact fracture pattern (CT + Judet views) and planned approach(es) so you can focus on the relevant danger zones and landmarks the night before
Be ready to discuss percutaneous options and fluoro views for safe screw corridors (L5 root, superior gluteal, femoral head)
limitations: Anatomy and approach-selection focused case prep for pelvis/acetabular ORIF; does not replace full fracture classification (Letournel/Judet), decision-making for nonoperative vs ORIF vs percutaneous, or full surgical technique (reduction maneuvers, implant choice)
Approach choice and sequence are highly patient- and fracture-specific; always correlate pre-op imaging with the exact columns/walls involved and discuss with the attending
approach_name: Approach-specific (ilioinguinal, Stoppa, Kocher-Langenbeck, percutaneous) for pelvis ring/acetabular ORIF

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Lateral window: iliac crest to ASIS, elevate iliopsoas, expose iliac fossa (LFCN at risk in fascia). Middle window: between iliopsoas and femoral vessels (corona mortis on ramus). Medial window: between vessels and symphysis (obturator n/v, protect femoral sheath).
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Classic anterior exposure for anterior column, anterior wall, and certain anterior+posterior hemitransverse patterns. Requires meticulous window-by-window protection.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Low Pfannenstiel or transverse incision, develop retropubic space extraperitoneally. Elevate iliopsoas from pelvic brim for quadrilateral surface and posterior column access from inside. Corona mortis still ligated on ramus; femoral and obturator protection required but LFCN risk lower than ilioinguinal lateral window.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Excellent anterior access to quadrilateral surface and posterior column for anterior column + posterior hemitransverse or selected T-shaped without a full lateral ilioinguinal window.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision posterior to GT (lateral or prone position). Split gluteus maximus. Identify sciatic nerve on quadratus femoris. Release short ERs (piriformis, obturator internus) from femur and use obturator internus as retractor cushion. Expose posterior wall, column, and quadrilateral surface. Hip extended, knee flexed throughout.
key_structures: Sciatic nerve (on quadratus, protected by obturator internus)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Workhorse for posterior wall, posterior column, transverse, and T-shaped patterns. Sciatic protection is the defining technical step.

danger_zones: Corona mortis on superior pubic ramus (anterior approaches) - identify and ligate before retraction into obturator foramen; 10-15% anomalous, major bleeding risk
Sciatic nerve on quadratus femoris (Kocher-Langenbeck) - hip extended/knee flexed, release short ERs, use obturator internus as cushion; 2-10% injury risk even with care
Femoral nerve/vessels (ilioinguinal/Stoppa medial retraction) - protect sheath with retractor/finger, limit retraction time; risk of permanent quad weakness or thrombosis
L5 nerve root (anterior to sacral ala) and superior gluteal bundle (supra-acetabular) during percutaneous iliosacral or column screws - perfect fluoro (inlet/outlet/lateral, Judet) mandatory
approach_specific_notes: Pelvis/acetabular ORIF is strictly approach-specific. Ilioinguinal and Stoppa are anterior (different windows/risks: LFCN/femoral/corona/obturator). Kocher-Langenbeck is posterior (sciatic, short ERs, gluteus maximus split). Percutaneous is corridor-based (L5, superior gluteal, intra-articular). This payload separates the anatomy and risks by approach; do not mix posterior sciatic/ER content into anterior cases or vice versa. Shared column anatomy is referenced only to guide approach selection and safe corridors.

procedure_overview: Plantar Fasciitis Release (decompression_or_release). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Plantar fascia: thick aponeurosis with central (strongest, to 2-4 toes), medial (to hallux), and lateral bands. Central band most commonly involved in fasciitis.
Release: open or endoscopic. Incision medial or plantar. Release only medial 1/3 to 2/3 of central band to avoid lateral column instability and arch collapse.
Baxter nerve (first branch of lateral plantar nerve): at risk in medial/plantar approaches; compression or iatrogenic injury causes heel pain (Baxter neuritis) mimicking or coexisting with fasciitis.
Medial calcaneal nerve: sensory to heel; at risk in medial incisions. Injury causes painful heel numbness or neuroma.
Plantar fat pad: important for shock absorption under calcaneus; preserve during plantar approaches.
Medial/lateral plantar vessels/nerves: deeper; at risk with over-aggressive or misdirected release. Over-release leads to flatfoot or lateral column pain.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Medial or plantar incision (open) or endoscopic portals. For open: medial heel incision, identify central band, release medial 1/3-2/3 under direct vision while protecting fat pad and nerves. Endoscopic: portals for visualization and release with care for Baxter.
Key protection: identify and protect Baxter nerve (1st lat plantar branch) and medial calcaneal nerve. Limit release to avoid lateral band and arch destabilization. Preserve fat pad.
Post-release: confirm adequate but not excessive release. Close carefully. Post-op protected weightbearing, stretching, and monitor for lateral column or nerve issues.

structures_at_risk.structure: Baxter nerve (1st branch of lateral plantar nerve)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Runs between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae near medial calcaneal tuberosity; compressed in fasciitis or injured during medial/plantar release. Common cause of recalcitrant heel pain.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify during medial approach (between abductor hallucis and quadratus). Protect with retractor during release. Endoscopic requires careful portal placement and visualization.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Heel pain (Baxter neuritis), abductor hallucis weakness, or painful neuroma. Can be more symptomatic than original fasciitis.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial or endoscopic plantar fascia release

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Sensory branch to medial heel skin; at risk in medial heel incisions for open release.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect under skin in medial approach. Avoid blind dissection or cautery. Use careful incision placement.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Medial heel numbness or painful neuroma. Can cause chronic heel pain.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial open plantar fascia release

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral plantar nerve / vessels and fat pad
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Deeper structures; over-release or misdirection can injure lateral plantar or destabilize lateral column via fat pad or band disruption.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit release to medial 1/3-2/3 central band. Preserve fat pad and lateral band. Direct visualization for open; careful endoscopic technique.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Lateral column pain, flatfoot deformity, or vascular/nerve injury to lateral foot. Arch collapse or transfer metatarsalgia.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Plantar fascia release (open or endoscopic)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Shock-absorbing pad; thinned or damaged in plantar approaches, leading to painful heel or altered biomechanics.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Preserve fat pad during dissection and release. Avoid excessive plantar exposure or retraction that thins it.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Painful heel (fat pad atrophy), altered gait, or need for orthotics/padding.

common_mistakes: Over-release (full or lateral band) leading to lateral column pain, flatfoot, or arch collapse.
Missing or injuring Baxter nerve (1st lat plantar branch), causing persistent or new heel neuritis (often worse than fasciitis).
Injuring medial calcaneal nerve in medial incision (heel numbness or neuroma).

attending_pimp_questions: Q: What portion of the plantar fascia is typically released and why limit it? A: Medial 1/3 to 2/3 of the central band only. Full or lateral release destabilizes the lateral column and arch, causing pain, flatfoot, or transfer lesions.

Q: Which nerve is commonly involved in recalcitrant heel pain (Baxter neuritis) and how is it protected during release? A: First branch of lateral plantar (Baxter) nerve, between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae. Identify and protect during medial approach; injury or compression causes heel pain mimicking fasciitis.

Q: What sensory nerve is at risk in medial heel incision for open plantar release and consequence? A: Medial calcaneal nerve (sensory to heel skin). Injury causes numbness or painful neuroma on medial heel. Identify under skin and protect.

Q: Why preserve the plantar fat pad during release and what happens if damaged? A: It is the main shock absorber under the calcaneus. Damage causes painful heel atrophy, altered gait, and need for padding/orthotics.

Q: What are the risks of over-release or missing Baxter nerve in plantar fasciitis surgery? A: Lateral column pain, arch collapse, flatfoot, or persistent/new neuritis pain (often worse than original). Limited release + nerve protection + post-op care are essential.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to plantar fascia / abductor hallucis)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Medial heel incision (open) or endoscopic portals. Identify central band of plantar fascia. Protect medial calcaneal nerve under skin and Baxter nerve (between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae).
key_structures: Central plantar fascia band (medial 1/3-2/3 for release)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard approach for release. Nerve identification (Baxter especially) and limited release (medial 1/3-2/3) prevent complications like neuritis or lateral column collapse.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (fascia release and protection of deeper structures)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Release only medial 1/3 to 2/3 of central band under direct vision (open) or with endoscopic control. Preserve lateral band and fat pad. Protect lateral plantar nerve/vessels deeper. Confirm adequate but not excessive release.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The release layer. Limited, protected release is key to success; over-release or nerve injury causes worse problems than original fasciitis.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Close in layers if open. Protected weightbearing, stretching, night splint, and orthotics post-op. Monitor for lateral column pain, nerve symptoms, or arch collapse. Most improve with time; revision rare but for over-release or missed Baxter.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Closure and aftercare determine outcome. Limited release + protection + monitoring prevent transfer problems or missed neuritis.
