Lateral band / column: limit release to medial 1/3-2/3 central; over-release causes lateral pain, flatfoot, arch collapse.

procedure_name: Posterior Lumbar Decompression and Fusion (Midline Approach)
procedure_overview: Posterior midline approach for lumbar decompression (laminectomy, foraminotomy, discectomy), nerve root exploration, and instrumented fusion (pedicle screws, interbody or posterolateral). Key anatomy: spinous processes, lamina, facet/pars, ligamentum flavum, traversing and exiting nerve roots, dura/cauda equina, and pedicle screw starting points (intersection of transverse process and facet). Level identification with iliac crest (L4-5) and fluoro is mandatory. Risks are primarily neural (dura, nerve roots) and vascular (epidural veins).
must_know_anatomy: Posterior midline exposure: incision over the spinous processes at the target level(s). Iliac crest highest point approximates L4-5 interspace (useful landmark). Incise lumbodorsal fascia to the spinous process tip, preserve the interspinous ligament if possible. Detach paraspinal muscles (erector spinae / multifidus) subperiosteally from the spinous process and lamina out to the facet joint and transverse process.

Ligamentum flavum: attaches to the superior half of the undersurface of the lamina above and the superior edge of the lamina below. Resection begins at the midline or lateral edge; it is thinner in the midline and thicker laterally. Removal exposes the dura and traversing nerve root in the lateral recess.

Neural elements: the thecal sac (dura/cauda equina) lies in the central canal. Traversing nerve root (e.g., L5 root at L4-5) is medial in the lateral recess and exits under the pedicle of the level below. Exiting nerve root (L4 at L4-5) exits through the foramen above the disc. Decompression must address both central stenosis (lamina/flavum) and lateral recess/foraminal stenosis (facet, ligamentum, disc).

Facet and pars anatomy for decompression and fusion: the pars interarticularis is the isthmus between superior and inferior articular processes. Over-resection of the pars or facet during decompression can cause iatrogenic instability. For fusion, pedicle screws start at the intersection of the transverse process and the facet (or mammillary process); trajectory is ~15-20° medial (convergent) and 10-15° caudal on lateral fluoro.

Pedicle screw landmarks and medial breach risk: the pedicle is bounded medially by the thecal sac and traversing root. A medial breach (too medial starting point or trajectory) can injure the dura or nerve root. The safe starting point is the upslope of the transverse process at the facet junction; confirm with AP fluoro (screw should not cross the medial pedicle border on AP before entering the body).

Vascular and epidural anatomy: epidural veins are prominent in the lateral recess and foramen; bleeding can obscure the nerve root and dura. Bone wax, bipolar, and hemostatic agents are used. The anterior structures (great vessels, sympathetic chain) are not directly exposed but are at risk with anterior perforation by screws or aggressive disc work.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies immediately deep to the ligamentum flavum and lamina in the central canal. At risk during flavum resection, laminectomy, or medial pedicle screw placement (medial breach). Thin or adherent dura (prior surgery, severe stenosis) increases risk of tear.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Begin flavum resection laterally or in the midline where it is thinnest; use a Woodson or blunt elevator to develop the plane between flavum and dura. Protect the dura with a Penfield or cottonoid during bone removal. For pedicle screws, confirm starting point lateral enough and trajectory convergent but not too medial on AP fluoro.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: CSF leak (post-op headache, pseudomeningocele, infection risk), nerve root or cauda equina injury (bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, leg weakness). May require repair, lumbar drain, or reoperation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Midline posterior lumbar decompression (laminectomy/foraminotomy)

structures_at_risk.structure: Traversing and exiting nerve roots (lateral recess and foramen)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Traversing root (e.g., L5 at L4-5) is compressed in the lateral recess by flavum, facet, or disc and is exposed during decompression. Exiting root is at the foramen. Aggressive medial retraction, Kerrison bites, or disc fragment removal can injure the root or its sleeve.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the traversing root early (medial in the lateral recess) and protect it with a retractor or cottonoid. Decompress the lateral recess and foramen under direct vision. For pedicle screws, the exiting root is at risk with too-cranial starting point or trajectory.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Radiculopathy, motor deficit (e.g., foot drop for L5), or sensory loss in the root distribution. Can be temporary (retraction) or permanent (direct injury or cautery).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior decompression (lateral recess and foraminal work)

structures_at_risk.structure: Pedicle medial wall (and traversing root / dura with medial breach)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The medial pedicle wall is the boundary between the screw and the spinal canal. Starting too medial, trajectory too convergent, or small pedicle diameter leads to medial breach. The traversing root and dura are immediately medial to the pedicle.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use the standard starting point (transverse process-facet junction, upslope). Confirm with AP fluoro before advancing (screw tip should not cross the medial pedicle border on AP until it has entered the vertebral body on lateral). Use gearshift or probe to feel the four walls of the pedicle before tapping/screwing.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Medial breach can cause direct nerve root or dural injury (radiculopathy, CSF leak, or cauda equina syndrome). Even without direct contact, the screw can cause chronic irritation or require revision.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Pedicle screw instrumentation during posterior lumbar fusion

structures_at_risk.structure: Epidural veins and bone bleeding (lateral recess / foramen)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Prominent venous plexus in the lateral recess and foramen; bone bleeding from decorticated lamina, facets, and transverse processes. Can rapidly obscure visualization of the dura and nerve roots during decompression.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use bipolar cautery for veins, bone wax or hemostatic agents (Gelfoam, Surgiflo) for bone bleeding, and cottonoids for temporary tamponade. Maintain clear visualization before using Kerrison or pituitary rongeurs near neural structures.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Poor visualization leading to dural or nerve root injury; postoperative hematoma causing compression; increased operative time and blood loss.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior decompression and fusion (any level with significant stenosis or bleeding)

key_landmarks: Iliac crest (highest point ~ L4-5 interspace for level identification)
Spinous processes (midline, palpable; confirm level with fluoro or needle)
Facet joint and transverse process junction (pedicle screw starting point)
Lateral edge of lamina / pars interarticularis (lateral recess decompression landmark)
Ligamentum flavum attachment (halfway up undersurface of superior lamina)
key_intervals: Superficial: midline skin, subcutaneous fat, lumbodorsal fascia incised to spinous process. Preserve supraspinous/interspinous ligament if fusion will spare the segment.

Paraspinal muscle: subperiosteal elevation of erector spinae/multifidus from spinous process and lamina laterally to the facet and transverse process. Self-retaining retractors (Gelpi or Adson) maintain exposure.

Deep bony/ligamentous: remove spinous process (if needed), resect lamina with Leksell/Kerrison, then ligamentum flavum (lateral to medial or vice versa). Lateral recess and foraminal decompression under the facet and ligamentum. Pedicle screw placement at TP-facet junction with medial convergence.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: Pedicle screw trajectory: starting at the junction of transverse process and facet (or mammillary process), ~15-20° medial (convergent) on AP, 10-15° caudal on lateral. Confirm no medial breach on AP before the screw enters the body.

Decompression extent: central (laminectomy), lateral recess (undercut facet and flavum), foraminal (foraminotomy under the pedicle). Preserve as much facet and pars as possible for stability unless fusion is planned.

Fusion options: posterolateral (decorticate transverse processes and facets, lay graft), TLIF/PLIF (interbody cage via unilateral or bilateral facetectomy), or ALIF (anterior, separate approach). Pedicle screws provide segmental fixation.
common_mistakes: Wrong level surgery (failure to confirm with fluoro or iliac crest landmark before decompression)
Dural tear from aggressive flavum or lamina resection without developing the plane or protecting the dura
Medial pedicle screw breach (starting point too medial or trajectory too convergent) injuring traversing root or dura
Inadequate lateral recess or foraminal decompression (persistent radiculopathy from under-decompressed traversing or exiting root)
Over-resection of pars or facet without fusion leading to iatrogenic spondylolysis or instability
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP and lateral (or oblique) to confirm level (spinous process marker or needle) before incision or decompression.

AP view during pedicle screw placement: starting point at transverse process-facet junction; screw should not cross medial pedicle border on AP until it has entered the vertebral body on lateral view.
Final AP/lateral (and oblique if needed) to confirm screw position (no medial breach, appropriate depth, no anterior perforation), interbody cage position (if used), and alignment.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is the iliac crest landmark for lumbar level identification and how is it used in the posterior approach? A: The highest point of the iliac crest approximates the L4-5 interspace (or L4 spinous process). Use it (plus fluoro confirmation) to plan the incision and confirm the correct level before any bone or ligament removal.

Q: Describe the attachment of the ligamentum flavum and the safe technique for its resection during posterior lumbar decompression. A: Attaches to the superior half of the undersurface of the lamina above and the superior edge of the lamina below. Begin resection laterally or in the midline (thinnest), use a blunt elevator (Woodson) to develop the plane between flavum and dura before biting with Kerrison. Protect the dura throughout.

Q: Where is the traversing nerve root relative to the lateral recess and pedicle, and how is it protected during decompression and pedicle screw placement? A: The traversing root (e.g., L5 at L4-5) lies medial in the lateral recess under the ligamentum flavum and facet, then exits under the pedicle of the level below. Identify it early, protect with a retractor or cottonoid during flavum/facet removal. For screws, avoid medial breach (the root is immediately medial to the pedicle).

Q: What is the standard pedicle screw starting point and trajectory, and what fluoro view confirms no medial breach? A: Starting point is the intersection (upslope) of the transverse process and facet joint (mammillary process). Trajectory ~15-20° medial (convergent) and 10-15° caudal. On AP fluoro, the screw tip should not cross the medial pedicle border until it has entered the vertebral body on the lateral view.

Q: What are the key risks of medial pedicle screw breach or over-aggressive lateral recess decompression? A: Medial breach: direct injury to the traversing nerve root or dura (radiculopathy, CSF leak, cauda equina). Over-decompression: iatrogenic instability if too much pars or facet is removed without fusion. Both can cause new or persistent neurologic deficit.

approach_name: Posterior midline approach for lumbar decompression and fusion

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to lumbodorsal fascia and spinous process)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Midline incision over the spinous processes at the planned levels (confirm with iliac crest landmark ~L4-5 and fluoro). Divide subcutaneous fat. Incise the lumbodorsal fascia in the midline down to the spinous process tip. Preserve the supraspinous and interspinous ligaments if the segment will not be fused or if a motion-preserving option is planned.
structures_at_risk: Minimal major structures; superficial bleeding controlled with electrocautery
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Midline orientation and fascial incision set up the entire subperiosteal exposure. Wrong level at this stage leads to wrong-level surgery.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Paraspinal muscle elevation (to facet and transverse process)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Using Cobb or periosteal elevator, detach the erector spinae and multifidus subperiosteally from the spinous process and lamina, working laterally to the facet joint and base of the transverse process. Self-retaining retractors maintain exposure. The facet capsule is preserved unless fusion or decompression requires partial facetectomy.
structures_at_risk: Dorsal rami (innervate paraspinals; usually sacrificed with elevation)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Subperiosteal elevation provides the working corridor for laminectomy, flavectomy, and pedicle screw placement. Excessive bleeding or muscle trauma increases post-op pain and infection risk.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep bony and ligamentous decompression (flavum, lamina, lateral recess)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Remove spinous process (if needed). Resect lamina with Leksell rongeur or burr. Identify and resect ligamentum flavum (begin laterally or midline, develop plane with Woodson elevator between flavum and dura). Undercut the medial facet and lateral recess to decompress the traversing nerve root. Foraminotomy under the pedicle for the exiting root if needed. Protect dura and root throughout with cottonoids and retractors.
key_structures: Ligamentum flavum (halfway up undersurface of superior lamina)
Traversing nerve root (medial in lateral recess)
structures_at_risk: Traversing and exiting nerve roots (retraction or direct injury)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The decompression itself. Adequate lateral recess and foraminal work is required for radiculopathy relief; over-resection of pars/facet without fusion risks instability.

danger_zones: Dura / cauda equina immediately deep to ligamentum flavum and lamina - develop plane with blunt elevator, protect with cottonoid; tear causes CSF leak, headache, infection risk, possible root injury
Traversing nerve root in lateral recess (medial to pedicle) - identify early, protect during flavum and facet resection; injury causes radiculopathy or motor deficit (e.g., foot drop)

Medial pedicle wall during screw placement - starting point too medial or trajectory too convergent leads to breach and direct root/dura injury; confirm on AP fluoro (screw should not cross medial border until in body on lateral)
Epidural veins in lateral recess/foramen - control with bipolar and hemostatics before using sharp instruments near neural structures; poor visualization is a major cause of dural/nerve injury
approach_specific_notes: Posterior midline lumbar approach for decompression and fusion. All content is specific to the posterior elements (lamina, flavum, facets, pedicles) and the neural structures in the canal and lateral recess/foramen (traversing and exiting roots, dura). This is distinct from anterior or lateral lumbar approaches (great vessels, sympathetic chain, psoas, genitofemoral n.), cervical approaches, or thoracic. Pedicle screw medial breach and dural tear are the highest-yield preventable complications for the resident to understand. Level confirmation with fluoro/iliac crest is mandatory before any bone removal.
validation_warnings: Some module files containing posterior lumbar facts also contain cervical anterior approach content (mixed in the same JSONL records). Only the posterior lumbar-specific facts (midline, flavum, paraspinal elevation, dura, pedicle landmarks, iliac crest L4-5) were used for this payload. No cervical anatomy is included.

procedure_overview: Quadriceps Tendon Repair (soft_tissue_repair). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Quadriceps tendon: 2-4 distinct layers (anterior rectus femoris, middle vastus medialis + vastus lateralis, deep vastus intermedius). Inserts on superior patella pole with retinacular expansions.
Complete rupture: palpable gap, inability to actively extend knee against gravity or straight leg raise. Partial may have intact extension but weakness/pain. MRI/US for confirmation.
Superior pole of patella is the insertion; retinacula (medial/lateral) provide important extensions for repair and stability. Suprapatellar pouch communicates with joint.
Patellofemoral tracking: quad tendon repair affects extensor mechanism and patellar position/tracking on trochlea. Assess after repair.
Sensory: infrapatellar branch of saphenous may be at risk in anterior incisions; protect if encountered.
Repair: transpatellar tunnels or anchors/sutures through tendon layers + retinacula. Chronic may need V-Y or graft augmentation.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Anterior midline or slightly medial incision over the tendon gap and superior patella. Develop subcutaneous plane, protect any saphenous branches. Expose the ruptured tendon ends and superior patella pole.

Repair: freshen tendon ends. Use heavy non-absorbable suture in Krackow or similar through the 2-4 layers. Pass through transpatellar bone tunnels or use anchors/screws in superior patella. Repair retinacula for stability and tracking.
Post-repair: assess tracking on trochlea, tension, and stability. Close in layers. Protect repair post-op (brace, limited flexion initially).

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses anterior knee in subcutaneous or retinacular plane; at risk during anterior incision for quad tendon exposure/repair.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify under skin flap if visible; protect or retract medially/laterally. Avoid blind cautery in subcutaneous layer.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterolateral knee/ patellar numbness or painful neuroma. Tolerable but can be annoying post-op.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterior approach for quadriceps tendon repair

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Insertion site for quad tendon; retinacula provide lateral/medial stability. Poor repair leads to tracking issues or re-rupture.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Freshen bone/tendon interface. Secure multi-layer repair through bone tunnels or anchors. Repair retinacula anatomically for stability and patellar tracking.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Extensor lag, patellar maltracking/tilt, re-rupture, or need for revision/augmentation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Quad tendon insertion/repair at superior patella

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Quad tendon repair affects patellar position and extensor pull; imbalance or overtensioning leads to tilt, subluxation, or pain on trochlea.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Assess tracking intra-op after repair (through ROM). Avoid overtension; ensure balanced retinacular repair. Check for trochlear dysplasia or other factors.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Patellofemoral pain, instability, maltracking, or arthritis. May require revision or realignment procedures.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Extensor mechanism repair and patellar tracking assessment

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Communicates with knee joint; hematoma or infection risk if not managed. Repair must restore extensor continuity without joint contamination issues.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Control bleeding into pouch. Repair in layers. Post-op wound care and monitor for effusion/infection.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Hematoma, stiffness, or septic arthritis if contamination occurs.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterior quad repair near suprapatellar pouch

key_landmarks: Patella superior pole
Quad tendon
Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.

common_mistakes: Failure to identify/protect infrapatellar saphenous branch in anterior incision, causing annoying numbness or neuroma.
Inadequate multi-layer repair or retinacular repair leading to extensor lag, patellar maltracking, or re-rupture.
Overtensioning or poor tracking assessment after repair, causing patellofemoral pain or instability.
Missing chronic nature or retraction (needs V-Y/graft); early repair has better outcomes.
Ignoring suprapatellar pouch communication (hematoma or infection risk).
attending_pimp_questions: Q: Describe the layers of the quadriceps tendon and why identifying them matters for repair. A: 2-4 layers (ant rectus femoris, mid vastus med + lat, deep vastus int). Identifying allows anatomic multi-layer repair for strength and prevents gapping or weakness.

Q: What is the role of the medial and lateral retinacula in quadriceps tendon repair and patellar tracking? A: Provide important medial/lateral extensions for repair stability. Repairing them restores balanced pull and prevents tilt/subluxation on the trochlea.

Q: How do you assess and protect patellofemoral tracking after quad tendon repair? A: After repair, cycle knee through ROM and observe patellar tracking on trochlea. Repair retinacula anatomically; avoid overtension. Check for dysplasia or other factors if maltracking persists.

Q: What sensory nerve is at risk in anterior approach for quad tendon repair and consequence of injury? A: Infrapatellar branch of saphenous. Injury causes anterolateral knee/patellar numbness or neuroma (tolerable but annoying). Identify/protect under skin flap.

Q: What are options for chronic quadriceps tendon rupture with retraction? A: V-Y advancement, tendon graft augmentation, or transpatellar repair with reinforcement. Early repair is ideal; chronic has higher re-rupture and stiffness risk.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to quad tendon fascia / retinacula)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Midline or slightly medial anterior incision over the palpable gap and superior patella. Develop subcutaneous flaps, identify/protect infrapatellar saphenous branches if crossing. Expose the ruptured quad tendon and superior patella pole with retinacular extensions.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard anterior approach for quad tendon repair. Early sensory nerve protection and full exposure of layers + retinacula are key for multi-layer repair and tracking.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Identify the 2-4 tendon layers (ant RF, mid VM+VL, deep VI). Freshen ends. Repair with heavy suture (Krackow or similar) through layers. Secure to superior patella via transpatellar tunnels or anchors. Repair retinacula for lateral/medial stability and patellar tracking on trochlea.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The repair layer. Multi-layer anatomic repair + retinacula is essential for strength, tracking, and preventing re-rupture or maltracking.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After repair, assess patellar tracking through full ROM on trochlea. Close in layers (fascia if possible, subcutaneous, skin). Protect repair post-op (brace in extension or limited flexion, gradual ROM). Monitor for stiffness or tracking issues.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Closure layer confirms tracking and protects the repair. Early protected motion balances healing vs stiffness.

validation_warnings: Quadriceps tendon repair payload is anterior approach focused on layers, insertion, and tracking. Patellar tendon rupture is separate (inferior pole). Source material previously contained TKA/ACL/meniscus bleed, stringified/empty SAR, generic, and placeholders; cleaned to public 3023 facts and rich 6-field content only.
danger_zones: Infrapatellar saphenous branch: identify/protect in anterior subcutaneous; injury causes knee numbness/neuroma.
Superior patella pole + retinacula: secure multi-layer repair; poor repair causes lag, maltracking, or re-rupture.
Patellofemoral tracking on trochlea: assess after repair; imbalance causes pain/instability/arthritis.
Suprapatellar pouch: control bleeding/contamination; infection or stiffness risk if issues.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for quadriceps tendon repair (acute or chronic). Focus on 2-4 tendon layers, superior patella insertion, retinacular repair for tracking/stability, and sensory nerve protection. No patellar tendon rupture, TKA, ACL, or meniscus content as primary (comparative notes only if in validation). Content is extensor mechanism-specific at quad level.

procedure_overview: Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty (arthroscopy_or_endoscopy). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: RSA: deltopectoral or superolateral (lateral split) approaches. Relies on deltoid for elevation after reversing ball/socket.
Deltopectoral preferred for most RSA (exposure, extension). Lateral split for some. Subscapularis management varies by implant/design.
surgical_approach_anatomy: RSA: deltopectoral or superolateral (lateral split) approaches. Relies on deltoid for elevation after reversing ball/socket.

structures_at_risk.structure: Axillary nerve (on deep surface of deltoid and inferior to subscapularis)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies 5-7 cm below acromion on deltoid undersurface and crosses inferior to subscapularis. At risk during inferior glenoid/baseplate work or medial retraction in RSA.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify early at inferior subscap border. Keep arm abducted and externally rotated during inferior work. Limit inferior capsular release and use blunt retractors.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Deltoid paralysis (loss of abduction/elevation) and teres minor weakness. Devastating for RSA outcome since deltoid is the primary elevator.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral or superolateral for RSA (inferior glenoid exposure)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Enters conjoint tendon deep/medial 5-8 cm below coracoid. Excessive medial retraction in deltopectoral for RSA can injure it.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit medial retraction force and duration. Identify before any conjoint release. Know the distance from coracoid.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Weak elbow flexion (biceps/brachialis) and lateral forearm numbness (LABC).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral approach for RSA

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Management (repair vs. not, tenotomy vs. peel) varies by RSA design and affects anterior stability and internal rotation. Poor handling leads to instability or loss of IR.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Follow implant-specific subscap protocol. Tag and repair if indicated for the chosen RSA prosthesis. Avoid overtensioning.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterior instability or loss of active internal rotation after RSA.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: RSA via deltopectoral (subscap handling)

key_landmarks: Coracoid process: proximal landmark for deltopectoral incision to deltoid insertion.
Acromion: reference for superolateral deltoid split; limit split to 3-5 cm from acromion to protect axillary nerve.
Cephalic vein in deltopectoral groove (retract laterally with deltoid).
Conjoint tendon: musculocutaneous nerve enters deep/medial 5-8 cm below coracoid.
common_mistakes: Inadequate identification of structures at risk leading to iatrogenic injury
Missing key landmarks resulting in malpositioned hardware
Ignoring deforming forces causing loss of reduction
Poor fluoroscopic confirmation of starting point or trajectory
Incomplete release or exposure leading to inadequate decompression or fixation

Memorize key SAR and landmarks

Always verify with latest imaging and team briefing

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial deltopectoral or superolateral (skin to interval)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: For deltopectoral (preferred for most RSA): incision from coracoid to deltoid insertion. Cephalic vein in groove (retract laterally with deltoid). For superolateral: split deltoid 3-5 cm from acromion (limit to protect axillary).
structures_at_risk: Axillary nerve (deep deltoid 5-7 cm below acromion; limit split)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Deltopectoral gives extensile exposure and is standard for RSA. Superolateral is shorter but risks more axillary nerve issues if split too far.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Identify conjoint tendon (musculocutaneous n 5-8 cm below coracoid - limit medial retraction). Subscapularis tenotomy or peel (management varies for RSA stability). Anterior capsule and glenoid exposure. Deltoid is the primary elevator post-RSA (no rotator cuff reliance).
key_structures: Glenoid and humerus (for baseplate and stem)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: RSA specific: subscap handling affects stability; deltoid is now the key muscle. Axillary protection remains critical (inferior work).

surgical_layers.layer_name: Glenoid and humeral preparation (RSA specific exposure)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Glenoid exposure for baseplate (inferior tilt, version). Humeral preparation (stem version, height for deltoid tension). Trial reduction and stability testing with the reversed ball-and-socket. No subscap repair in some RSA designs.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The RSA-specific working layer. Deltoid tensioning is more important than anatomic reconstruction. Axillary nerve at highest risk during inferior glenoid exposure.

validation_warnings: Reverse shoulder arthroplasty payload is deltopectoral-primary for RSA. Anatomic TSA content is in the separate total_shoulder_arthroplasty payload. RSA relies on deltoid tensioning rather than anatomic reconstruction. Axillary nerve at highest risk during inferior glenoid work. Source material from 12000/12061; further RSA-specific pearls recommended.
approach_specific_notes: Strictly for reverse shoulder arthroplasty (RSA), primarily via deltopectoral (preferred for exposure and extensibility). Deltoid is the primary elevator (no rotator cuff reliance). Axillary nerve protection (5-7 cm deep deltoid) and musculocutaneous (5-8 cm on conjoint) remain critical. Subscapularis management is prosthesis-specific. No anatomic TSA or cuff repair content mixed. Lateral split is alternative but has higher axillary risk if extended.

procedure_overview: SCFE in situ percutaneous pinning (or open Modified Dunn for severe deformity). Key anatomy centers on the proximal femoral physis (weak point, posteroinferior slip), retinacular blood supply to the epiphysis (AVN risk), and precise fluoroscopically guided screw trajectory from anterior proximal femur across physis into epiphysis center. Must achieve 5+ threads across physis and confirm no joint penetration on true lateral view. High stakes in unstable slips; gentle handling and accurate pin placement protect the remaining blood supply.
must_know_anatomy: SCFE: epiphysis typically slips posteroinferior relative to metaphysis. Assessed by Klein line on AP (superior neck line should intersect epiphysis) and Southwick angle on lateral. Unstable growth plate from shear overload.

Primary femoral head blood supply via retinacular branches of the medial femoral circumflex artery; at highest risk for AVN in unstable slips, forceful manipulation, or iatrogenic pin injury. Capsule and physis also relevant.

Percutaneous in situ fixation (stable or unstable): single cannulated screw (or 2 for unstable) from anterior proximal femur (starting point on lateral view), trajectory perpendicular or slightly oblique to physis, center-center on AP and true lateral fluoro. Avoid anterior superior penetration.

Advance screw until at least 5 threads cross the physis (<5 increases progression risk). Screw tip should end ~5 mm from subchondral bone. Use approach-withdraw technique on fluoro to confirm position.

Modified Dunn (surgical hip dislocation/open realignment): indicated for severe/unstable slips needing correction (Ganz technique: lateral decubitus, straight lateral incision, gluteus maximus split, trochanteric flip, develop retinacular flap to protect blood supply during open reduction).
High-risk patients (age <10, obese male, endocrine disorders) warrant consideration of contralateral prophylactic pinning due to high incidence of bilateral disease.
Fluoroscopy is the primary 'approach' tool: AP pelvis/hip + true lateral (or frog-lateral equivalent) mandatory before committing to final pin position and length.

structures_at_risk.structure: Femoral head retinacular vessels (branches of medial femoral circumflex artery)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Unstable slips disrupt the physis and may already compromise perfusion; additional risk from aggressive closed reduction, pin penetration into head, or open surgery without vessel protection.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: For unstable SCFE prefer in situ pinning without forceful reduction. Confirm pin trajectory on true lateral to avoid anterior superior head penetration. For open Modified Dunn, use careful retinacular flap technique (Ganz) with hip extended/knee flexed to relax sciatic and protect vessels.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: AVN of the femoral epiphysis leading to collapse, deformity, and premature arthritis in a child or adolescent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Primarily percutaneous in situ pinning; open surgical dislocation for Modified Dunn variant

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The physis is the mechanical weak point in SCFE; further displacement can occur with manipulation or inadequate fixation. Iatrogenic damage during pinning affects future growth.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use gentle positioning and traction. Achieve stable fixation with adequate thread purchase (5+ threads across physis). Avoid multiple passes or large diameter reaming across the physis.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Acute slip progression, loss of reduction, premature physeal closure, leg length discrepancy or angular deformity.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Percutaneous pinning and open realignment

structures_at_risk.structure: Articular cartilage of femoral head (and acetabulum)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Pin penetration through the articular surface (especially anterior superior) or prominent hardware causes mechanical abrasion and chemical chondrolysis.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Center-center trajectory on both AP and true lateral fluoro views. Confirm screw tip is 5 mm short of subchondral bone and does not breach on any view. Use approach-withdraw to verify.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Chondrolysis: rapid joint space narrowing, pain, stiffness, and early degenerative change in the pediatric hip.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Percutaneous in situ pinning (primary risk); also relevant in open procedures

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral structures at starting point (IT band, vastus lateralis, cutaneous nerves)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Limited percutaneous incision on proximal lateral thigh; variable branches of lateral femoral cutaneous nerve or perforators can be injured during access or retraction.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Small incision directly over the planned starting point (anterior on lateral fluoro). Split fascia in line; minimal retraction. For open Modified Dunn use the full surgical dislocation interval.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness, neuroma, or minor bleeding; rarely clinically limiting but avoidable with careful portal placement.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Percutaneous pinning access; open variant uses trochanteric flip interval

key_landmarks: Greater trochanter (starting point reference on lateral fluoro)
Klein line (AP fluoro: superior neck line intersecting epiphysis confirms no slip or reduction)
Physis (target for thread purchase on AP and lateral views)
Anterior proximal femoral cortex (starting point for screw on true lateral view)
Center of epiphysis (final screw position goal on both orthogonal views)
key_intervals: Percutaneous: limited lateral proximal thigh interval through IT band and vastus lateralis fascia directly onto bone at the anterior starting point (no classic internervous plane for pinning).

Modified Dunn open: Kocher-Langenbeck-type interval (gluteus maximus split), trochanteric flip osteotomy, development of retinacular flap between piriformis and gluteus minimus/medius to preserve blood supply during open reduction.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: SCFE: post/inf epiphysis slip on metaphysis. Southwick angle, Klein line. Pinning: perc, center-center AP/lat fluoro (avoid ant sup pin penetration/chondrolysis). Start lat prox femur, traj ant to post/inf.

Percutaneous in situ fixation: single cannulated screw (or 2 for unstable) perpendicular or slightly oblique to physis from anterior proximal femur. Advance until 5 threads cross physis (<5 increases progression risk). Screw tip ~5 mm from subchondral.
Modified Dunn open realignment for severe/unstable slips: surgical hip dislocation with trochanteric flip and retinacular vessel protection (Ganz technique).
common_mistakes: Inadequate true lateral fluoro confirmation leading to anterior superior pin penetration and chondrolysis
Fewer than 5 threads across physis resulting in slip progression or loss of fixation
Aggressive closed reduction of unstable SCFE causing further retinacular vessel injury and AVN
Starting point too posterior or trajectory errors from misinterpreting AP view alone
Failure to consider contralateral prophylactic pinning in high-risk young patients
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP pelvis/hip: confirm Klein line intersects epiphysis (or post-reduction), assess slip angle, plan starting point and trajectory.
True lateral (or cross-table equivalent): confirm starting point is anterior on proximal femur, trajectory crosses physis center-center, no anterior or posterior penetration of head.
Final AP + lateral after provisional pin: verify 5+ threads across physis, tip 5 mm from subchondral bone, no joint penetration, and adequate purchase before advancing to final screw.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: In SCFE, the epiphysis typically slips in which direction relative to the metaphysis, and why does this dictate the pin starting point? A: Posteroinferior. This requires an anterior proximal femoral starting point (on lateral view) with trajectory directed posteriorly and inferiorly to reach the center of the slipped epiphysis.

Q: How many threads of the cannulated screw must cross the physis and what happens with fewer? A: At least 5 threads. Fewer than 5 significantly increases the risk of continued slip progression or loss of fixation.

Q: What is the primary blood supply at risk in SCFE pinning and which technical error most threatens it? A: Retinacular branches of the medial femoral circumflex artery. Anterior superior pin penetration into the head or aggressive reduction in unstable slips can disrupt the remaining supply leading to AVN.

Q: Why is the true lateral fluoroscopic view non-negotiable during SCFE pinning? A: It is the only view that reliably confirms the pin has not penetrated the anterior superior articular cartilage (chondrolysis) and that the trajectory is center-center in the epiphysis. AP view alone is insufficient.

Q: When is open Modified Dunn realignment preferred over in situ percutaneous pinning? A: For severe slips with unacceptable deformity where in situ pinning would leave the head in a poor position. Requires surgical dislocation, trochanteric flip, and careful retinacular flap mobilization to protect blood supply during open reduction (typically when physis is closed or near closed).

Memorize: 5+ threads across physis, center-center on AP + true lateral, 5 mm short of subchondral, avoid ant-sup penetration
Know the difference in management and risk for stable vs unstable SCFE
Review indications and technique pearls for Modified Dunn (retinacular protection)
Confirm high-risk criteria for contralateral prophylactic pinning
Practice the 5 pimp questions with concise answers and consequence/avoidance language
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique, attending preferences, or institutional protocols for unstable SCFE
Always verify pin position intraoperatively with high-quality fluoro in both planes before committing
Modified Dunn is a technically demanding open procedure with its own specific approach anatomy and blood supply preservation steps
approach_name: SCFE Percutaneous Pinning (primarily image-guided); Modified Dunn open variant

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Small incision on proximal lateral thigh directly over planned starting point. Split IT band and vastus lateralis fascia in line with fibers to reach bone. No major internervous plane; minimal soft-tissue dissection required for percutaneous screw placement.
structures_at_risk: Variable branches of lateral femoral cutaneous nerve
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Direct access for guidewire and cannulated screw placement under fluoro. Avoid excessive stripping or posterior starting point.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Lateral decubitus, straight lateral incision, split gluteus maximus, trochanteric flip osteotomy (digastric), develop interval between piriformis and gluteus minimus/medius to create retinacular flap. Hip extended and knee flexed to relax sciatic nerve during exposure and reduction.
key_structures: Short external rotators (protect or release carefully)
structures_at_risk: Medial femoral circumflex artery terminal branches
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Allows open reduction and realignment of severe slips while protecting the critical blood supply to the epiphysis. Different from standard adult posterior approach THA or trauma exposures.

danger_zones: Anterior superior femoral head penetration by pin or screw (chondrolysis) - confirm on true lateral fluoro
Aggressive closed reduction or multiple reduction attempts in unstable SCFE (further vessel stretch/avulsion leading to AVN)
Inadequate thread purchase (<5 across physis) - progression of slip or fixation failure
Starting point too far posterior or distal on the femur - poor trajectory, risk of posterior cortical blowout or inadequate epiphyseal purchase
approach_specific_notes: SCFE pinning is a percutaneous, fluoroscopy-driven reduction and implant procedure for most cases. The 'approach' is a limited lateral access portal rather than a formal open layered dissection. The open Modified Dunn uses a surgical hip dislocation (trochanteric flip) with specific retinacular preservation steps that must not be confused with adult THA posterior or direct lateral approaches. All content is pediatric-specific; no adult hip arthroplasty or fracture reduction anatomy is mixed in.
validation_warnings: SCFE pinning is primarily percutaneous image-guided fixation rather than a classic open approach procedure. Formal surgical_layers are limited (2 entries provided for the percutaneous access and the Modified Dunn open variant). See linked reduction_implant and fluoroscopy modules for trajectory and technique specifics. This explains fewer layer entries than open arthrotomy cases.

procedure_name: Supracondylar Humerus Fracture Pediatric (Closed Reduction + Perc Pinning)
procedure_overview: Extension-type (most common) supracondylar humerus fracture in children: posterior displacement of distal fragment. Closed reduction + percutaneous pinning (lateral or crossed). Key anatomy: distal humerus physis and capitellum (reduction landmarks via anterior humeral line and Baumann angle), brachial artery and median nerve (AIN) at risk with flexion-type or severely displaced, radial nerve (most common neuropraxia in extension type), ulnar nerve (at risk with medial pin). Pin trajectory critical: start lateral epicondyle, 30-40° cephalad on AP, slight anterior-lateral to center-center without anterior superior penetration (chondrolysis risk). Document all nerve function pre/post.
must_know_anatomy: Extension type most common (95+%); posterior displacement of distal fragment (Gartland classification based on displacement). Flexion type rarer, anterior displacement, often requires open reduction. Nerves: radial (most common in extension; document wrist and thumb extension), median (AIN branch with flexion-type or anterior displacement; document OK sign), ulnar (at risk with medial pin or flexion-type; document intrinsics and abductor digiti minimi).

Pinning technique (lateral or crossed): start at lateral epicondyle or just proximal, trajectory 30-40° cephalad on AP view, slight anterior and lateral to engage both cortices in center-center position of the capitellum/distal fragment. On lateral: avoid anterior superior penetration into the joint (chondrolysis). Check AP for pin divergence and no improper physis cross; lateral for no anterior penetration and adequate purchase.

Reduction landmarks: anterior humeral line should pass through the middle third of the capitellum on true lateral view (confirms sagittal reduction). Baumann angle (AP) assesses coronal alignment (normal ~70-75°; compare to contralateral). Medial and lateral epicondyles, olecranon, radial head, and brachial pulse are key palpable/fluoro landmarks.

Brachial artery: lies anterior to the fracture site in the antecubital fossa; can be tethered or lacerated with anterior spike of proximal fragment in displaced extension-type or with hyperflexion reduction. Vascular exam (pulse, color, Doppler, capillary refill) mandatory before and after reduction/pinning.

Distal humerus blood supply and physis: the capitellum and trochlea receive end-arterial supply; the physis is the weak point in children. Anterior humeral line and Baumann angle on fluoro confirm that reduction restores alignment without excessive flexion/extension or varus/valgus that would lead to cubitus varus or extension loss.

Medial pin (crossed pinning): placed from medial epicondyle anteriorly and proximally to avoid the ulnar nerve (which lies posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel). Mini-open or stay strictly anterior to the medial epicondyle reduces ulnar injury risk.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Most common nerve injury with extension-type supracondylar fractures (posterior displacement tents or contuses the nerve against the proximal fragment spike or in the spiral groove region). Can also be at risk with lateral pin placement if trajectory too proximal or posterior.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Document wrist and thumb extension pre-reduction and post-pinning. Use careful closed reduction technique (traction, flexion, pronation for extension-type). Lateral pins start at or distal to epicondyle with controlled trajectory; avoid blind proximal percutaneous passes.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Wrist drop, loss of thumb extension/abduction, sensory loss in first dorsal web. Usually neurapraxia that recovers, but document and follow; rare laceration requires exploration.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Closed reduction + percutaneous pinning (extension-type); open reduction if irreducible or vascular

structures_at_risk.structure: Median nerve (especially anterior interosseous nerve - AIN)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: AIN is vulnerable with flexion-type fractures or when the anteriorly displaced proximal fragment spike compresses the nerve in the antecubital fossa. Hyperflexion during reduction can also tether it.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Document AIN function (OK sign: flexion at index DIP and thumb IP) pre- and post every maneuver. Avoid excessive or prolonged hyperflexion during reduction; use gentle traction first. If AIN palsy present, ensure anatomic reduction.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Loss of index FDP and FPL function (inability to make OK sign), with or without sensory loss in median distribution. AIN palsy is often isolated motor.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Closed reduction + pinning; higher risk in flexion-type or severely displaced extension-type

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies immediately posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel. Medial pin for crossed pinning can directly injure or encircle the nerve if starting point is too posterior or if the nerve is subluxed.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: For crossed pinning, use a mini-open medial approach or place the medial pin with the elbow in extension and starting point strictly anterior on the medial epicondyle, directing the pin anterior-to-posterior and proximal. Document ulnar function (intrinsics, abductor digiti minimi, sensation) pre/post.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Intrinsic weakness, clawing of ring/small fingers, loss of abduction/adduction, sensory loss on ulnar border of hand and ulnar 1.5 digits. Can be permanent if transected.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Crossed percutaneous pinning (medial pin placement)

structures_at_risk.structure: Brachial artery (and median nerve/vein bundle in antecubital fossa)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: The anterior spike of the proximal humeral fragment in displaced extension-type or the reduction maneuver itself can tether, compress, or lacerate the brachial artery. Hyperflexion >90-100° for reduction can occlude flow even if pulse returns on extension.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Full vascular exam (radial pulse, color, Doppler, cap refill, forearm compartments) before reduction, after reduction, and after pinning. If pulse lost, gently extend the elbow slightly or accept less flexion once pinned. Explore if white/pulseless or compartment signs.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Limb-threatening ischemia, compartment syndrome, Volkmann ischemic contracture, or late thrombosis/aneurysm. Even 'pink pulseless' hand requires close observation and may need exploration.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Closed reduction + pinning of displaced supracondylar; open vascular exploration if indicated

key_landmarks: Medial and lateral epicondyles (pin starting points and alignment references)
Olecranon (posterior landmark; fracture often has posterior skin puckering or olecranon prominence in extension-type)
Radial head (lateral landmark for reduction and pin trajectory reference)
Capitellum (on lateral fluoro: anterior humeral line target is middle 1/3)
Brachial artery pulse (antecubital fossa - document and recheck after every reduction step)
key_intervals: Lateral pin access: percutaneous through skin and subcutaneous tissue into lateral epicondyle/distal humerus. Can split or go through triceps laterally or brachioradialis origin depending on starting point. Minimal open dissection.
Medial pin access (crossed): either pure percutaneous (higher ulnar risk) or limited medial approach anterior to medial epicondyle to identify/protect ulnar nerve before pin placement.

Open reduction (when needed): anterior approach (through brachialis or between brachioradialis and brachialis) for vascular exploration and irreducible fractures; posterior triceps-splitting less common in acute peds pinning.
reduction_or_implant_anatomy: Extension-type: posterior displacement. Reduce with traction, correct varus/valgus, then flex and pronate while pushing anteriorly on the olecranon. Anterior humeral line through middle capitellum on lateral confirms sagittal reduction.

Pinning: 2-3 lateral divergent pins or crossed (lateral + medial). Trajectory 30-40° cephalad AP, slight anterior-lateral on lateral view to achieve center-center without anterior joint penetration or excessive physis cross.

Gartland type influences: Type I nondisplaced (cast or pin), Type II displaced with intact posterior cortex (closed reduction + pin), Type III completely displaced (closed or open reduction + pin, often crossed for stability).
common_mistakes: Failure to document all three nerves (radial, median/AIN, ulnar) and vascular status before and after reduction/pinning
Anterior superior pin penetration on lateral fluoro causing chondrolysis of the capitellum/trochlea
Medial pin placed too posteriorly or without protection leading to permanent ulnar nerve injury
Hyperflexion reduction maintained after pinning causing brachial artery occlusion or compartment syndrome
Inadequate reduction (anterior humeral line not through middle capitellum or abnormal Baumann angle) resulting in cubitus varus or loss of extension
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP: Baumann angle ~70-75° (compare contralateral), pin divergence, no improper physis crossing, medial/lateral column alignment.
True lateral: anterior humeral line passes through middle third of capitellum (sagittal reduction); confirm no anterior superior pin penetration into joint; verify pin purchase in both cortices.
After pinning with elbow slightly extended from the flexed reduction position: recheck vascular (pulse returns or remains), then final AP/lateral with pins in place before cutting and bending.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: For percutaneous pinning of extension-type supracondylar humerus fracture, what is the ideal starting point and trajectory on AP and lateral fluoro to achieve center-center purchase while minimizing anterior superior pin penetration (chondrolysis) or radial nerve injury? A: Start at or just proximal to lateral epicondyle. AP: 30-40° cephalad with slight anterior and lateral trajectory to center the pins in the distal fragment. Lateral: slight anterior trajectory to avoid anterior superior joint penetration; confirm center-center and no breach.

Q: Which nerve is most commonly injured with extension-type supracondylar fractures, and what motor function must be documented pre- and post-reduction/pinning? A: Radial nerve (most common). Document wrist extension and thumb extension/abduction (first dorsal web sensation also). Neurapraxia is common but must be recorded.

Q: What are the Gartland types based on displacement, and how does type influence closed vs open reduction and pinning configuration (lateral vs crossed)? A: Type I: nondisplaced (cast or pin). Type II: displaced, posterior cortex intact (usually closed reduction + 2-3 lateral pins). Type III: completely displaced (closed or open reduction + pinning; often crossed configuration for rotational stability in unstable patterns).

Q: During medial pin placement for crossed pinning, what structure is at risk and what landmark/technique reduces ulnar nerve injury? A: Ulnar nerve (posterior to medial epicondyle in cubital tunnel). Use mini-open medial approach or place pin strictly anterior on the medial epicondyle with elbow extended, directing anterior-to-posterior and proximal. Document intrinsics and ulnar sensation pre/post.

Q: What is the blood supply to the distal humerus and why is the anterior humeral line or Baumann's angle important on fluoro for reduction? A: End-arterial supply to capitellum/trochlea via posterior perforators and anterior vessels. Anterior humeral line (lateral view) through middle 1/3 capitellum confirms sagittal reduction; Baumann angle (AP) assesses coronal alignment. Malreduction leads to cubitus varus or extension loss.

Memorize: document radial (wrist/thumb ext), AIN (OK sign), ulnar (intrinsics) and pulse before/after every step

Know when to go open (irreducible, vascular injury, open fracture) and pink-pulseless hand management
