Always perform and document complete neurovascular exam before and after reduction and pinning; thresholds for exploration are clinical (white/pulseless, tense compartment, irreducible)
Crossed vs lateral-only pinning is surgeon- and stability-dependent; ulnar nerve risk with medial pin is the key anatomic consideration
approach_name: Closed reduction + percutaneous pinning (lateral or crossed); limited open for medial pin protection or vascular exploration

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Percutaneous stab incisions over or just proximal to lateral epicondyle. Pins pass through skin, subcutaneous tissue, and into the distal humerus lateral column. Can traverse lateral head of triceps or brachioradialis origin depending on exact starting point and trajectory. Minimal soft tissue disruption.
structures_at_risk: Radial nerve (if pin too proximal or posterior on lateral column)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Primary pinning corridor for most extension-type fractures. Trajectory control on fluoro is more important than open layers.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Either pure percutaneous (higher risk) or limited open: small incision anterior to medial epicondyle, identify ulnar nerve in cubital tunnel or confirm it is posterior, then place pin from anterior aspect of medial epicondyle directed anterior-to-posterior and proximal into the medial column.
structures_at_risk: Ulnar nerve (direct injury or encircling by pin if starting point posterior)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Crossed pinning provides better rotational stability for Type III or unstable patterns. Ulnar protection is the critical anatomic step for the medial pin.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anterior approach (interval between brachioradialis and brachialis or through brachialis split) to the antecubital fossa for exploration of brachial artery, median nerve, and fracture. Allows direct reduction of the anterior spike and vascular repair if needed. Posterior triceps approach less commonly used acutely.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Reserved for white/pulseless hand after reduction, open fractures, or irreducible closed fractures with neurovascular compromise. Different from elective elbow approaches.

danger_zones: Anterior superior pin penetration into the joint (chondrolysis of capitellum/trochlea) - confirm on true lateral fluoro before final seating
Medial pin injury to ulnar nerve (use mini-open or strictly anterior starting point on medial epicondyle)
Brachial artery tethering or occlusion from hyperflexion reduction or unreduced anterior spike - check pulse after pinning with elbow slightly extended
Radial nerve injury from fracture displacement or overly proximal/posterior lateral pin placement - document wrist/thumb extension
approach_specific_notes: Supracondylar humerus fracture pinning in children is primarily closed reduction + percutaneous pinning (lateral divergent or crossed). There is no formal open 'approach' for the majority of cases; the 'layers' are limited to pin portals. A limited medial open is used only for safe medial pin placement in crossed constructs. Full open reduction (usually anterior) is reserved for vascular exploration or irreducible fractures. All content is strictly pediatric extension-type (and flexion) supracondylar; do not mix with adult distal humerus fracture ORIF, elbow arthroplasty approaches, or lateral condyle fracture anatomy.
validation_warnings: Supracondylar peds pinning is a closed reduction + percutaneous pinning procedure for most cases. Formal surgical_layers are limited to the pin access portals and the rare open reduction for vascular/irreducible cases (3 entries provided). This is expected for CRPP-dominant trauma; see reduction and fluoroscopy modules for trajectory details.

procedure_overview: Direct Anterior (Smith-Petersen/Hueter) approach for total hip arthroplasty. Internervous/intermuscular plane between TFL (superior gluteal n.) laterally and sartorius (femoral n.) medially. Key structures: LFCN (often in or superficial to interval), ascending branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery (crosses interval distal to ASIS), rectus femoris (femoral n.), anterior hip capsule. Exposure of femoral neck and acetabulum through this plane without violating abductors or short external rotators. Extend distally along TFL as needed.
must_know_anatomy: Direct anterior interval: TFL (superior gluteal innervation, retract lateral) and sartorius (femoral n., retract medial). LFCN typically lies in the fascia or just medial in the interval and is at risk during superficial dissection.
Ascending branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery crosses the interval ~5-8 cm distal to ASIS; must be identified, coagulated or ligated to prevent bleeding during deep exposure and capsulotomy.

Deep to the interval: develop plane along rectus femoris (femoral n. innervated) medially or release its origin/reflected head as needed for femoral and acetabular exposure. Anterior capsule is directly accessible for arthrotomy.
Femoral nerve and vessels lie medial to sartorius; excessive or prolonged medial retraction (especially with hip flexion or during acetabular reaming) risks neuropraxia or vascular injury.
Key landmarks: ASIS (incision start ~2-3 cm lateral/distal), TFL (lateral boundary), sartorius (medial boundary), anterior superior capsule and femoral neck.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Skin incision ~2-3 cm lateral and distal to ASIS, extending 8-12 cm along anterior border of TFL. Superficial interval between TFL (sup. gluteal) and sartorius (femoral). Protect LFCN early in fascia.

Deep: develop TFL-sartorius interval, control ascending LFCA branches that cross ~5-8 cm distal to ASIS. Release rectus origin or reflected head as needed for acetabular exposure. Anterior capsulectomy or capsulotomy for joint access. No abductor split or ER release is performed.

Closure: repair capsule if possible, close fascia and skin. Capsule repair is anterior-only; no structures from other approaches are involved. Leg length and stability assessed with trial components before final implantation.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Courses in or immediately adjacent to the TFL-sartorius interval; vulnerable to direct transection during skin/fascial incision, or stretch from retraction.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the nerve in the superficial fascia or interval early (often 1-2 cm medial to TFL border). Protect by retracting sartorius medially or TFL laterally with the nerve. Avoid blind cautery in the interval.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterolateral thigh numbness, painful neuroma, or dysesthesia. Usually tolerable but can be distressing for patients; sometimes permanent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct anterior (Smith-Petersen/Hueter) THA

structures_at_risk.structure: Ascending branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery (LFCA)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the TFL-sartorius interval transversely a few centimeters distal to the ASIS in the deep fascia or on the capsule. Uncontrolled bleeding obscures the field.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and ligate or coagulate the vessel(s) before they retract into the capsule or muscle. Have clips/sutures ready before deep capsular work.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Obscured visualization, increased blood loss, possible hematoma. Rarely major vascular compromise but can lead to revision for bleeding or infection risk.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct anterior approach deep dissection and capsulotomy

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lie medial to the sartorius; excessive or prolonged medial retraction (especially with hip flexion or during acetabular reaming) can cause neuropraxia or vascular injury.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit duration and force of medial retraction. Use careful retractor placement (Hohmann or pointed retractors on the anterior column under direct vision). Monitor quadriceps function post-op if retraction was heavy.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Quadriceps weakness or foot drop (femoral n.), vascular injury requiring repair, or compartment syndrome from hematoma. Usually neuropraxia resolves but can be permanent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct anterior THA medial retraction and acetabular preparation

structures_at_risk.structure: Anterior capsule and rectus femoris origin
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Directly exposed and often partially released or excised for acetabular access; over-release can affect hip flexion strength temporarily.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Perform controlled anterior capsulectomy or H-shaped capsulotomy. Tag rectus if released for later repair if desired. Avoid excessive medial dissection into iliopsoas.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Transient hip flexion weakness, instability if capsule not managed, or iliopsoas irritation. Rare permanent deficit.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Direct anterior capsular exposure for THA

key_landmarks: ASIS (incision reference ~2-3 cm lateral/distal)
TFL (lateral boundary of interval)
Sartorius (medial boundary, origin at ASIS)
Anterior superior capsule and femoral neck (deep target)
Ascending LFCA crossing (5-8 cm distal to ASIS)
key_intervals: Superficial: skin and subcutaneous fat over the TFL-sartorius interval; LFCN identified in fascia.
Deep muscular interval: TFL retracted laterally, sartorius medially; ascending LFCA branches crossing the field are controlled.
Capsular plane: anterior capsule exposed between or under rectus femoris; anterior capsulectomy or H-shaped capsulotomy for arthroplasty access.
common_mistakes: Injury to LFCN from failure to identify/protect in the superficial interval (anterolateral thigh numbness or neuroma).
Uncontrolled bleeding from ascending LFCA branches obscuring capsule and joint during capsulotomy.
Excessive or prolonged medial retraction causing femoral neuropraxia or vascular compromise.
Over-release of rectus origin affecting hip flexion strength or stability.
Inadequate capsule management leading to instability or excessive post-op precautions.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is the internervous/intermuscular plane for the direct anterior hip approach and which nerves define it? A: Between TFL (superior gluteal n., retract lateral) and sartorius (femoral n., retract medial). LFCN is at risk in the interval.

Q: Where and why is the ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery encountered in direct anterior THA, and what is the consequence of missing it? A: Crosses the TFL-sartorius interval ~5-8 cm distal to ASIS. Uncontrolled bleeding obscures the capsule and joint; ligate early.

Q: How is the femoral nerve/vessel bundle protected during direct anterior exposure and acetabular work? A: Limit duration and force of medial retraction (sartorius side). Use careful retractor placement under direct vision. Monitor quad function post-op.

Q: What are the key steps of the direct anterior approach for THA and the primary structures at risk in each layer? A: Superficial: protect LFCN in TFL-sartorius interval. Deep: control ascending LFCA. Deep: anterior capsule exposure and capsulectomy. Risks: LFCN, LFCA, femoral bundle.

Q: Why is the direct anterior approach considered muscle-sparing for THA and what are the trade-offs? A: No abductor violation or anterior-only ER release. Trade-offs: LFCN risk, limited extensibility anterior-onlyly, steep learning curve for femoral preparation.
night_before_review_checklist: Review the tha-approaches page (12116) focusing on the anterior/DA section and interval descriptions
Memorize: TFL-sartorius plane, LFCN protection, coagulate ascending LFCA, rectus plane to capsule, femoral bundle medial
Know extension options and landmarks (ASIS, TFL, rectus origin at AIIS)
Review consequence of LFCN injury (anterolateral thigh numbness) and femoral n. stretch (quad weakness)
Practice the 5 pimp questions emphasizing how DA differs from Hardinge/posterior (no abductor or ER violation)
Confirm case is planned direct anterior (not posterior or lateral) and any patient factors (e.g., body habitus, prior surgery) affecting exposure
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep for direct anterior THA only; does not replace full surgical technique, implant-specific details, or attending preferences for capsulotomy/repair or femoral preparation

Dedicated direct anterior module content in current project artifacts is limited and was previously mixed with posterior, Hardinge, and Perthes material; enrichment uses only supported anterior interval descriptions from tha-approaches and shared hip texts
approach_name: Direct Anterior (Smith-Petersen / Hueter) approach to the hip for THA

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision begins ~2-3 cm lateral and distal to ASIS and extends ~8-12 cm along the anterior border of TFL. Identify and protect the LFCN in the subcutaneous tissue or fascia before splitting the interval.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard direct anterior approach for THA. The internervous plane is between TFL (superior gluteal) and sartorius (femoral). LFCN protection is the first critical step.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep muscular interval and vessel control (LFCA)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Develop the plane between TFL (lateral) and sartorius (medial). The ascending branch of the LFCA crosses the interval ~5-8 cm distal to ASIS. Identify, ligate or coagulate before it retracts. Rectus femoris origin or reflected head may be released for acetabular exposure.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Gives direct access to the anterior capsule and femoral neck without violating abductors. Vessel control prevents obscuring the field during capsulotomy.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anterior capsule is exposed between or beneath the rectus. Perform anterior capsulectomy or capsulotomy (H-shaped or flap) for head dislocation and acetabular/femoral preparation. Protect medial structures with careful retraction.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working layer for THA. No anterior-only structures or abductors are violated. Capsule management affects stability and post-op precautions.

danger_zones: LFCN in superficial TFL-sartorius interval - identify early, protect with sartorius medially or TFL laterally; injury causes anterolateral thigh numbness/neuroma.
Ascending LFCA crossing the interval - identify and control before it retracts and bleeds into the field, obscuring capsule.
Femoral nerve/vessels with medial retraction (sartorius side) - limit force/duration especially during acetabular reaming; risk of quad weakness or vascular injury.
Anterior capsule and rectus - controlled release for exposure; over-release affects flexion strength or stability.
approach_specific_notes: Strictly direct anterior (Smith-Petersen/Hueter) approach for THA. Internervous plane TFL (sup. gluteal) / sartorius (femoral). Key risks: LFCN in/superficial to interval, ascending LFCA crossing distal to ASIS, femoral bundle with medial retraction. No abductor violation or anterior-only ERs. All educational content is anterior-only.
validation_warnings: This payload is for direct anterior (Smith-Petersen/Hueter) THA only. Content is restricted to anterior interval facts (TFL/sartorius, LFCN, LFCA, rectus, anterior capsule) from available tha-approaches and hip module snippets. No Hardinge, anterior-only, or other approach anatomy is included. Further dedicated direct anterior source extraction is recommended for complete distances and technique pearls.

procedure_overview: Total Hip Arthroplasty via Posterior (Moore/Southern) approach. Patient in lateral decubitus position. Incision is posterior to the greater trochanter. Split gluteus maximus in line with its fibers. Identify sciatic nerve early on the posterior surface of quadratus femoris. Protect sciatic nerve by extending the hip, flexing the knee, and using obturator internus tendon as a cushion during gentle retraction. Detach short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus, gemelli) from their femoral insertion for exposure and tag for later repair. Expose and incise posterior capsule. Dislocate hip with flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. Acetabular and femoral preparation performed under direct posterior visualization. After component implantation, repair short external rotators and posterior capsule to the greater trochanter to restore posterior stability and reduce risk of dislocation. Inferior gluteal neurovascular bundle lies in the field near quadratus femoris.
must_know_anatomy: Posterior (Moore/Southern) approach: patient lateral decubitus. Incision posterior to greater trochanter, split gluteus maximus in line with fibers (no true internervous plane in maximus).

Short external rotators (piriformis superior, obturator internus with gemelli inferior) attach to posterior femur. They are detached from bone for exposure and must be repaired at closure to restore posterior soft-tissue tension and prevent dislocation.

Sciatic nerve lies on quadratus femoris posterior to the hip. Protection: locate along quadratus femoris early; maintain hip extension and knee flexion throughout; use obturator internus tendon as a retractor cushion; peroneal division is more vulnerable distally.

Posterior capsule is incised for dislocation (flex/adduct/IR the hip). Capsule is repaired at the end together with the short external rotators to the posterior greater trochanter or trochanteric crest.

Inferior gluteal artery and nerve travel with the sciatic nerve in the greater sciatic notch region and can be injured during deep posterior dissection or aggressive retraction near quadratus femoris.

Greater trochanter is the key palpable landmark. Piriformis tendon inserts just proximal to the posterior facet of the greater trochanter; obturator internus inserts more distally on the posterior femur. These serve as guides for safe ER release.

After repair of short ERs and capsule, the posterior soft-tissue envelope is restored. Failure to repair leads to increased posterior translation and dislocation risk in the early postoperative period.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Posterior skin incision is centered over the posterior third of the greater trochanter and extends proximally and distally as needed. Gluteus maximus is split in the direction of its fibers from the tip of the trochanter proximally.

Deep to maximus split: identify the sciatic nerve on the posterior surface of quadratus femoris. The short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus/gemelli) cross superficial to the nerve and are detached from the posterior femur.
Posterior capsule is exposed between the released short rotators. Capsule is opened for dislocation. After implantation the capsule edges are tagged and repaired with the short rotators to the femur.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies directly on quadratus femoris posterior to the hip joint. Can be stretched, compressed by retractors, or injured during ER release or reduction maneuvers if not identified and protected.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the nerve on the posterior surface of quadratus femoris before any deep retraction. Maintain hip in extension and knee in flexion to slack the nerve. Use the obturator internus tendon as a cushion for the nerve during retraction. Release short ERs from bone rather than cutting across the nerve.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop, loss of ankle dorsiflexion/eversion, sensory loss on dorsum of foot and lateral leg. Can be temporary neurapraxia or permanent if lacerated or severely stretched.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior (Moore/Southern) approach - entire deep exposure and dislocation

structures_at_risk.structure: Short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus, gemelli)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Detached from posterior femur for exposure. If not repaired or repaired poorly, posterior soft-tissue tension is lost, increasing risk of posterior hip dislocation.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Tag each tendon with suture before detachment. Detach directly from bone insertion. At closure, repair the tendons (with capsule) back to the posterior greater trochanter or trochanteric crest using heavy non-absorbable suture through bone tunnels or anchors.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Early posterior dislocation (especially with flexion/adduction/internal rotation activities). Loss of the posterior soft-tissue envelope that acts as a checkrein against posterior translation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior approach - exposure and closure

structures_at_risk.structure: Inferior gluteal artery / nerve (near quadratus femoris)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Travel in the region of quadratus femoris and can be injured during identification of the sciatic nerve or during release of the inferior short rotators.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Careful blunt dissection when exposing quadratus femoris. Bipolar cautery for any bleeding in this area rather than blind clamping. Protect the bundle when placing retractors posterior to the femur.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Bleeding/hematoma in the posterior compartment; possible compromise of sciatic nerve perfusion if vessel is sacrificed. Nerve injury can affect gluteus maximus function.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deep posterior exposure near quadratus femoris

structures_at_risk.structure: Posterior capsule and external rotator interval
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Incised for dislocation. If not repaired, the posterior checkrein against dislocation is lost. The interval between the released rotators must be closed as a unit with the capsule.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Tag capsule edges at the time of incision. After final reduction, repair capsule edges to each other and to the repaired short external rotators. Use the repaired ERs as the primary posterior stabilizer.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Posterior instability and dislocation in the early post-operative period, particularly with positions of flexion >90°, adduction, and internal rotation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Posterior approach - capsulotomy and closure

key_landmarks: Greater trochanter (posterior border is the key for incision and ER attachment reference)
Piriformis tendon (most superior short ER, inserts just proximal to the posterior facet of the GT)
Obturator internus tendon with gemelli (inferior short ERs, insert on the posterior femur distal to piriformis)
Quadratus femoris (sciatic nerve lies on its posterior surface - primary nerve landmark)
Posterior capsule (between the released short rotators and the posterior acetabulum/femoral neck)
common_mistakes: Failure to identify and protect the sciatic nerve on quadratus femoris before deep retraction or ER release, leading to iatrogenic nerve injury.
Not repairing (or poorly repairing) the short external rotators and posterior capsule to the greater trochanter, resulting in early posterior dislocation.
Releasing the short ERs too close to the sciatic nerve or cutting across the nerve while attempting to detach the tendons.
Excessive or prolonged retraction on the sciatic nerve without hip extension/knee flexion or without using obturator internus as a cushion.
Placing retractors or hardware in a position that compresses the sciatic nerve or inferior gluteal bundle posterior to the femur.
fluoroscopy_checkpoints: AP pelvis and cross-table lateral after reduction to confirm component position, leg length, and offset before final closure (standard for all THA approaches).
attending_pimp_questions: Q: Describe the short external rotators and their role in sciatic nerve protection during the posterior approach. A: Piriformis (most superior), obturator internus with gemelli (inferior). They cross superficial to the sciatic nerve on quadratus femoris. They are detached from the femur for exposure; the obturator internus tendon is used as a cushion to gently retract the nerve. They must be repaired at closure for posterior stability.

Q: What maneuvers protect the sciatic nerve during posterior THA exposure and dislocation? A: Identify the nerve early on the posterior surface of quadratus femoris. Maintain the hip in extension and the knee in flexion to slack the nerve throughout the case. Use the obturator internus tendon as a retractor cushion. Release the short ERs directly from bone rather than cutting near the nerve.

Q: Why is repair of the short external rotators and posterior capsule important after posterior THA? A: The repaired rotators and capsule restore the posterior soft-tissue envelope that acts as the primary checkrein against posterior translation. Failure to repair increases the risk of early posterior dislocation, especially with flexion/adduction/internal rotation.

Q: Where is the inferior gluteal artery/nerve relative to the sciatic nerve in the posterior approach, and why does it matter? A: It travels in the region of quadratus femoris near the sciatic nerve. Injury can cause bleeding/hematoma and may affect sciatic perfusion or gluteus maximus function. Careful blunt dissection and bipolar use near quadratus femoris are required.

Q: What is the typical direction of hip dislocation in the posterior approach, and how does this relate to the structures at risk? A: Flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. This position tensions the posterior structures and brings the sciatic nerve into the field; the short ERs must be released and the capsule opened to allow safe dislocation without excessive nerve stretch.
night_before_review_checklist: Review the posterior hip approach page (12023) and tha-approaches (12116) focusing on Moore/Southern landmarks, short ER anatomy, and sciatic protection maneuvers.

Memorize: incision posterior to GT, gluteus maximus split, sciatic on quadratus femoris, hip extension + knee flexion + obturator internus cushion, detach and tag short ERs (piriformis, obturator internus), repair at closure to GT.
Know the pimp questions on sciatic protection, why repair the rotators, and inferior gluteal relationship.
Confirm case is planned posterior approach (not anterior or lateral) and review any patient factors (prior surgery, nerve symptoms) that increase sciatic risk.
Walk through the dislocation maneuver (flex/adduct/IR) and the closure/repair steps that restore posterior stability.
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep for the posterior (Moore/Southern) approach only. Does not replace full surgical technique, implant positioning details, or attending-specific preferences for capsular repair or stability assessment.
Always identify the sciatic nerve early and maintain hip extension/knee flexion. Document sciatic nerve function pre- and post-operatively.
approach_name: Posterior (Moore/Southern) approach to the hip for THA

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Patient in lateral decubitus. Incision is centered over the posterior third of the greater trochanter and extends proximally along the posterior border of the trochanter and distally along the femoral shaft as needed. Split the gluteus maximus in line with its fibers starting at the tip of the trochanter. There is no true internervous plane within the maximus itself.
key_structures: Subcutaneous fat and fascia over maximus
structures_at_risk: Inferior gluteal neurovascular bundle (deep to maximus proximally)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Establishes the posterior corridor. The maximus split must be long enough proximally to allow retraction without tearing. This layer exposes the short external rotators and the sciatic nerve lying on quadratus femoris.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (short external rotators and sciatic nerve)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Deep to the maximus split, the short external rotators (piriformis most superior, then obturator internus with gemelli) are seen crossing the field superficial to the sciatic nerve. The sciatic nerve is identified on the posterior surface of quadratus femoris. The short ERs are tagged with sutures and detached directly from their insertion on the posterior femur. The obturator internus tendon is used as a cushion to gently retract the sciatic nerve.
structures_at_risk: Sciatic nerve (stretch, compression, or laceration during ER release or retraction)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: This is the critical layer for sciatic protection and exposure. Releasing the short ERs from bone (rather than cutting near the nerve) and using obturator internus as a retractor cushion are the key technical points that reduce nerve injury risk.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Capsule and joint (posterior capsulotomy, dislocation, exposure)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: The posterior capsule is exposed between the released short rotators. The capsule is incised (often in a T or H fashion) to allow dislocation. The hip is dislocated with flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. After component implantation the hip is reduced and the short ERs plus posterior capsule are repaired as a unit back to the posterior greater trochanter or trochanteric crest using heavy suture through bone.
key_structures: Posterior acetabulum and femoral neck (after dislocation)
structures_at_risk: Sciatic nerve (during reduction or while delivering the proximal femur)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Final exposure for acetabular and femoral preparation. The quality of the short ER + capsule repair directly determines early posterior stability. This layer is unique to the posterior approach and is not performed in anterior or lateral approaches.

danger_zones: Sciatic nerve on quadratus femoris - identify early, hip extension + knee flexion + obturator internus cushion, release ERs from bone; injury causes foot drop and sensory loss.
Inferior gluteal artery/nerve near quadratus femoris - blunt dissection, bipolar cautery; injury causes bleeding and possible sciatic compromise.
Short external rotators - tag before detachment, repair to GT at closure with capsule; failure causes early posterior dislocation with flexion/adduction/IR.
Posterior capsule - repair as a unit with the short ERs; inadequate repair removes the primary posterior checkrein against dislocation.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is strictly for the posterior (Moore/Southern) approach to THA. Patient is positioned lateral decubitus. The defining steps are gluteus maximus split, early sciatic identification on quadratus femoris with consistent hip extension/knee flexion, detachment and tagged repair of the short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus/gemelli), and posterior capsulotomy with repair at closure for stability. There is no abductor split (unlike Hardinge/direct lateral) and no anterior interval (TFL/sartorius/rectus). Content from direct lateral or anterior approaches is explicitly not applicable. The short ER and capsule repair is the posterior-specific step that restores the soft-tissue envelope against posterior dislocation.
validation_warnings: This payload contains only posterior (Moore/Southern) approach anatomy. Any Hardinge/direct lateral (superior gluteal/abductor split) or anterior (TFL/sartorius/rectus) content has been excluded. The payload is strictly posterior-only.

procedure_overview: Tibial Shaft Fracture ORIF (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Tibia subcutaneous anteromedially (easy landmark for approach); leg has 4 compartments (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior). IMN gold standard; plate for specific proximal/distal or soft tissue needs.
Anterior compartment: tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL, deep peroneal nerve, anterior tibial vessels. At risk in anterolateral approaches or swelling.
Lateral compartment: peroneus longus/brevis, superficial peroneal nerve (sensory to dorsum foot). Nerve exits fascia ~10-12 cm proximal to lateral malleolus.
Medial: saphenous vein and nerve (sensory) cross anteromedial distal third. At risk in medial approaches or incisions.
Posterior: superficial (gastroc/soleus/plantaris), deep (tib post, FDL, FHL, posterior tibial vessels, tibial nerve). Popliteal trifurcation proximally.
Compartment syndrome risk high in tibial shaft (swelling, bleeding); monitor pressures if tense or high-energy. Fasciotomy if indicated.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Anteromedial: along subcutaneous border (easy, but skin issues in high energy). Incision medial to crest, develop to bone avoiding saphenous. Good for medial plate or open fractures with medial wound.

Anterolateral: for lateral plate or when medial skin bad. Interval or split anterior compartment muscles; protect deep peroneal and ant tib vessels. Lateral compartment muscles (peronei) retracted posteriorly if needed.

Reduction/plating: traction for length, direct clamp or indirect for alignment. Plate on safe corridor (medial or lateral depending approach). Check rotation with contralateral or fluoro. Respect compartments throughout.

structures_at_risk.structure: Deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial vessels (anterior compartment)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In anterior compartment; at risk during anterolateral approach, swelling, or retraction for lateral plating. Nerve supplies EHL/EDL/TA and sensation 1st web.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect with vessel loop in anterior compartment work. Limit retraction time/force. Monitor for compartment syndrome (high risk in tibial shaft).
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop (EHL/EDL/TA weakness), 1st web numbness. May be neuropraxia or ischemic if compartment missed.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterolateral or anterior exposure for tibial shaft plating

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Exits lateral compartment fascia ~10-12 cm above lateral malleolus; at risk in lateral or anterolateral incisions or swelling.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Know the exit point; protect during lateral compartment retraction or fasciotomy. Use direct visualization for distal lateral work.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Sensory loss dorsum foot (except 1st web). Painful neuroma possible. Motor usually spared (peronei innervated more proximally).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Lateral/anterolateral tibial shaft approaches

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Cross anteromedial distal leg; at risk in medial incisions or during saphenous vein harvest-like exposure for medial plate.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify under skin in distal medial approaches. Protect with vessel loop or retract carefully. Avoid blind clamping.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Saphenous neuralgia (medial leg/foot numbness/pain) or vein injury (bleeding, later varicosities if harvest context).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial approach for tibial shaft plating

structures_at_risk.structure: Posterior tibial vessels and tibial nerve (deep posterior compartment)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In deep posterior; at risk with posterior swelling, missed compartment syndrome, or if posteromedial work needed for specific fractures.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Monitor compartments (high risk in tibial shaft). If posterior exposure needed, use careful plane and direct visualization. Fasciotomy if pressures elevated.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Tibial nerve deficit (intrinsic foot weakness, plantar numbness), vascular compromise to foot. Missed compartment leads to contractures (Volkmann).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Tibial shaft with compartment concerns or posteromedial access

common_mistakes: Failure to identify/protect superficial peroneal nerve at its fascial exit in lateral/anterolateral approaches, causing dorsal foot numbness.
Inadequate protection of deep peroneal/ant tib vessels during anterior compartment work, leading to foot drop or ischemic injury.
Missing compartment syndrome (high risk in tibial shaft); delayed fasciotomy leads to Volkmann contracture and severe disability.
Saphenous injury in medial approaches (neuralgia or vein damage).
Ignoring rotation/length in reduction or using unsafe plate corridors without checking neurovascular.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the 4 leg compartments and key contents at risk in tibial shaft fractures/ORIF? A: Anterior (TA/EHL/EDL, deep peroneal n, ant tib v); Lateral (peronei, sup peroneal n); Superficial post (gastroc/soleus); Deep post (tib post/FDL/FHL, post tib v, tibial n). All at risk for compartment syndrome.

Q: Describe the superficial peroneal nerve course and why it is at risk in lateral/anterolateral tibial approaches. A: Runs in lateral compartment, exits fascia ~10-12 cm above lateral malleolus to become subcutaneous. At risk in lateral incisions or swelling; injury causes dorsal foot numbness (motor usually spared).

Q: How do you protect the deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial vessels during anterolateral exposure for tibial plating? A: Develop anterior compartment carefully, identify and loop the neurovascular bundle, limit retraction, and monitor for compartment syndrome (high risk in these fractures).

Q: What is the saphenous nerve/vein risk in medial approaches to the tibial shaft and how to avoid? A: Cross anteromedial distal third. Identify under skin, protect with loop or careful retraction. Injury causes medial leg/foot sensory loss or neuroma.

Q: Why is compartment syndrome monitoring critical after tibial shaft ORIF and what are the consequences if missed? A: High risk due to swelling/bleeding into closed compartments. Missed leads to Volkmann contracture (severe disability, foot drop, claw toes, sensory loss). Measure pressures if tense/pain out of proportion; fasciotomy if elevated.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to fascia over anteromedial border or anterior/lateral compartments)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anteromedial: incision along subcutaneous border (medial to tibial crest). Develop to bone, protect saphenous vein/nerve crossing distally. Anterolateral: incision over anterior compartment, develop to fascia, identify superficial peroneal exit point distally if lateral work.
key_structures: Superficial peroneal nerve exit (lateral, ~10-12cm above malleolus)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Anteromedial is simplest for medial plating/open wounds. Anterolateral for lateral plate when medial skin compromised. Identify nerves early to prevent iatrogenic injury.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (compartment muscles to bone, safe corridors)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anteromedial: strip periosteum carefully from medial border. Protect saphenous. For lateral: develop anterior compartment (protect deep peroneal/ant tib v), retract peronei if needed for lateral surface. Identify safe plate corridors (medial or lateral depending approach).
key_structures: Deep peroneal n / ant tib vessels
structures_at_risk: Deep peroneal n / ant tib v (anterior)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working layer for reduction and fixation. Neurovascular protection and compartment awareness are essential; tibial shaft has high compartment risk.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Close fascia loosely or not at all if swelling/compartment concern. Skin in layers. High vigilance for compartment syndrome post-op (pain, swelling, tense compartments) - measure pressures if uncertain. Fasciotomy if indicated (4-compartment).
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Tibial shaft fractures have high compartment syndrome risk due to swelling and bleeding into closed compartments. Loose closure and monitoring prevent disaster.

validation_warnings: Tibial shaft payload emphasizes compartment anatomy and neurovascular risks (high syndrome risk). IMN is preferred for most; this covers plate ORIF anatomy when indicated. Source material previously mixed femur/ankle and contained stringified/empty/placeholder SAR + dev paths + generic; cleaned to public OB 1043 facts and rich 6-field content only.
danger_zones: Deep peroneal n / ant tib v (anterior): protect in anterolateral work; injury causes foot drop + 1st web numbness; compartment risk high.
Superficial peroneal n (lateral fascia exit ~10-12cm above malleolus): identify in lateral approaches; injury causes dorsal foot sensory loss.
Saphenous v/n (medial distal): protect in medial incisions; injury causes medial leg/foot neuralgia or bleeding.
Posterior tibial n/v (deep post): at risk in swelling/missed compartment; injury causes plantar numbness + intrinsic weakness + vascular compromise to foot.
All 4 compartments: monitor post-op (tense, pain); missed syndrome = contractures, poor function.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for tibial shaft fracture fixation (IMN gold standard; plate ORIF for specific indications like proximal/distal metaphyseal, soft tissue, or open fractures with specific needs). Focus on 4 compartments, deep/superficial peroneal, saphenous, posterior structures if relevant, and compartment monitoring. No femoral/hip or isolated ankle content mixed. Content is shaft-specific with safe corridor awareness.

procedure_overview: Total Knee Arthroplasty (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Medial parapatellar approach: skin incision medial to midline or along medial border of patella/quadriceps tendon; develop medial skin flap, incise medial retinaculum and vastus medialis obliquus fascia to enter joint.
Extensor mechanism: quadriceps tendon, patella, patellar tendon (protect insertion on tibial tubercle to avoid avulsion). Patella can be everted or subluxated laterally.
Medial structures: MCL (superficial and deep) at risk with over-release especially in valgus deformity correction; protect during tibial cut and retraction.
Infrapatellar branch of saphenous nerve: crosses field in subcutaneous tissue or retinaculum; injury common cause of postoperative numbness or neuroma pain.
Posterior structures: popliteal vessels and tibial nerve lie posterior to capsule; risk with overzealous posterior capsular release or saw penetration during tibial/femoral cuts.
Lateral structures in valgus: common peroneal nerve at fibular head/neck; risk increased with correction of severe valgus or lateral release.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Superficial: midline or slightly medial incision, raise medial flap to expose quadriceps tendon, patella, and medial retinaculum. Identify and protect infrapatellar saphenous branch if visible.

Deep: incise medial parapatellar retinaculum from quadriceps tendon through medial patellar border to tibial tubercle (leave 1-2cm cuff on patella for repair). Enter joint, evert patella laterally for exposure of femoral trochlea, condyles, and tibial plateau.

Deep joint: protect MCL medially with retractors during tibial cut; control posterior capsule bleeding; avoid saw penetration into popliteal fossa. For valgus, protect common peroneal with careful lateral work.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Crosses the anterior knee in the subcutaneous plane or within the medial retinaculum; transected during skin incision or retinacular opening in medial parapatellar approach.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Make incision slightly medial or identify the branch under the skin flap and protect/retract it medially or laterally. Avoid blind cautery in the subcutaneous layer.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Numbness over the anterolateral knee and patella or painful neuroma. Common and often tolerated but can be a source of patient dissatisfaction.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial parapatellar approach for TKA

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: At risk of avulsion at tibial tubercle insertion during patellar eversion or if excessive force used in stiff knee or obese patient.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Protect insertion with a retractor or pin during eversion. Perform quadriceps snip or tibial tubercle osteotomy if difficult exposure rather than forcing eversion.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Patellar tendon rupture or attenuation leading to extensor lag and need for reconstruction or augmentation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial parapatellar exposure in TKA

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Superficial and deep MCL can be over-released or injured during medial tibial plateau exposure or in valgus deformity correction to balance the knee.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use careful subperiosteal release only as needed for balance; protect with retractors during saw cuts. Avoid over-release in valgus knees.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Medial instability, need for constrained implant or revision, or late failure of the MCL repair/augmentation.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial parapatellar approach and balancing in TKA

structures_at_risk.structure: Popliteal vessels and posterior neurovascular structures
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lie immediately posterior to the capsule and tibial plateau; at risk from saw blade penetration or aggressive posterior capsular release during tibial or femoral preparation.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Use posterior retractors, control saw depth, perform posterior capsular release under direct vision with knee flexed. Avoid plunging instruments.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Vascular injury, popliteal fossa hematoma, or nerve injury (tibial or peroneal division) leading to ischemia or foot drop.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deep exposure and bone cuts in medial parapatellar TKA

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: At fibular head/neck; stretched or injured during correction of severe valgus deformity or lateral retinacular release/lateral osteophyte removal.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect with vessel loop if severe valgus or lateral work; limit lateral release to necessary; avoid excessive correction force.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop, sensory loss on dorsum of foot, or chronic pain. May require bracing or tendon transfer if permanent.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Valgus TKA via medial parapatellar with lateral balancing

key_landmarks: Medial border of the patella and quadriceps tendon; tibial tubercle as distal landmark for the arthrotomy interval and extensor mechanism protection.
Proximal femur landmarks not applicable; focus on patella, tibial tubercle, femoral epicondyles for MCL origin reference.
Patellar tracking on femoral trochlea after arthrotomy and eversion; assess for tilt or subluxation.
Posterior capsule and popliteal fossa reference for safe tibial cut depth.
Fibular head/neck for common peroneal nerve reference in valgus correction cases.
common_mistakes: Injury to the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve during skin incision or retinacular opening, causing postoperative anterolateral knee numbness or painful neuroma.
Avulsion of the patellar tendon at the tibial tubercle during forceful patellar eversion in a stiff or obese knee; leads to extensor mechanism failure.
Over-release of the MCL during medial exposure or valgus balancing, resulting in medial instability and need for constrained prosthesis.
Saw blade penetration into the popliteal fossa during tibial or femoral cuts, injuring vessels or tibial nerve.
Failure to protect the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head during valgus correction or lateral retinacular release, causing foot drop.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is the internervous or intermuscular interval in the medial parapatellar approach for TKA and what nerve branch is most commonly injured? A: No true internervous plane (splits quadriceps fascia and retinaculum). Infrapatellar branch of saphenous nerve is most commonly injured, causing anterolateral knee numbness or neuroma.

Q: How is the patellar tendon protected during patellar eversion in a stiff or obese knee during TKA? A: Protect the tibial tubercle insertion with a retractor or temporary pin. If eversion is difficult, perform a quadriceps snip proximally or tibial tubercle osteotomy rather than forcing and risking avulsion.

Q: What structures are at risk during medial release and tibial cut in a valgus knee, and how is the common peroneal nerve protected? A: MCL can be over-released causing medial instability; common peroneal at fibular head during valgus correction or lateral release. Protect peroneal with vessel loop if severe valgus; limit lateral work and avoid excessive correction force.

Q: Describe the key steps of the medial parapatellar approach for TKA and the primary structure at risk in each layer. A: Superficial: protect infrapatellar saphenous in subcutaneous/medial flap. Arthrotomy: protect patellar tendon insertion during eversion. Deep: protect MCL medially and popliteal structures posteriorly during cuts and balancing.

Q: Why is careful posterior capsular management important in TKA via medial parapatellar, and what is the consequence of saw penetration? A: Popliteal vessels and tibial nerve lie immediately behind the capsule and plateau. Penetration causes vascular injury, hematoma, or nerve deficit (foot drop or ischemia). Use posterior retractors and controlled saw depth.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to medial retinaculum / quadriceps fascia)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Midline or slightly medial incision centered on patella. Raise a medial skin flap to expose the quadriceps tendon, patella, and medial retinaculum. Identify and protect the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve in the subcutaneous tissue.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard medial parapatellar approach for TKA provides excellent exposure to the entire knee joint while allowing repair of the extensor mechanism. Nerve protection prevents annoying postoperative numbness.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Extensor mechanism and medial parapatellar arthrotomy
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incise the medial retinaculum 1-2 cm medial to the patellar border from the quadriceps tendon through the medial patellofemoral ligament to the tibial tubercle. Evert the patella laterally (or subluxate) to expose the trochlea, femoral condyles, and tibial plateau. Protect the patellar tendon insertion.
key_structures: Patellar tendon insertion at tibial tubercle
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working interval for TKA. Eversion gives full access for bone cuts and balancing. Patellar tendon protection and secure retinacular repair are essential for extensor function.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep joint exposure, balancing, and posterior protection
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After bone cuts, balance the knee by selective MCL release medially (avoid over-release). Protect posterior capsule and popliteal structures with retractors during tibial preparation. In valgus, protect common peroneal nerve during lateral release or osteophyte removal.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Balancing and posterior protection determine stability and safety. Over-release or saw injury leads to instability or neurovascular catastrophe.

validation_warnings: TKA payload is medial parapatellar focused. Some surgeons use midvastus or subvastus variants or lateral approach for valgus; this covers the most common. Source material previously mixed ACL/TKA and contained dev paths/stringified SAR; cleaned to public OB URLs and rich 6-field content only.
danger_zones: Infrapatellar saphenous branch: identify in medial flap or retinaculum; transection causes numbness/neuroma over anterolateral knee.
Patellar tendon insertion: protect at tibial tubercle during eversion; avulsion leads to extensor lag and difficult reconstruction.
MCL: avoid over-release especially in valgus; results in medial instability requiring constrained implant or revision.
Popliteal neurovascular bundle: protect with retractors and controlled saw depth; injury causes ischemia, hematoma, or tibial nerve deficit.
Common peroneal nerve (valgus knees): protect at fibular head during correction/lateral release; injury causes foot drop.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for total knee arthroplasty (TKA) via the medial parapatellar approach (standard/default for most TKA). Focus is on extensor mechanism protection, MCL balancing, infrapatellar saphenous nerve, and posterior neurovascular safety during bone cuts. Content is TKA-specific; no isolated ACL reconstruction, hip/THA, or other approach anatomy is mixed in.

procedure_name: Total Shoulder Arthroplasty (Anatomic TSA via Deltopectoral)
procedure_overview: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty via deltopectoral approach. Standard anterior approach that can be extended. Key anatomy: deltopectoral interval (cephalic vein), subscapularis (tenotomy 1 cm medial to insertion or peel from lesser tuberosity - critical for post-op stability and rehab), axillary nerve (inferior border of subscapularis, 5-7 cm below acromion on deep deltoid surface - protect with arm abduction/external rotation), conjoint tendon (musculocutaneous nerve deep and medial ~5-8 cm below coracoid). Deforming forces in fracture context or for exposure: pec major (anterior/medial shaft displacement), deltoid (lateral proximal fragment). Focus on subscapularis management for exposure and repair.
must_know_anatomy: Deltopectoral is the standard approach for anatomic TSA. Superficial interval between deltoid (axillary nerve on its deep surface) laterally and pectoralis major (medial and lateral pectoral nerves) medially. Cephalic vein lies in the groove and is typically taken laterally with the deltoid.

Subscapularis is the anterior gatekeeper: tenotomy ~1 cm medial to its insertion on lesser tuberosity or full peel from the tuberosity. Repair is critical for anterior stability and to allow early passive external rotation rehab. Inadequate repair or overstuffing leads to instability or stiffness.

Axillary nerve: courses on the deep surface of deltoid ~5-7 cm below the acromion. At the inferior border of subscapularis it is vulnerable during inferior capsular release or glenoid exposure. Arm abduction and external rotation relaxes the nerve and increases the safe zone.

Conjoint tendon (coracobrachialis + short head of biceps): lies medial to the interval. The musculocutaneous nerve (lateral cord) pierces or runs on its deep surface 5-8 cm below the coracoid tip. Excessive medial retraction or conjoint release risks nerve injury (biceps weakness, lateral forearm numbness).

Deforming forces relevant to exposure or proximal humerus pathology: pectoralis major displaces the shaft anteriorly and medially; deltoid displaces the proximal fragment laterally; supraspinatus and infraspinatus contribute to superior/posterior pull on the head. These must be understood for releases and implant positioning.

For glenoid and humeral preparation: the long head of biceps (in bicipital groove) is often tenodesed or released. The rotator interval and capsule are managed for exposure while preserving or reconstructing the subscapularis for stability.

structures_at_risk.structure: Axillary nerve (on deep surface of deltoid and inferior to subscapularis)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies 5-7 cm below acromion on deltoid undersurface and crosses inferior to subscapularis near the inferior glenohumeral capsule. Inferior capsular release, glenoid reaming, or aggressive medial/inferior retraction can stretch or lacerate it, especially if arm is not abducted/externally rotated.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the nerve early by palpation or visualization at the inferior subscapularis border. Keep arm abducted and externally rotated during inferior work to increase the safe distance. Limit inferior capsular release to what is needed for glenoid exposure; use blunt retractors carefully.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Deltoid paralysis (loss of abduction and forward elevation), teres minor weakness, and lateral upper arm sensory loss. Devastating for TSA outcome and rehab.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral approach for anatomic TSA (inferior subscapularis and glenoid exposure)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Enters the conjoint tendon (coracobrachialis) on its deep/medial surface 5-8 cm distal to the coracoid. Medial retraction of the conjoint tendon or release of the tendon for exposure can compress or transect the nerve.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify the conjoint tendon and limit medial retraction time/force. Avoid releasing the conjoint tendon unless absolutely necessary for exposure; if released, identify and protect the nerve first. Know the ~5-8 cm distance from coracoid.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Weakness or paralysis of coracobrachialis, biceps, and brachialis (elbow flexion weakness, especially supinated), plus lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve sensory loss (lateral forearm).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral approach (medial retraction or conjoint tendon work)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Tenotomy or peel is required for anterior exposure to the glenohumeral joint and humerus. Inadequate length for repair, poor tissue quality, or overtensioning after implant placement leads to failure of repair and anterior instability or poor external rotation.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Plan tenotomy 1 cm medial to insertion or peel with good cuff of tissue for repair. Tag the tendon. After trial components, repair with heavy nonabsorbable suture (transosseous or anchors) with the arm in slight external rotation to avoid overtension. Consider lesser tuberosity osteotomy in some techniques for bone-to-bone healing.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterior instability (subscap is the primary anterior stabilizer), loss of active internal rotation, and inability to perform early passive ER rehab leading to stiffness.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral TSA - subscapularis take-down and repair is the key step for exposure and post-op function

structures_at_risk.structure: Cephalic vein (in deltopectoral groove) and pectoral nerves
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Cephalic vein is the landmark for the interval; can be injured during superficial dissection or retraction, leading to bleeding or thrombosis. Medial and lateral pectoral nerves innervate pec major and can be injured with excessive medial dissection or pectoralis release.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify cephalic vein early and protect it (usually retracted laterally with deltoid). If injured, ligate cleanly. Limit pec major release to the upper 1-2 cm of insertion if needed for exposure; avoid complete release.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Hematoma, swelling, or (rarely) significant bleeding from cephalic injury. Pectoral nerve injury causes pec major atrophy/weakness affecting cosmesis and some adduction strength.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Superficial deltopectoral interval

key_landmarks: Deltopectoral groove and cephalic vein (interval landmark)
Coracoid process (medial boundary; conjoint tendon attachment)
Lesser tuberosity (subscapularis insertion - for tenotomy or peel planning)
Bicipital groove (long head biceps - often tenodesed/released)
Inferior border of subscapularis (axillary nerve landmark)
key_intervals: Superficial: deltopectoral groove (between deltoid laterally and pectoralis major medially); cephalic vein usually taken laterally with deltoid.

Deep: after developing interval and releasing upper pec major insertion as needed, the conjoint tendon is identified medially. Subscapularis is exposed and taken down for joint access. The axillary nerve is identified inferiorly for protection during capsular work.
common_mistakes: Injury to axillary nerve during inferior capsular release or glenoid exposure (failure to abduct/ER the arm or identify the nerve)
Musculocutaneous nerve injury from prolonged or forceful medial retraction of the conjoint tendon without identifying the nerve entry point
Poor subscapularis repair (insufficient tissue, overtension, or poor fixation) leading to anterior instability or loss of ER
Excessive pectoralis major release causing internal rotation weakness or cosmetic deformity
Confusing deltopectoral anatomy with superior or lateral approaches used for RSA or cuff repair
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What is the superficial interval for the deltopectoral approach in TSA and which nerve is at risk on the deep surface of the lateral muscle? A: Between deltoid (lateral) and pectoralis major (medial). The axillary nerve runs on the deep surface of the deltoid approximately 5-7 cm below the acromion and must be protected during inferior work.

Q: How is the subscapularis typically managed during deltopectoral TSA and why is its repair critical? A: Tenotomy 1 cm medial to the lesser tuberosity insertion or peel from the tuberosity. Repair restores the primary anterior stabilizer and allows safe early passive external rotation rehab. Failure leads to anterior instability or stiffness.

Q: Where is the musculocutaneous nerve at risk during deltopectoral exposure and what is the safe distance from the coracoid? A: It enters the conjoint tendon (coracobrachialis) on its deep surface 5-8 cm below the coracoid tip. Limit medial retraction force and duration; identify before any conjoint release.

Q: What arm position increases the safe zone for the axillary nerve during inferior capsular release or glenoid exposure in TSA? A: Abduction and external rotation of the arm relaxes the nerve and moves it inferiorly away from the surgical field, increasing the safe distance from the inferior glenoid/capsule.

Q: What are the key deforming forces on the proximal humerus that must be understood for exposure and implant positioning in shoulder arthroplasty or fracture work via deltopectoral? A: Pectoralis major pulls the shaft anteriorly and medially; deltoid pulls the proximal fragment laterally; supraspinatus/infraspinatus pull superiorly/posteriorly on the head.

Memorize: cephalic vein in groove (protect, usually lateral), subscap tenotomy/peel 1cm medial or from tuberosity, axillary at 5-7cm on deltoid deep + inferior subscap border, musculo 5-8cm on conjoint deep surface
Know arm abduction + ER position to relax and protect axillary during inferior work
Review subscap repair importance for anterior stability and rehab protocol
Practice the 5 pimp questions focused on deltopectoral-specific risks and subscap handling
Confirm case is anatomic TSA (deltopectoral standard) vs RSA (may use superior/lateral in some hands) - do not mix content
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep for deltopectoral approach to anatomic TSA only; does not cover RSA-specific considerations (deltoid reliance, glenosphere positioning) or alternative approaches (superior, lateral deltoid-split)
Subscapularis management (tenotomy vs peel vs osteotomy) is surgeon- and patient-specific; repair technique must be tailored to tissue quality and implant version/offset
Always identify and protect the axillary nerve at the inferior subscapularis border and the musculocutaneous nerve medial to the conjoint before aggressive retraction or release
approach_name: Deltopectoral (anterior) approach to the shoulder for anatomic TSA

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial deltopectoral interval (skin to cephalic vein)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Incision from coracoid tip toward deltoid insertion (lateral). Develop the deltopectoral groove; cephalic vein is the key landmark (usually retracted laterally with deltoid to preserve venous drainage of the deltoid). Pectoralis major is medial.
structures_at_risk: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves (with excessive medial pec release)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Defines the entire anterior approach corridor. Protecting the cephalic vein and identifying the interval early prevents bleeding and orients all deeper work. This is strictly deltopectoral; lateral split is a different approach.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep interval to conjoint tendon and subscapularis
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: After developing the groove and releasing the upper 1-2 cm of pec major insertion if needed for exposure, identify the coracoid and conjoint tendon medially. The subscapularis is the anterior rotator cuff structure covering the joint; it is tenotomized or peeled for access.
structures_at_risk: Musculocutaneous nerve (deep to conjoint 5-8 cm below coracoid)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: This layer gives access to the glenohumeral joint. Musculocutaneous identification protects elbow flexion; subscapularis handling determines post-op anterior stability and ER rehab.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Inferior subscapularis / capsular exposure (axillary nerve protection)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: At the inferior border of subscapularis (after tenotomy/peel), the axillary nerve is identified by palpation or visualization on the deep deltoid surface. Inferior capsular release and glenoid exposure occur here. Arm abduction + external rotation relaxes the nerve.
key_structures: Axillary nerve (inferior subscap border, deep deltoid)
structures_at_risk: Axillary nerve (inferior capsular release or medial retraction)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Critical danger zone for the most devastating nerve injury in shoulder arthroplasty. This step is deltopectoral-specific; superior or arthroscopic approaches have different nerve risks (suprascapular).

danger_zones: Axillary nerve at inferior subscapularis border and deep deltoid (5-7 cm below acromion) - abduct/ER arm, identify early, limit inferior release
Musculocutaneous nerve deep to conjoint tendon (5-8 cm below coracoid) - limit medial retraction force/duration, identify before conjoint release
Subscapularis repair failure (anterior instability, poor ER rehab) - plan adequate tissue for repair, repair in slight ER, use strong fixation
Cephalic vein injury in deltopectoral groove (hematoma) - identify and protect (retract laterally with deltoid)
approach_specific_notes: Strictly deltopectoral (anterior) approach for anatomic TSA. No superolateral/lateral split (RSA) or arthroscopic portal anatomy included. Subscapularis management (tenotomy vs peel) and axillary nerve protection (arm abduction/ER) are the highest-yield pearls. All content is anatomic TSA deltopectoral-specific.
validation_warnings: Total shoulder arthroplasty payload is deltopectoral-specific for anatomic TSA. RSA content (deltoid tension, baseplate, subscap handling variants) is in the separate reverse_shoulder_arthroplasty payload. Source material is limited to 12061; dedicated RSA vs TSA separation is maintained.
